title: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses
authors: Anthony S. Fauci, Gregory K. Folkers, H. Clifford Lane

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.title: Understanding the CDC Classification System for HIV/AIDS
items.content: The CDC classification system categorizes HIV infection based on clinical conditions and CD4+ T lymphocyte count, providing a framework for understanding disease progression.
keyPoints: HIV cases are classified into three stages (A, B, C) and three CD4+ categories (1, 2, 3).
Advanced HIV disease (AIDS) is stage C or stage 3 if CD4+ count < 200 cells/μL in individuals over 6 years.
The classification system serves surveillance purposes; clinicians should view HIV as a spectrum of disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.title: Identifying the Etiologic Agent of AIDS
items.content: HIV, specifically HIV-1, is identified as the primary etiologic agent causing AIDS, with a focus on its classification within retroviruses.
keyPoints: HIV belongs to the Retroviridae family and lentivirus subfamily.
HIV-1 is the most common cause of HIV disease worldwide, including in the U.S.
HIV-1 has several subtypes with different geographic distributions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.title: Geographic Distribution and Subtype Variability of HIV-1
items.content: HIV-1's global impact is driven by its subtype variability, with the M group being predominant in the AIDS pandemic.
keyPoints: HIV-1 strains are divided into one major group (M) and three minor groups (N, O, P).
HIV-2 was first identified in West Africa but has since spread globally.
The AIDS pandemic is primarily caused by HIV-1 M group viruses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.title: HIV Entry Mechanism and Co-Receptor Utilization
items.content: The entry of HIV into host cells is initiated by the binding of gp120 to CD4 on T lymphocytes, followed by a conformational change that allows interaction with co-receptors CCR5 or CXCR4. This step determines viral tropism and facilitates membrane fusion mediated by gp41.
keyPoints: gp120 binds to CD4 (55-kDa protein) on T cells
Conformational change in gp120 enables binding to CCR5 or CXCR4 co-receptors
CCR5 and CXCR4 are G protein-coupled receptors crucial for viral entry
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.title: Viral Vulnerabilities to Host Restriction Factors
items.content: HIV faces several host restriction factors during its replication cycle. TRIM5α causes premature disassembly of viral capsids, APOBEC3 induces hypermutation by deaminating cytidine in the viral DNA, and SAMHD1 limits reverse transcription by reducing dNTPs. HIV counteracts these defenses with proteins like Vif.
keyPoints: TRIM5α causes premature disassembly of retroviral capsids
APOBEC3 induces hypermutation via cytidine deamination
Vif targets APOBEC3 for degradation to protect the virus
SAMHD1 depletes dNTPs, inhibiting reverse transcription
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.title: Role of Cellular Activation in HIV Replication and Pathogenesis
items.content: Cellular activation is crucial for efficient integration of incompletely reverse-transcribed DNA intermediates into the host genome. It also initiates transcription of integrated proviral DNA, influencing both viral replication and disease progression.
keyPoints: Incompletely reverse-transcribed DNA integrates efficiently only with cellular activation
Cellular activation is required for transcription initiation from integrated provirus
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.title: Mechanisms of HIV Latency and Activation
items.content: HIV can remain latent in resting CD4+ T cells without detectable activation markers. Activation from latency requires interactions between cellular and viral factors, leading to transcription and subsequent protein processing.
keyPoints: Cell-associated HIV RNA can be detected in infected resting CD4+ T cells.
Activation of HIV expression depends on various cellular and viral factor interactions.
Potential therapeutic targets include reverse transcriptase, protease, integrase, and virus-target cell binding/fusion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.title: HIV Genome Complexity and Therapeutic Targets
items.content: The HIV-1 genome encodes structural proteins, enzymes for replication, and regulatory genes that enhance virus growth. These include tat, rev, nef, vif, vpr, and vpu, with vpu counteracting tetherin to facilitate virion release.
keyPoints: HIV-1 has genes encoding gag (core proteins), pol (enzymes for protease processing, reverse transcription, integration), and env (envelope glycoproteins).
Regulatory genes like tat, rev, nef, vif, vpr, and vpu modify host cells to enhance virus growth.
Vpu counteracts tetherin, an IFN-induced protein that inhibits virion release.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.title: Understanding the Molecular Diversity of HIV-1
items.content: HIV-1 exhibits significant molecular diversity, with sequence differences ranging from a few percent to over 50% among isolates. This variability is influenced by factors such as immune pressure and functional constraints on viral proteins.
keyPoints: Sequence diversity in the coding sequences of HIV-1 envelope protein ranges from a few percent (same individual) to more than 50% (different groups M, N, O, P).
HIV evolves through base substitution, insertions/deletions, recombination, and glycosylation site changes.
Envelope proteins are highly variable due to immune selective pressure; reverse transcriptase is conserved in the absence of drug pressure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.title: HIV-1 Subtypes and Recombinant Forms
items.content: The M group of HIV-1, responsible for most global infections, has diversified into subtypes and circulating recombinant forms (CRFs), influenced by human transmission dynamics.
keyPoints: Group M includes ten subtypes (A-L) and over 150 CRFs.
Subtype C dominates the global pandemic, accounting for ~50% of infections worldwide.
In sub-Saharan Africa, subtype C is predominant; in South Africa, 98% of isolates are subtype C.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.title: Geographic Distribution and Recombination of HIV-1 Subtypes
items.content: HIV-1 subtypes exhibit distinct geographic distributions, with recombination occurring in areas where multiple subtypes co-circulate.
keyPoints: Nine strains account for most global infections: subtypes A, B, C, D, F, G and CRFs 01_AE, 02_AG, 07_BC.
Subtype B is predominant in the US, Canada, parts of South America, western Europe, and Australia due to a founder effect.
Recombination among different clades occurs when an individual is infected with multiple subtypes, especially in regions where subtypes overlap.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.title: Impact of HIV-1 Molecular Diversity on Disease Management and Vaccine Development
items.content: The molecular heterogeneity of HIV-1, including multiple subtypes and recombinant forms, affects transmission rates, disease progression, and antiretroviral resistance. This diversity poses challenges for developing a universally effective vaccine.
keyPoints: HIV-1 has multiple subtypes and circulating recombinant forms.
Diversity impacts transmission rates, disease progression, and drug resistance.
A broadly useful HIV vaccine must protect against various viral strains.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.title: Modes of HIV Transmission and Associated Risks
items.content: HIV is primarily transmitted through sexual contact, blood products, and from mother to infant. There is no evidence supporting transmission via other modalities.
keyPoints: Primary modes: sexual contact (heterosexual and male-to-male), blood/blood products, maternal-infant transmission.
No evidence of HIV transmission by other means after decades of study.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.title: Risk Factors for Heterosexual Transmission of HIV
items.content: HIV transmission through heterosexual intercourse is generally inefficient, with a low per-act risk in the absence of antiretroviral therapy or condom use. However, factors such as viral load and presence of ulcerative genital diseases can increase transmission efficiency.
keyPoints: Per-act risk for female-to-male transmission: 0.04%
Per-act risk for male-to-female transmission: 0.08%
Risk approaches zero with effective antiretroviral therapy or prophylaxis
Increased lymphocytes and monocytes in seminal fluid raise transmission risk
Other STIs, especially those causing genital ulcerations (e.g., Treponema pallidum, Haemophilus ducreyi, HSV), increase HIV transmission risk
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.title: Increased Risk of HIV Transmission via Unprotected Receptive Anal Intercourse
items.content: Unprotected receptive anal intercourse carries a higher risk of HIV transmission compared to vaginal intercourse due to the thin rectal mucosal membrane and potential for microtrauma.
keyPoints: Per-act risk for HIV transmission via unprotected receptive anal intercourse: ~1.4%
Rectal mucosa is more susceptible to trauma, increasing infection risk
Anal douching and practices causing mucosal trauma further elevate risk
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.title: Impact of STIs on HIV Transmission Efficiency
items.content: The presence of other sexually transmitted infections, particularly those causing genital ulcerations and inflammation, significantly increases the efficiency of HIV transmission.
keyPoints: Treating STIs can decrease HIV transmission, especially in low-prevalence populations
HSV treatment does not reduce HIV acquisition due to persistent HIV receptor-positive cells
Non-ulcerative inflammatory STIs (e.g., Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae) also increase HIV risk
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.title: Viral Load as a Determinant of HIV Transmission Risk
items.content: The risk of HIV transmission is significantly influenced by the viral load in plasma. Lower viral loads correlate with reduced transmission risk.
keyPoints: Transmission was rare when the infected partner had a plasma level of <1700 copies/mL.
Antiretroviral therapy reducing viral load to <20 copies/mL results in essentially no chance of sexual transmission (U = U).
Highest transmission rates occur during early and advanced stages of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Male Circumcision on HIV Acquisition Risk
items.content: Male circumcision is associated with a lower risk of acquiring HIV, potentially due to reduced susceptibility to ulcerative STIs and other factors.
keyPoints: Circumcision reduces the risk of HIV acquisition for heterosexual men and those reporting mainly insertive sex.
Circumcised men show decreased rates of HSV type 2, HPV, genital ulcer disease, bacterial vaginosis, and Trichomonas vaginalis infections.
Indirect benefits include reduced risk of these infections in female partners.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.title: Risks Associated with Oral Contraceptives and Substance Use
items.content: Oral contraceptives may increase HIV infection risk due to changes in cervical mucosa, while substance use is linked to unsafe sexual practices.
keyPoints: Use of oral contraceptives might increase HIV incidence by making the cervical mucosa more vulnerable.
Adolescent girls with immature genital tracts are particularly susceptible to HIV upon exposure.
Alcohol and illicit drugs like methamphetamine, MDMA, ketamine, GHB, and poppers increase risky sexual behavior and HIV transmission risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE
items.title: Risk Factors and Transmission Mechanisms for HIV among Injection Drug Users (IDUs)
items.content: HIV transmission among IDUs occurs through sharing injection paraphernalia, including needles, syringes, water used in drug preparation, or cotton filters. Notably, subcutaneous ('skin popping') and intramuscular ('muscling') injections can also transmit HIV, contrary to common misconceptions of their low risk.
keyPoints: Transmission does not require IV puncture; skin popping and muscling are risky.

Risk increases with longer duration of drug use, frequent needle sharing, more partners, comorbid psychiatric conditions (e.g., antisocial personality disorder), cocaine use in injectable or smoked form, and high HIV prevalence areas.
Per-act transmission risk from a contaminated needle is approximately 0.6%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.title: Historical Context and Current Risk of HIV Transmission via Blood Transfusions in Developed Countries
items.content: The risk of HIV transmission through blood transfusions has significantly decreased since the implementation of mandatory blood screening for HIV-1 antibodies in 1985. Modern safety measures include donor health history questionnaires, self-deferral opportunities, and nucleic acid testing.
keyPoints: Over 90% of HIV infections from contaminated blood products occurred before 1985.
Current risk in the U.S. is approximately 1 in 2 million units due to rigorous screening processes.
Annual transfusion volume: nearly 21 million components, indicating residual transmission risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.title: Ongoing Threats and Safety Measures for HIV Transmission via Blood Products in Developing Countries
items.content: In some developing countries where routine blood screening is not universally practiced, the risk of HIV transmission through transfusions remains significant. Sporadic breakdowns in screening procedures can lead to clusters of infections.
keyPoints: Routine screening and safety measures are less consistent in certain developing regions.
Reports indicate sporadic failures in screening leading to contaminated blood or plasma use.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.title: Risk and Management of Occupational HIV Transmission
items.content: Health care workers face a small but definite risk of occupational HIV transmission, primarily through percutaneous injuries or mucous membrane exposure to potentially infectious body fluids. The implementation of postexposure prophylaxis (PEP) with antiretroviral therapy has significantly reduced the incidence of such transmissions.
keyPoints: More than 300,000 health care workers in the U.S. experience needle sticks or sharp injuries annually.
Global annual HIV infections among health care workers due to sharps injuries are estimated at 1000 cases (range: 200–5000).
In the U.S., 58 documented and 150 possible occupational transmissions have been reported by the CDC.
Risk of HIV transmission from a needle stick is approximately 0.23%, and mucous membrane exposure is about 0.09% without PEP within 24 hours.
PEP with combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) significantly reduces the risk of HIV transmission following occupational exposure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.title: Comparative Risk Assessment for Bloodborne Pathogens
items.content: The risk of transmission varies among bloodborne pathogens following occupational exposure. While the risk of HIV is relatively low, HBV and HCV present higher risks, necessitating specific postexposure prophylaxis measures.
keyPoints: Risk of HIV transmission from a needle stick: ~0.23%; mucous membrane exposure: ~0.09% without PEP.
HBV infection risk after similar exposures is 6–30% in nonimmune individuals; effective postexposure prophylaxis can prevent HBV infection >90% of the time.
HCV transmission risk following percutaneous injury is approximately 1.8%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.title: Factors Influencing Risk of Occupational HIV Transmission
items.content: The likelihood of occupational HIV transmission is influenced by the volume and nature of exposure to infectious materials. Larger volumes or deeper injuries increase risk, as does exposure to blood from patients with high viral loads.
keyPoints: Increased risk associated with large quantities of blood, deep injuries, or procedures involving hollow-bore needles in veins/arteries.
Higher transmission risk for exposures to blood from untreated patients with elevated HIV levels.
Transmission via intact skin has not been documented; nonintact skin exposure presents a lower risk than mucous membrane exposure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.title: Importance of Universal Precautions in Preventing Occupational HIV Transmission
items.content: Universal precautions are critical to prevent the rare transmission of HIV among health care workers and patients due to breaches in infection control, such as reuse of contaminated syringes or improper sterilization.
keyPoints: Transmission can occur through breaches in infection control practices.
Reuse of contaminated syringes and failure to properly sterilize equipment are risk factors.
Universal precautions should be used for all patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.title: Mother-to-Child Transmission of HIV and the Role of Antiretroviral Therapy (ART)
items.content: Without ART during pregnancy, labor, delivery, and post-birth for infants, mother-to-child transmission rates are 15%-25% in industrialized countries and 25%-35% in developing countries. ART significantly reduces this risk to below 1%.
keyPoints: Transmission rates without ART: 15%-25% (industrialized), 25%-35% (developing).
ART during pregnancy, labor, delivery, and for infants post-birth reduces transmission <1%.
Both US Public Health Service and WHO recommend lifelong ART for pregnant women with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.title: Factors Influencing Mother-to-Child Transmission of HIV via Breast-Feeding
items.content: In developing countries, untreated mothers breast-feeding can transmit HIV. Risk factors include detectable HIV in breast milk and low maternal CD4+ T cell counts. ART during breastfeeding reduces transmission risk significantly.
keyPoints: Breast-feeding is a significant transmission route in developing countries without treatment.
Risk factors: detectable HIV in breast milk, mastitis, low maternal CD4+ T cells, vitamin A deficiency.
ART during breastfeeding greatly diminishes the risk of transmission.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.title: Saliva is not a significant route for HIV transmission due to antiviral factors.
items.content: HIV can be isolated in low titers from the saliva of some infected individuals, but there is no convincing evidence that it transmits through kissing or occupational exposure. Saliva contains endogenous antiviral factors such as immunoglobulins (IgA, IgG, and IgM) and other inhibitors like secretory leukocyte protease inhibitor (SLPI), which block HIV infection in vitro.
keyPoints: No convincing evidence of saliva transmitting HIV through kissing or occupational exposure.
Saliva contains endogenous antiviral factors including immunoglobulins (IgA, IgG, and IgM).
Secretory leukocyte protease inhibitor (SLPI) in saliva inhibits HIV infection in vitro.
Higher salivary levels of SLPI are associated with decreased risk of HIV transmission through breast milk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.title: Universal precautions are crucial to prevent rare HIV transmission via body fluids.
items.content: Transmission of HIV by a human bite is possible but rare. There is no evidence that tears, sweat, or urine transmit HIV. However, isolated cases have occurred when body fluids potentially contaminated with blood were handled without universal precautions, highlighting the importance of these safety measures in nursing care for individuals with HIV.
keyPoints: HIV transmission by human bite is rare but possible.
No evidence supports transmission through tears, sweat, or urine.
Isolated cases of transmission occurred when body fluids possibly contaminated with blood were handled without universal precautions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.title: Global Prevalence and Regional Impact of HIV/AIDS
items.content: HIV infection is a global pandemic, with significant regional disparities in prevalence. The WHO Eastern and Southern Africa region is most severely affected, with over half of the world's HIV cases.
keyPoints: 39.9 million individuals were living with HIV worldwide as of 2023.
~95% of people living with HIV/AIDS reside in low- and middle-income countries.
The WHO Eastern and Southern Africa region has an adult prevalence rate of 5.7%, accounting for more than half of the global total.
South Africa has the highest number of people living with HIV (7.7 million).
Eswatini has the highest adult HIV prevalence globally at 25.1%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.title: Trends in New HIV Infections and Prevention Efforts
items.content: Global new HIV infections have significantly decreased since their peak, largely due to improved prevention efforts and increased access to antiretroviral therapy.
keyPoints: New HIV infections globally fell by 60% from the peak in 1995 (3.3 million) to 1.3 million in 2023.
Between 2010 and 2023, new HIV infections declined by 39%, from 2.1 million to 1.3 million.
~62% reduction in HIV infections among children <15 years was observed between 2010 and 2023.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.title: Access to Antiretroviral Therapy and Impact on AIDS Deaths
items.content: Increased access to antiretroviral therapy has been pivotal in reducing global AIDS deaths, highlighting the importance of treatment accessibility.
keyPoints: 77% of people living with HIV globally were accessing antiretroviral therapy as of December 2023.
84% of pregnant women with HIV had access to antiretroviral medicines to prevent transmission to their child in 2023.
Global AIDS deaths totaled 630,000 in 2023, a 65% decrease since 2000.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.title: Progress in Eastern and Southern Africa
items.content: Significant progress has been made in reducing HIV incidence and AIDS-related deaths in the Eastern and Southern Africa region, with a substantial increase in treatment coverage.
keyPoints: Annual number of people acquiring HIV fell by 59% from 2010 to 2023 (from 1.1 million to 450,000).
AIDS-related deaths decreased by 57% during the same period (from 600,000 in 2010 to 260,000 in 2023).
93% of people living with HIV knew their infection status as of 2023.
83% of those living with HIV were on antiretroviral therapy, and 78% had suppressed viral loads.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.title: Rising HIV Infections in Eastern Europe and Central Asia
items.content: The region has seen a significant increase in new HIV infections and AIDS-related deaths, driven largely by injection drug use.
keyPoints: 20% increase in annual new HIV infections between 2010 and 2023.
34% increase in AIDS deaths during the same period.
~94% of new infections are among key populations and their sexual partners.
The Russian Federation, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, and Uzbekistan reported 92% of all registered cases in 2023.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.title: Key Populations Drive HIV Infections in Asia and the Pacific
items.content: Despite a decrease in new infections, key populations remain disproportionately affected by HIV in the Asia and Pacific region.
keyPoints: Estimated 6.7 million people living with HIV at end of 2023.
Overall adult prevalence rate is 0.2%.
~80% of new HIV infections were among key populations and their partners in 2022.
Three populous countries—China, India, and Indonesia—account for around three-quarters of all people living with HIV in the region.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.title: Disproportionate Impact of HIV on Minority Populations
items.content: In the United States, HIV disproportionately affects minority populations. Blacks/African Americans and Hispanics/Latinos account for a significant majority of new infections despite being smaller proportions of the overall population.
keyPoints: 37% of new HIV infections from 2018 to 2022 were among Blacks/African Americans, who make up only 12% of the U.S. population.
Hispanics/Latinos accounted for 33% of new HIV infections, while they constitute 18% of the U.S. population.
HIV is concentrated in urban areas but affects minority populations across both urban and rural settings.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.title: Decline in Perinatal HIV Transmission
items.content: The implementation of universal counseling, voluntary testing for pregnant women, and antiretroviral therapy has significantly reduced perinatal HIV transmission rates in the United States.
keyPoints: Perinatal HIV transmission has declined from a peak of approximately 1750 cases in 1991 to 62 new diagnoses in 2022.
Universal counseling and voluntary testing for pregnant women, along with antiretroviral therapy for mothers and newborns, have been key strategies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.title: Reduction in HIV-Related Mortality Rates
items.content: Improved treatment options, particularly the use of antiretroviral therapy (ART), have led to a significant decline in HIV-related mortality rates.
keyPoints: HIV-related deaths decreased from 2.6 per 100,000 in 2010 to 1.3 per 100,000 in 2022.
The most influential factor in reducing mortality has been the increased use and refinement of ART.
Despite progress, HIV remains a leading cause of death among persons aged 25-44 years, especially within Black/African-American communities.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Mechanisms of CD4+ T Cell Depletion in HIV Infection
items.content: The profound immunodeficiency seen in HIV disease is primarily due to the depletion and dysfunction of CD4+ T cells. This occurs through direct infection by HIV, immune-mediated clearance of infected cells, cell death from aberrant activation and inflammation, and immune exhaustion.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cells are targeted by HIV via the CD4 molecule and co-receptors CCR5 or CXCR4.
Mechanisms include direct infection, immune clearance, pyroptosis due to abortive infection, and immune exhaustion from persistent activation.
These processes lead to both quantitative and qualitative deficiencies in helper T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Clinical Implications of CD4+ T Cell Levels in HIV Disease Progression
items.content: Patients with CD4+ T cell levels below certain thresholds are at high risk for opportunistic infections and neoplasms, which are AIDS-defining illnesses. Some complications like Kaposi's sarcoma may occur before severe immunologic impairment.
keyPoints: Low CD4+ T cell counts correlate with increased risk of opportunistic diseases.
AIDS-defining illnesses include certain infections and cancers.
Some HIV-related complications can precede significant immunodeficiency.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of Initial HIV Mucosal Transmission
items.content: HIV can cross the mucosal barrier via Langerhans cells or through microscopic rents, with significant disruptions in the mucosa increasing infection efficiency.
keyPoints: Langerhans cells transport HIV beneath the surface.
Microscopic rents and ulcerative genital disease facilitate viral entry.
Spatial dispersion of CD4+ T cells poses an obstacle to infection establishment.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.title: Role of CD4+ T Cells in Early HIV Infection
items.content: Both resting and activated CD4+ T cells are early amplifiers, with activated cells supporting more productive infection.
keyPoints: Resting CD4+ T cells are abundant but less efficient.
Activated CD4+ T cells support higher levels of viral replication.
Basic reproductive rate (R0) must be ≥1 for established infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics of Founder Viruses in Sexual Transmission
items.content: Sexual transmission involves a viral genetic bottleneck, with founder viruses having specific envelope glycoprotein characteristics.
keyPoints: Transmission results from a single infectious event.
Founder viruses have shorter V1–V2 loop sequences and fewer N-linked glycosylation sites.
These viruses are typically R5 strains sensitive to neutralizing antibodies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2)
items.title: Acute HIV Syndrome and Viremia
items.content: During primary HIV infection, approximately 50% of individuals experience an acute HIV syndrome within 2 to 4 weeks. This syndrome is characterized by high levels of plasma viremia, often in the millions of copies per milliliter, which correlates with mononucleosis-like symptoms.
keyPoints: Acute HIV syndrome occurs in ~50% of individuals within 2-4 weeks of initial infection.
High levels of plasma viremia during acute HIV infection can reach millions of copies/mL.
Symptoms correlate with high plasma viremia and include mononucleosis-like symptoms.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2)
items.title: Transmission Risk During Acute Infection
items.content: The high levels of viremia observed during acute HIV infection increase the likelihood of virus transmission through sexual contact, shared needles, and mother-to-child routes.
keyPoints: High plasma viremia in acute HIV infection increases transmission risk.
Transmission can occur via sexual contact, shared needles, and mother-to-child (intrapartum, perinatal, or breast milk).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Persistence of HIV Despite Immune Response
items.content: Despite robust immune responses, HIV establishes a chronic infection with persistent virus replication. In untreated patients, this can last for about 10 years before clinical illness manifests.
keyPoints: HIV persists despite strong cellular and humoral immune responses.
Chronic infection develops with continual virus replication in untreated patients.
Median duration of asymptomatic chronic infection is ~10 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Mechanisms of HIV Immune Evasion
items.content: HIV evades immune control through sustained replication and mutation, leading to viral diversity. Mutants escape CD8+ CTLs, while high mutation rates hinder antibody neutralization. The humoral response struggles to produce effective neutralizing antibodies against the envelope proteins gp120 and gp41.
keyPoints: Viral escape from CD8+ CTLs is critical for HIV progression.
High mutation rates prevent effective antibody neutralization.
Broadly neutralizing antibodies occur in about 20% of people with HIV, requiring 2–3 years to develop.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Immune Exhaustion and Dysfunction
items.content: Persistent viral replication leads to immune exhaustion in both CD8+ T cells and B cells. This is characterized by upregulation of inhibitory receptors like PD-1, TIGIT, Tim-3, and Lag-3, which impair the functional capacity of these immune cells.
keyPoints: Immune exhaustion involves upregulation of inhibitory receptors such as PD-1, TIGIT, Tim-3, and Lag-3.
This exhaustion affects both CD8+ T cell and B cell responses.
Persistent viremia contributes to immune dysfunction.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Viral Strategies for Immune Evasion
items.content: HIV employs several strategies to evade immune detection, including downregulation of HLA class I molecules by viral proteins Nef, Tat, and Vpu. This prevents CD8+ CTLs from recognizing infected cells. Additionally, HIV-infected cells can sequester in immunologically privileged sites like the CNS.
keyPoints: HIV downregulates HLA class I molecules via Nef, Tat, and Vpu to evade CD8+ CTL recognition.
Infected cells may hide in immunologically privileged sites such as the CNS.
NK cell-mediated elimination of HIV-infected cells is ineffective despite HLA class I downregulation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.title: Impact of HIV on CD4+ T Cells
items.content: HIV preferentially infects activated CD4+ T cells, including those specific to HIV, leading to a significant loss in viral-specific helper T cell responses. This early depletion has profound negative consequences for controlling HIV replication.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell help is crucial for both humoral and cell-mediated immune responses.
HIV infects activated CD4+ T cells, including HIV-specific ones.
40–70% of memory CD4+ T cells in the GALT are eliminated during acute infection.
Loss of viral-specific helper T cell responses negatively impacts immunologic control of HIV replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.title: Characteristics and Challenges of the HIV Reservoir
items.content: The HIV reservoir consists of latently infected, resting CD4+ T cells that persist despite ART. These cells carry integrated HIV DNA and can reactivate upon cellular activation.
keyPoints: Latent reservoirs exist in lymphoid tissue, peripheral blood, CNS, and other locations.
Cells contain integrated HIV DNA, with a small fraction harboring replication-competent virus.
Persistent low-level virus replication replenishes the latent reservoir.
Elimination of the entire pool of latently infected cells would take many years, even under prolonged viral suppression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.title: Challenges in Eradicating HIV
items.content: Efforts to eliminate the latent HIV reservoir using 'shock and kill' strategies have been unsuccessful. The persistent reservoir remains a major obstacle to achieving a cure.
keyPoints: 'Shock and kill' strategies aim to activate resting CD4+ T cells to reinitiate viral expression without systemic activation during ART.
These attempts have not succeeded in eliminating the latent viral reservoir.
The long-lived nature of the reservoir, continually replenished by low-level replication, hinders eradication efforts.
Research is ongoing into achieving ART-free HIV remission through passive transfer of broadly neutralizing antibodies and immune-enhancing agents.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.title: Impact of ART on Viral Dynamics
items.content: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) significantly reduces plasma viremia in HIV patients. The administration of reverse transcriptase and protease inhibitors leads to a rapid decline in viral load, primarily due to the targeting of rapidly turning over CD4+ T cells.
keyPoints: Treatment with ART results in a >90% reduction in plasma viremia within 2 weeks.
93–99% of circulating virus originates from recently infected CD4+ T cells; ~1–7% from longer-lived cells like monocytes/macrophages.
Half-life of circulating virion is ~30–60 minutes, and productively infected cells have a half-life of ~1 day.
Extremely large amounts of virus (~10^10-10^11 virions) are produced and cleared daily.
Minimal duration of HIV-1 replication cycle in vivo is ~2 days.
Decrease in plasma viremia correlates with decreased virus replication in lymph nodes, confirming lymphoid tissue as the main site of HIV replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.title: Prognostic Implications of Viral Set Point
items.content: The viral set point, established ~1 year after HIV acquisition, is a critical predictor for disease progression in untreated patients. A lower set point correlates with slower progression to AIDS.
keyPoints: Viral set point at ~1 year post-infection has important prognostic implications.
Untreated individuals with a low viral set point (6 months to 1 year) progress to AIDS more slowly than those with a high set point.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.title: Critical CD4+ T Cell Threshold in Advanced HIV Disease
items.content: In advanced HIV disease, untreated patients or those with uncontrolled virus replication experience a significant drop in CD4+ T cell counts below 200/μL, leading to high susceptibility to opportunistic infections. The CDC defines AIDS at this stage for individuals over 5 years old with such low CD4+ levels.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell count falls below 200/μL in advanced HIV disease
CDC case definition of AIDS includes CD4+ <200/μL for patients >5 years old
Untreated patients may see CD4+ counts drop to as low as 10/μL or zero
ART and prophylaxis significantly increase survival even with advanced disease
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.title: Distinguishing Long-Term Survivors and Nonprogressors
items.content: Long-term nonprogressors are a subset of long-term survivors who maintain normal CD4+ T cell counts and low plasma viremia without ART. Approximately 5–15% of people with HIV fall into this category, though many progress over time.
keyPoints: Long-term nonprogressors have normal CD4+ T cell counts and low plasma viremia
~13% of early-infected homosexual/bisexual men may remain free of clinical AIDS for >20 years without ART
5–15% of people with HIV are long-term nonprogressors, but many eventually require ART
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.title: Characteristics of Elite Controllers in HIV
items.content: Elite controllers represent a rare subgroup (fraction of 1%) with undetectable plasma viremia and normal CD4+ T cell counts. They possess robust HIV-specific immune responses, particularly effective CD8+ CTLs, and certain HLA class I haplotypes like HLA-B57-01 are overrepresented.
keyPoints: Elite controllers constitute a fraction of 1% of people with HIV
They have undetectable plasma viremia and normal CD4+ T cell counts without ART
Robust HIV-specific immune response, especially effective CD8+ CTLs
HLA-B57-01 and HLA-B27-05 haplotypes are overrepresented in elite controllers
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Role of Lymphoid Tissues in HIV Pathogenesis
items.content: Lymphoid tissues are critical for the establishment and propagation of HIV infection, regardless of the portal of entry. Virus replication primarily occurs in these tissues rather than in blood, with plasma viremia reflecting virus production in lymphoid tissue.
keyPoints: Lymphoid tissues are major sites for HIV establishment and propagation.
Virus replication mainly occurs in lymphoid tissue, not in blood.
Plasma viremia levels directly reflect virus production in lymphoid tissue.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Lymphadenopathy and Cellular Activation in HIV
items.content: HIV infection often leads to progressive generalized lymphadenopathy, reflecting cellular activation and immune response. This is characterized by follicular or germinal center hyperplasia due to the involvement of lymphoid tissue.
keyPoints: Progressive generalized lymphadenopathy occurs early in HIV infection for some patients.
Lymphadenopathy reflects cellular activation and immune response.
Characterized by follicular or germinal center hyperplasia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: GALT's Role in Early Virus Replication
items.content: The GALT is crucial for early virus replication and dissemination during acute HIV infection. It amplifies virus replication, leading to high plasma viremia levels, with virions trapped on follicular dendritic cells (FDCs) serving as a reservoir.
keyPoints: GALT plays a major role in early virus replication and dissemination.
Virus replication amplification leads to high plasma viremia levels.
Trapped virions on FDCs serve as a viral reservoir.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Mechanisms of Lymphoid Tissue Destruction in Advanced HIV
items.content: In advanced stages of HIV, the architecture of lymphoid tissues is disrupted due to destruction of fibroblastic reticular cell (FRC) and follicular dendritic cell (FDC) networks. This disruption involves collagen deposition causing fibrosis and altered cytokine expression, notably decreased IL-7 and lymphotoxin α, and increased TGF β levels.
keyPoints: Destruction of FRC and FDC networks in lymphoid tissues
Collagen deposition causes fibrosis
Decreased IL-7 and lymphotoxin α expression
Increased TGF β levels
Lymph nodes become 'burnt out' in advanced stages
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Impact of HIV on GALT and CD4+ T Cells
items.content: HIV infection leads to rapid infection and elimination of a significant proportion of CD4+ T cells in the gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT), with estimates ranging from 50–90%. This is due to basal cellular activation combined with virus-mediated activation.
keyPoints: Rapid infection and elimination of 50–90% of CD4+ T cells in GALT
Caused by basal cellular activation plus virus-mediated activation
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Aberrant Immune Activation Drives Persistent HIV Replication
items.content: In untreated HIV patients, persistent immune activation leads to continuous virus replication. Even with ART reducing plasma viremia below 20 copies/mL, low-level replication persists due to chronic immune activation.
keyPoints: HIV replicates most efficiently in activated CD4+ T cells.
Chronic immune activation provides substrates for persistent HIV replication.
ART can suppress plasma viremia to <20 copies/mL but does not completely eliminate low-level virus replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Microbial Translocation and Persistent Immune Activation in HIV
items.content: Disruption of the gut mucosal barrier due to HIV replication leads to microbial translocation, causing systemic immune activation. This persists even with ART reducing viral load below 20 copies/mL.
keyPoints: HIV disrupts submucosal lymphoid tissue in the gut.
Increased bacterial products like LPS translocate into circulation.
Persistent systemic immune activation occurs despite ART reducing viral load to <20 copies/mL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Co-infections Elevate HIV Viral Load
items.content: Co-infections with pathogens like TB and malaria increase plasma viremia in individuals with HIV. Active TB significantly elevates viral load, which can be reversed by treating the co-infection.
keyPoints: TB is the most common opportunistic infection in people with HIV.
Active TB increases plasma viremia in untreated HIV patients.
Effective treatment of malaria reduces increased HIV viral loads due to acute Plasmodium falciparum infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Chronic Immune Activation and Its Consequences in HIV
items.content: In HIV infection, persistent immune activation leads to various forms of immune dysfunction. This includes hyperactivation of B cells, increased lymphocyte turnover, monocyte activation, and expression of activation markers on T cells. It also results in elevated levels of proinflammatory cytokines and other markers such as IL-6, type I interferons, CRP, CXCL10, D-dimer, neopterin, β2-microglobulin, sCD14, sTNFR, sCD27, sCD163, and sCD40L. These changes contribute to immune exhaustion and dysfunction.
keyPoints: Hyperactivation of B cells leads to hypergammaglobulinemia
Increased lymphocyte turnover and monocyte activation
Elevated levels of IL-6, type I interferons, CRP, CXCL10, D-dimer, neopterin, β2-microglobulin, sCD14, sTNFR, sCD27, sCD163, and sCD40L
Functional exhaustion of virus-specific T cells mediated by upregulation of inhibitory receptors like PD-1, LAG-3, Tim-3, CTLA-4, 2B4, and CD160
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Mechanisms of Apoptosis in HIV Pathogenesis
items.content: HIV infection induces apoptosis through both intrinsic and extrinsic pathways. Activation-induced cell death is triggered by upregulation of death receptors such as Fas/CD95, TNFR1, and TRAIL receptors. Intrinsic apoptosis can occur due to downregulation of antiapoptotic proteins like Bcl-2. Pyroptosis, an inflammatory form of cell death involving caspase 1 and cytokines IL-1β and IL-18, is also significant in HIV pathogenesis.
keyPoints: Apoptosis occurs via intrinsic or extrinsic pathways
Upregulation of death receptors like Fas/CD95, TNFR1, TRAIL receptors
Intrinsic apoptosis due to downregulation of Bcl-2
Pyroptosis involves caspase 1 and release of IL-1β and IL-18
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Chronic Immune Activation on HIV Disease Progression
items.content: Chronic immune activation in HIV leads to immune dysfunction and tissue damage. Regulatory T cells, which help control hyperimmune activation, may be dysfunctional or depleted in advanced HIV disease. This contributes to the progression of HIV by impairing the immune system's ability to respond effectively.
keyPoints: Chronic immune activation leads to immune dysfunction
Regulatory T cells (CD4+, FoxP3+, CD25+) may be dysfunctional or depleted
Impaired immune response due to chronic activation
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.title: Chronic Immune Activation and Inflammation Lead to Accelerated Aging in HIV
items.content: In individuals with HIV, chronic immune activation and inflammation contribute to accelerated aging. This is evident even when patients have achieved viral suppression through ART, defined as plasma viremia <50 copies per milliliter of plasma.
keyPoints: Chronic immune activation and inflammation are linked to multiple medical complications in HIV.
Complications include bone fragility, certain cancers, diabetes, kidney and liver disease, and neurocognitive dysfunction.
These conditions present as accelerated aging despite ART-induced viral suppression (plasma viremia <50 copies per milliliter).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.title: Autoimmune Phenomena and IRIS in the Context of HIV
items.content: Autoimmune phenomena are common in HIV due to chronic immune activation, affecting B and T cells. Despite ART reducing these manifestations, conditions like autoimmune hemolytic anemia and thrombocytopenia can occur. Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) is seen with ART initiation, especially in those with low CD4+ counts, characterized by a paradoxical clinical deterioration.
keyPoints: Autoimmune phenomena include antibodies against autoantigens and proteins from dying cells.
ART has reduced the frequency of autoimmune diseases but not eliminated them.
IRIS is associated with ART initiation in patients with low CD4+ T cell counts, often linked to residual microbial antigens.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Role of Cytokines in Early and Chronic Phases of HIV Infection
items.content: Cytokines play a crucial role in both the early and chronic phases of HIV infection. During the acute phase, a 'cytokine storm' occurs with elevated levels of IFN-α, IL-15, CXCL10, followed by IL-6, IL-12, TNF-α, and a delayed peak of IL-10. These cytokines contribute to immune activation but paradoxically enhance HIV replication.
keyPoints: Cytokine storm in acute phase includes IFN-α, IL-15, CXCL10, IL-6, IL-12, TNF-α, and delayed IL-10.
Persistent cytokines during chronic phase include IFN-γ, RANTES (CCL5), MIP-1β (CCL4), and IL-18.
Plasma levels of IP-10 predict disease progression; IL-6, sCD14, D-dimer are linked to increased mortality risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Cytokines as Predictors of Disease Progression and Mortality in HIV
items.content: Certain cytokines and soluble factors serve as biomarkers for disease progression and mortality risk in HIV patients. Elevated plasma levels of IP-10 predict faster disease progression, while IL-6, sCD14, and D-dimer are associated with increased all-cause mortality and cardiovascular risks.
keyPoints: IP-10 levels predict HIV disease progression.
IL-6, sCD14, and D-dimer linked to increased risk of mortality and cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Mechanisms of Chemokine Inhibition of HIV Strains
items.content: Chemokines such as RANTES (CCL5), MIP-1α (CCL3), and MIP-1β (CCL4) inhibit the infection by blocking the binding of R5 HIV-1 strains to CCR5. SDF-1 inhibits X4 strains by blocking CXCR4, preventing viral entry into host cells.
keyPoints: RANTES (CCL5), MIP-1α (CCL3), and MIP-1β (CCL4) block R5 HIV-1 strain binding to CCR5.
SDF-1 inhibits X4 HIV-1 strains by blocking CXCR4.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.title: Impact of ART on Lymphocyte Turnover in HIV
items.content: Effective antiretroviral therapy (ART) significantly reduces the increased lymphocyte turnover seen in HIV infection. This turnover involves CD4+, CD8+ T cells, and B lymphocytes across peripheral blood and lymphoid tissues.
keyPoints: Increased lymphocyte turnover correlates with plasma viremia.
Effective ART immediately reduces this turnover.
Turnover affects both CD4+ and CD8+ T cells as well as B lymphocytes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.title: Consequences of Increased Lymphocyte Turnover in HIV
items.content: The heightened turnover rate in HIV infection leads to a shift from quiescent lymphocytes to those with higher turnover, resulting in increased cell death. This is particularly notable for CD4+ T cells due to activation-induced death influenced by cytokine alterations.
keyPoints: Higher lymphocyte turnover results in increased cell death.
CD4+ T cells decline more rapidly than CD8+ T cells.
This disparity may be linked to changes in inflammatory and homeostatic cytokines.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.title: HIV Co-Receptor Tropism and Disease Progression
items.content: HIV strains can utilize CCR5 or CXCR4 co-receptors, with R5 viruses predominating early in infection. Transition to X4 variants is associated with rapid CD4+ decline and increased viremia.
keyPoints: R5 viruses predominate early in HIV disease; X4 variants are more common in therapeutic failures or absence of ART.
Approximately half of individuals infected with subtype B virus transition to predominantly X4 virus.
X4 variant detection correlates with rapid CD4+ T cell decline and increased viremia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Mechanism of Chemokine Inhibition on HIV Entry
items.content: Natural ligands for CCR5 and CXCR4 can block HIV entry through steric inhibition, preventing viral binding without affecting signal transduction.
keyPoints: RANTES (CCL5), MIP-1α (CCL3), and MIP-1β (CCL4) block R5 virus entry by binding to CCR5.
SDF-1 blocks X4 virus entry by binding to CXCR4.
Inhibition mechanism involves steric blocking of viral binding, not signal transduction.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Role of Integrin $\alpha4\beta7$ in HIV Transmission and Pathogenesis
items.content: Integrin $\alpha4\beta7$ facilitates HIV transmission at mucosal surfaces by enhancing virologic synapse formation, particularly with high expression on CD4+ T cells.
keyPoints: $\alpha4\beta7^{high}$ CD4+ T cells are more susceptible to HIV infection due to higher metabolic activity and CCR5 expression.
Integrin $\alpha4\beta7$ binds to the V2 loop of gp120, activating LFA-1 for virologic synapse formation.
Transmitted virus binds more efficiently to $\alpha4\beta7$ than diversifying viruses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.title: Why are CD4+ T cells and myeloid lineage cells principal targets for HIV?
items.content: CD4+ T lymphocytes, along with certain myeloid lineage cells, are the primary targets of HIV due to their ability to be productively infected by the virus. This is facilitated by their expression of CD4, although other factors like SAMHD1 in dendritic cells (DCs) and langerin in Langerhans cells can restrict viral replication.
keyPoints: CD4+ T lymphocytes are principal targets for HIV infection.
Myeloid lineage cells also express low levels of CD4, allowing some level of infection.
SAMHD1 in myeloid and plasmacytoid DCs limits HIV replication by depleting dNTPs and degrading viral RNA.
Langerhans cells restrict HIV replication through high expression of langerin despite expressing CD4.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.title: How might thymic precursor cells contribute to impaired CD4+ T cell regeneration in HIV?
items.content: Thymic precursor cells, which express low levels of CD4 and can be infected with HIV in vitro, may play a role in the impaired ability of the CD4+ T cell pool to reconstitute itself. This is particularly relevant when antiretroviral therapy (ART) suppresses plasma viremia below detectable levels.
keyPoints: Thymic precursor cells express low levels of CD4 and can be infected with HIV in vitro.
Infection of thymic epithelial cells transplanted into immunodeficient mice by direct inoculation suggests potential for in vivo infection.
Impaired regeneration of the CD4+ T cell pool may occur due to infection and depletion of these precursor cells, even when ART suppresses plasma viremia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.title: Impact of CD4+ T Cell Depletion in HIV
items.content: In advanced HIV disease, the primary immunopathogenic lesion involves CD4+ T cells. Both quantitative and qualitative defects in these cells impact nearly all immune system functions, highlighting their critical role as inducer/helper cells.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell depletion is central to HIV pathogenesis.
Defects are both quantitative (depletion) and qualitative (dysfunction).
Even with low-normal CD4+ counts early in infection, dysfunction can occur.
Dysfunctions increase as disease progresses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.title: Role of T Helper Subsets in HIV Pathogenesis
items.content: HIV infection disrupts specific CD4+ helper T cell subsets, notably Th17 and TFH cells. Th17 cells are crucial for gut mucosal defense but are depleted by HIV, leading to loss of gut homeostasis. TFH cells increase in number during viremia but may impair the quality of humoral responses.
keyPoints: Th17 cells are important for intestinal mucosa defense and are depleted early in HIV infection.
TFH cells assist B cell function via cytokines like IL-21; their numbers increase in viremic patients.
Increased TFH cells may not benefit the humoral immune response quality.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.title: Consequences of CD4+ T Cell Functional Abnormalities in HIV
items.content: Functional abnormalities in CD4+ T cells, such as impaired IL-2 production and reduced expression of co-stimulatory molecules like CD28, occur early in HIV infection. These defects contribute to the progressive loss of antigen-specific responses, impacting viral control.
keyPoints: Loss of polyfunctional HIV-specific CD4+ T cells occurs early.
Impaired IL-2 production and reduced CD127 expression are noted.
Decreased CD28 expression leads to impaired T cell activation.
Correlation between HIV-specific CD4+ T cell responses and infection control.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.title: Mechanisms of CD4+ T Cell Dysfunction in Early HIV Infection
items.content: CD4+ T cell dysfunction in early HIV infection is not solely due to direct infection and depletion. It involves indirect effects such as binding of soluble viral proteins like gp120 to uninfected cells, leading to immunopathogenic processes.
keyPoints: Direct infection and quantitative depletion of CD4+ T cells are insufficient explanations for profound immunodeficiency in early HIV stages.
Indirect effects include high-affinity binding of gp120 to CD4 molecules on uninfected T cells and monocytes, leading to anergy, apoptosis, and trafficking abnormalities.

Mechanisms involve dysregulation of pathways like the T cell receptor-phosphoinositide pathway, p56ck activation, phosphorylation of focal adhesion kinase, MAP kinase, ras signaling pathways, and downregulation of co-stimulatory molecules CD40 ligand and CD80.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.title: Predictors of CD4+ T Cell Reconstitution in HIV
items.content: The degree and duration of CD4+ T cell decline before ART initiation are critical predictors for reconstitution. Prolonged low counts lead to incomplete recovery, influenced by destruction of lymphoid precursors and disruption of lymphoid architecture.
keyPoints: Degree and duration of CD4+ T cell decline at therapy initiation predict reconstitution success.
Prolonged very low CD4+ T cell counts before ART result in incomplete reconstitution.
Mechanisms include destruction of lymphoid precursor cells and disruption of lymphoid tissue architecture.
Increased serum IL-7 levels during advanced CD4+ T lymphopenia are due to reduced utilization related to loss of CD127-expressing cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.title: CD8+ T Cell Dynamics in HIV Infection
items.content: In HIV infection, CD8+ T cells initially respond to the virus but eventually exhibit functional exhaustion and altered phenotypes. Early in disease progression, HIV-specific CD8+ CTLs emerge and may control viremia temporarily. However, as the disease advances, these cells face challenges from viral escape mutants and persistent immune activation, leading to decreased functionality.
keyPoints: CD8+ T cell reduction occurs despite high viremia during late HIV stages.
HIV-specific CD8+ CTLs can initially decrease plasma viremia but are often overcome by escape mutants in untreated individuals.
Functional exhaustion of CD8+ T cells is marked by upregulation of inhibitory receptors (PD-1, TIGIT, LAG-3, TIM-3).
Abnormal phenotypes include HLA-DR+/CD38+ with reduced IL-2 and IL-7 receptor expression.
Elite controllers maintain high proliferative capacity and perforin expression in CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.title: Prognostic Significance of CD8+ T Cell Phenotypes
items.content: The phenotypic changes in CD8+ T cells during HIV infection can serve as prognostic indicators. Post-seroconversion, individuals with HLA-DR+/CD38- phenotype tend to stabilize their CD4+ counts, whereas those with an HLA-DR+/CD38+ phenotype experience a more aggressive disease course and poorer prognosis.
keyPoints: HLA-DR+/CD38- phenotype in CD8+ T cells post-seroconversion is associated with stabilization of CD4+ T cell counts.
HLA-DR+/CD38+ phenotype correlates with an aggressive HIV progression and poor prognosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.title: Functional Defects in CD8+ T Cells
items.content: In addition to issues with HIV-specific CTLs, functional defects are observed in other MHC-restricted CTLs. The integrity of CD8+ T cell function is compromised by inadequate signals from dysfunctional and depleted CD4+ T cells, exacerbating the immune response challenges.
keyPoints: Functional defects extend beyond HIV-specific CTLs to include responses against other pathogens like influenza and GMV.
CD8+ T cell dysfunction is compounded by quantitative loss and qualitative impairment of CD4+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.title: B Cell Dysfunction in HIV: Mechanisms and Clinical Implications
items.content: In HIV, B cells exhibit aberrant activation leading to terminal differentiation and increased immunoglobulin secretion. This dysfunction is due to viral-induced activation and exhaustion pathways, contributing to hypergammaglobulinemia and immune complex formation.
keyPoints: Increased propensity for B cell terminal differentiation and immunoglobulin secretion
Decreased capacity for cell signaling and proliferative response ex vivo in HIV viremic patients
Hypergammaglobulinemia and circulating immune complexes restrict further B cell stimulation
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.title: Increased Risk of EBV-Related Lymphomas in HIV
items.content: HIV patients have a higher incidence of EBV-related B cell lymphomas due to defective T cell surveillance and increased B cell turnover, which heightens oncogenesis risk.
keyPoints: Defective T cell immune surveillance contributes to increased risk of EBV-related B cell lymphomas
Increased B cell turnover in HIV patients promotes oncogenesis
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.title: Impaired Humoral Immunity and Vaccination Response in HIV
items.content: HIV patients show poor response to immunizations due to memory B cell defects, particularly in those with high viremia. This leads to inadequate humoral responses and increased susceptibility to bacterial infections.
keyPoints: Memory B cell defect demonstrated by poor response to influenza vaccine in HIV
Enrichment of abnormal B cell subsets prone to apoptosis or exhaustion
Increased risk of certain bacterial infections due to impaired humoral immunity
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.title: Monocyte Activation and Biomarkers in HIV
items.content: In individuals with HIV, circulating monocytes are typically normal in number but exhibit increased activation. This is evidenced by elevated levels of soluble CD14 (sCD14) and other biomarkers, even when plasma viremia is suppressed by antiretroviral therapy (ART). The persistent elevation of sCD14 indicates ongoing immune activation and inflammation affecting the monocyte/macrophage lineage.
keyPoints: Circulating monocytes are generally normal in number but show increased activation.
Elevated levels of soluble CD14 (sCD14) serve as an indirect marker of monocyte activation.
sCD14 levels can remain elevated even with suppressed plasma viremia due to ART, indicating residual immune activation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.title: Role of Monocytes in HIV-Related Inflammation
items.content: Activated monocytes in HIV infection secrete inflammatory cytokines and chemokines, contributing to the systemic inflammation observed. These include CXCL10, IL-1β, and IL-6. The expansion of monocytes expressing CD16, HLA-DR, CD40, and CD86 is particularly noted in viremic individuals.
keyPoints: Activated monocytes secrete inflammatory cytokines such as CXCL10, IL-1β, and IL-6.
Expansion of monocytes with markers like CD16, HLA-DR, CD40, and CD86 is observed, especially in viremic individuals.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.title: Tissue Macrophages as HIV Reservoirs
items.content: Tissue macrophages, including those in the brain and lung, are easily infected by HIV and serve as persistent reservoirs. They contribute to viral persistence during inflammatory responses associated with opportunistic infections, posing challenges for HIV eradication despite ART.
