keyPoints: Tissue macrophages can be productively infected by HIV.
They act as persistent reservoirs of the virus.
Contribute to viral persistence during inflammation from opportunistic infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.title: NK Cell Dysfunction in HIV Infection
items.content: HIV infection leads to functional abnormalities in NK cells, which worsen as the disease progresses. Initially, there is increased activation of NK cells with maintained IFN-γ secretion but reduced cytotoxic function due to altered maturation. During chronic infection, both cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion are impaired.
keyPoints: NK cell impairments increase with HIV progression.
Early HIV infection shows increased NK cell activation but reduced cytotoxic function.
Chronic HIV infection impairs NK cell cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion.
Expansion of anergic CD56-/CD16+ NK cell subset is associated with high virus replication levels.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.title: Impact of iNKRs and NCRs on NK Cell Function in HIV
items.content: The relative expression of inhibitory NK cell receptors (iNKRs) and natural cytotoxicity receptors (NCRs), along with their ligands, affects NK cell antiviral functions. Polymorphisms in these receptor alleles are linked to HIV-1 disease outcomes, suggesting early control may be mediated by NK cell responses.
keyPoints: iNKRs and NCR expression impacts NK cell antiviral functions.
Polymorphisms in iNKR and NCR alleles affect HIV-1 outcomes.
Early HIV control may involve cytotoxic NK cell-mediated responses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Impact of CCR5 Δ32 Polymorphism on HIV Acquisition and Progression
items.content: The CCR5 Δ32 polymorphism significantly influences the risk of acquiring HIV, virologic set point, and progression to AIDS. Individuals homozygous for this allele (Δ32/Δ32) are highly resistant to HIV infection due to lack of CCR5 surface expression, while heterozygotes have a reduced risk.
keyPoints: CCR5 Δ32 deletion leads to resistance against R5 strains of HIV.
Homozygous individuals (Δ32/Δ32) are highly resistant to acquiring HIV infection.
Up to 18% of Europeans are heterozygous for the CCR5 Δ32 allele, reducing their risk of HIV acquisition.
The allele is rare in non-European populations.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Role of Genetic Polymorphisms in HIV Pathogenesis and ART Response
items.content: Genetic polymorphisms influence various aspects of HIV pathogenesis, including acquisition risk, virologic set point, disease progression to AIDS, immune reconstitution post-ART, and adverse reactions to antiretroviral agents.
keyPoints: Polymorphisms affect the risk of acquiring HIV, steady-state viral levels, and rate of progression to AIDS (CD4+ T cell count < 200 cells/mm³).
Genetic factors influence immune reconstitution and non-AIDS-associated diseases after ART initiation.
Adverse reactions to antiretroviral agents are also influenced by genetic polymorphisms.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.title: Population-Specific Distribution of CCR5 Δ32 Allele
items.content: The distribution of the CCR5 Δ32 allele varies by population, being more prevalent in individuals of European ancestry. This polymorphism's evolutionary emergence is speculated to be due to historical pandemics.
keyPoints: Approximately 1% of Europeans are homozygous for the CCR5 Δ32 allele.
Up to 18% of Europeans are heterozygous for this allele.
The allele is rare in non-European populations.
Evolutionary pressure from ancestral pandemics, such as the plague, may have contributed to its prevalence.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Impact of CCR5 Haplotypes on HIV Acquisition and Disease Progression
items.content: CCR5 haplotypes influence susceptibility to HIV infection and disease progression. Specific haplotypes affect gene expression levels, which in turn modulate the risk of acquiring HIV and the speed of disease progression.
keyPoints: Homozygosity for CCR5-HHE is associated with increased risk of acquiring HIV, rapid progression to AIDS, and reduced immune recovery during ART.
The ancestral HHA haplotype is linked to slower disease progression in African populations.
Pairing of HHC/HHG*2 genotype reduces the risk of acquiring HIV and slows disease progression.
CCR5-HHE paired with HHG*2 has opposite effects, increasing risk and accelerating progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Role of CCR5 Δ32 Polymorphism in HIV Resistance
items.content: The CCR5 Δ32 polymorphism confers strong resistance to HIV infection, leading to the development of novel therapies targeting this pathway.
keyPoints: CCR5 Δ32/Δ32 genotype is associated with strong resistance to HIV infection.
Entry inhibitors like maraviroc block CCR5 interaction with HIV envelope protein.
Allogeneic stem cell transplantation from a donor lacking CCR5 expression (due to Δ32/Δ32) resulted in 13 years without detectable HIV-1 post-transplant.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.title: Gene Editing and Cellular Therapies for HIV Cure
items.content: Novel cellular therapies involving gene editing of CCR5 in CD4+ T cells show promise but face challenges due to latent viral reservoirs.
keyPoints: Autologous transplantation of CD4+ T cells with ex vivo CCR5 gene inactivation is a novel therapeutic approach.
Mixed success in cellular strategies, primarily due to persistent latent HIV reservoirs.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.title: Impact of HLA-B Alleles on HIV Disease Progression
items.content: Certain HLA-B alleles are associated with different outcomes in HIV infection. Protective alleles like HLA-B$^{57}$ and HLA-B$^{27}$ correlate with slower disease progression, lower viral set points, and stronger CD8+ T cell responses. Conversely, the HLA-B$^{35}$ allele is linked to faster AIDS progression and higher viral loads.
keyPoints: HLA-B$^{57}$ and HLA-B$^{27}$ alleles are associated with slower disease progression and lower virologic set points.
Carriage of these protective alleles is prevalent among elite controllers who spontaneously control HIV replication.
The HLA-B$^{35}$ allele correlates with faster progression to AIDS and higher viral load.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.title: Role of Extended HLA Haplotypes in HIV Progression
items.content: The extended HLA ancestral haplotype 8.1, characterized by the presence of HLA-A1, HLA-B8, and HLA-DR3 alleles, is associated with rapid CD4+ T cell decline and faster AIDS progression in HIV-seropositive individuals.
keyPoints: AH 8.1 haplotype is common in persons of European descent (10%) and linked to autoimmune diseases.
In HIV-positive individuals, AH 8.1 correlates with rapid CD4+ T cell decline and faster AIDS progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.title: Potential Therapeutic Targeting of NKG2A in HIV Treatment
items.content: The HLA-B–21M allele, while not affecting HLA-B expression, is linked with higher HLA-A and HLA-E expression. Higher HLA-E levels inhibit NK cell activity via the NKG2A receptor, suggesting that targeting NKG2A could be a therapeutic strategy in HIV.
keyPoints: HLA-B–21M allele is associated with increased HLA-A and HLA-E expression.
Higher HLA-E levels inhibit NK cells through NKG2A, reducing their ability to eliminate virally infected cells.
Targeting the NKG2A receptor may offer a new therapeutic avenue for HIV treatment.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.title: Role of HLA Class I Loci SNVs in HIV Virologic Control
items.content: Certain single nucleotide variants (SNVs) within or near the HLA class I loci are associated with better virologic control in individuals of European ancestry. These include SNVs in PSORS1C3, HLA-C, MICA, and HCP5 genes. The protective effects may be due to linkage disequilibrium with known protective HLA-B alleles, such as HLA-B*57:01. Specifically, the HLA-C SNV (rs9264942; T→C) increases HLA-C expression by disrupting miR-148a binding, leading to better HIV outcomes.
keyPoints: SNVs in PSORS1C3, HLA-C, MICA, and HCP5 genes associated with virologic control
Protective HLA-C SNV (rs9264942; T→C) increases HLA-C expression
miR-148a binding disrupted on C-deletion haplotype, leading to higher HLA-C expression
Linkage disequilibrium with protective HLA-B*57:01 and HLA-B*27:05 alleles
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.title: Impact of rs1015164G→A SNV on HIV Viral Load
items.content: The SNV rs1015164G→A, located approximately 34 kb downstream of the CCR5 loci, is associated with higher viral load set points and lower CD4+ T cell counts in therapy-naïve individuals. This variant increases expression of the lncRNA CCR5AS, which correlates with elevated CCR5 mRNA levels. The effects are suggested to be independent of the CCR5-HHE haplotype, although both variants show high linkage disequilibrium.
keyPoints: rs1015164G→A associated with higher viral load and lower CD4+ T cell counts
Increased expression of lncRNA CCR5AS linked to elevated CCR5 mRNA levels
Effects independent but in high linkage disequilibrium with CCR5-HHE haplotype
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.title: Genetic Risk Factors for HIVAN and Their Impact on Susceptibility
items.content: HIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN) is more prevalent in individuals of African descent, with genetic polymorphisms in the MYH9 and APOL1 genes being significant determinants. The presence of two APOL1 risk alleles accounts for nearly 35% of HIVAN cases.
keyPoints: Polymorphisms in MYH9 and APOL1 genes are strong determinants of susceptibility to HIVAN, especially in persons of African descent.
Carrying two APOL1 risk alleles explains approximately 35% of the risk for developing HIVAN.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.title: APOBEC3 Variants and Their Role in HIV Progression and Pneumocystis Pneumonia Risk
items.content: Genetic variations in the APOBEC3 family, particularly a common haplotype derived from 6 SNVs in the APOBEC3F gene, are associated with lower viral load set points, slower progression to AIDS, and delayed development of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP).
keyPoints: A common haplotype from 6 SNVs in APOBEC3F is linked to a significantly lower viral load set point.
This genetic variation is associated with slower progression to AIDS and delayed PCP development.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.title: HLA-B*57:01 Allele and Abacavir Hypersensitivity Risk
items.content: The HLA-B*57:01 allele is associated with a higher risk of hypersensitivity reactions to abacavir, despite its association with a slower HIV disease course. Pharmacogenetic screening for this allele is recommended before initiating abacavir treatment.
keyPoints: HLA-B*57:01 allele increases the risk of abacavir-associated hypersensitivity reactions (2–9% of cases).
Pharmacogenetic screening for HLA-B*57:01 is advised prior to starting abacavir therapy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Mechanisms of HIV Entry into the Brain
items.content: HIV enters the brain through infected and immune-activated macrophages that induce adhesion molecules on brain endothelium. This process involves E-selectin, VCAM-1, ICAM-1 expression in glial cells, and disruption of tight junctions by HIV Tat protein.
keyPoints: HIV-infected macrophages induce adhesion molecules like E-selectin and VCAM-1 on brain endothelium.
HIV gp120 enhances ICAM-1 expression in glial cells.
HIV Tat disrupts tight junctions of brain endothelial cells, facilitating HIV entry into the CNS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Indirect Pathways of Neuronal Damage in HIV
items.content: HIV-mediated neuronal damage is indirect, involving viral proteins gp120 and Tat that trigger the release of neurotoxins from macrophages and astrocytes. These toxins can kill neurons through mechanisms like NMDA receptor activation and oxidative stress.
keyPoints: Viral proteins gp120 and Tat induce neurotoxin release from macrophages and astrocytes.
Neurotoxins cause neuronal death via NMDA receptor activation and oxidative stress.
HIV gp120 can antagonize vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP) function, elevate intracellular calcium, and decrease neurotrophic factor levels.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Correlation between MCP-1 Levels and HIV Encephalopathy in ART-Naive Patients
items.content: In HIV infection, elevated levels of monocyte chemotactic protein 1 (MCP-1 or CCL-2) in the brain and CSF correlate best with the presence and degree of HIV encephalopathy in patients who are not yet on antiretroviral therapy (ART).
keyPoints: Elevated MCP-1 levels in brain and CSF correlate with HIV encephalopathy.
Correlation is most significant in ART-naive patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Role of Astrocytes in HIV Neuropathogenesis
items.content: Astrocytes contribute to HIV neuropathogenesis through mechanisms such as reactive gliosis or astrocytosis, proliferation induced by TNF-α and IL-6, and the induction of HIV expression via astrocyte-derived IL-6. They may also downregulate macrophage-produced neurotoxins.
keyPoints: Astrocytes exhibit reactive gliosis in HIV-infected brains.
TNF-α and IL-6 induce astrocyte proliferation.
Astrocyte-derived IL-6 can induce HIV expression in vitro.
Astrocytes may downregulate macrophage-produced neurotoxins.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.title: Epidemiologic Forms and HHV-8 Association in KS
items.content: Kaposi's Sarcoma (KS) manifests in four epidemiologic forms, with the form associated with HIV-1 infection being an opportunistic disease. Human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8), also known as Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV), is universally linked to KS across all forms. HHV-8 DNA sequences are found in KS lesions, and patients with KS are nearly always seropositive for HHV-8.
keyPoints: Four epidemiologic forms of KS: classic, equatorial African, organ transplantation-related, and HIV-associated.
HHV-8 (KSHV) is linked to all forms of KS.
HHV-8 DNA sequences are universally found in KS lesions.
Patients with KS are virtually all seropositive for HHV-8.
Prevalence of HHV-8 seropositivity: 30–35% in men with HIV, ~4% in women with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.title: Role of HHV-8 and Cytokines in KS Pathogenesis
items.content: HHV-8 is the etiologic agent of KS, transforming endothelial cells in vitro. The development of KS involves an activated state mediated by cytokines such as TNF-$ \\$alpha $, IL-1$ \beta $, IL-6, GM-CSF, basic fibroblast growth factor, and oncostatin M. KSHV-derived IL-6 induces proliferation of lymphoma cells and inhibits the cytostatic effects of IFN-$ \alpha $.
keyPoints: HHV-8 is the etiologic agent of KS.
Endothelial cells can be transformed by HHV-8 in vitro.
Cytokines involved: TNF-$ \\$alpha $, IL-1$ \beta $, IL-6, GM-CSF, basic fibroblast growth factor, oncostatin M.
KSHV-derived IL-6 induces proliferation of lymphoma cells and inhibits IFN-$ \alpha $ effects.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.title: Understanding Immune Response Dynamics in HIV Infection
items.content: Following primary infection, individuals with HIV experience a robust immune response that significantly reduces plasma viremia levels and delays the onset of clinical disease for about 10 years in untreated cases. This response involves both humoral and cell-mediated immunity, targeting multiple antigenic determinants on the virus and infected cells.
keyPoints: Robust immune response curtails plasma viremia
Delays clinical disease development by a median of 10 years in untreated individuals
Involves humoral and cell-mediated immunity
Targets multiple antigenic determinants of HIV
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.title: Challenges in Identifying Key Immunologic Mechanisms in HIV Progression
items.content: Despite extensive research, it remains unclear which immunologic mechanisms are crucial for delaying HIV progression or contribute to the pathogenesis of HIV disease. This uncertainty complicates efforts to develop an effective vaccine.
keyPoints: Unclear which immunologic mechanisms delay infection progression
Uncertainty hampers vaccine development
Research has not yet identified key effector mechanisms
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.title: Timing and Detection of HIV Antibodies
items.content: Antibodies to HIV typically appear within 3–6 weeks after primary infection, almost invariably by 12 weeks. These antibodies are crucial for the diagnosis of HIV through screening tests that also measure plasma levels of HIV RNA and p24 antigen.
keyPoints: HIV-specific antibodies usually appear within 3–6 weeks post-infection; almost always by 12 weeks.
Detection of these antibodies is fundamental to most HIV diagnostic screening tests.
Screening involves measuring plasma levels of HIV RNA, p24 antigen, and antibodies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.title: Neutralizing Antibodies and Immune Escape
items.content: Neutralizing antibodies against HIV envelope proteins gp120 and gp41 are protective but can be evaded by the virus through mutations, such as adding N-linked glycosylation sites to form a glycan shield.
keyPoints: Only gp120 and gp41 elicit neutralizing antibodies.
Neutralizing antibodies appear after approximately 12 to 24 weeks of infection.
Virus can escape these antibodies by mutating and forming a glycan shield.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.title: Role of Antibodies in ADCC and Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Antibodies against HIV envelope proteins can mediate antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC), aiding in the destruction of infected cells. However, they may also contribute to disease pathogenesis by facilitating infection or causing bystander killing.
keyPoints: Anti-envelope antibodies participate in ADCC, especially early in infection.
Antibodies directed against gp41 can facilitate infection via Fc receptor-mediated enhancement.
ADCC antibodies might kill uninfected CD4+ T cells through bystander killing.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.title: Role of Complement System in HIV Infection
items.content: The complement system, part of innate immunity, consists of ~30 proteins that can be activated by HIV. Despite weak lysis due to host cell regulatory proteins, complement-opsonized HIV virions may have increased infectivity.
keyPoints: ~30 proteins in the complement system
Complement activation by HIV
Weak lysis due to host cell regulatory proteins
Potential increase in infectivity of complement-opsonized HIV
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.title: Importance of CD4+ T Cells in HIV Immune Response
items.content: CD4+ T cells are crucial for orchestrating immune responses against HIV by aiding B cells and CD8+ T cells. They can be detected via flow cytometry or proliferation assays, but no clear correlation exists between their levels and plasma HIV RNA.
keyPoints: Detection of HIV-specific CD4+ T cells using flow cytometry
Role in helping B cells and CD8+ T cells
Shift from IL-2 to IFN-$\gamma$ production under high viral loads
No clear correlation between CD4+ T cell levels and plasma HIV RNA
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.title: Function of CD8+ T Cells in Controlling HIV
items.content: CD8+ T cells, including CTLs and cytokine-producing cells, are identified early post-infection. They exert selective pressure on viral evolution but often fail to control infection effectively, except in elite nonprogressors.
keyPoints: Identification of CD8+ T cells within weeks post-HIV infection
Production of perforins, granzyme, and cytokines like IFN-$\gamma$
Direct correlation between CD8+ T cell levels producing IFN-$\gamma$ and plasma HIV RNA
Elite nonprogressors have effective CD8+ T cell responses controlling viral replication
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.title: Mechanisms of Cell-Mediated Immunity in HIV Infection
items.content: Cell-mediated immunity to HIV includes non-cytolytic CD8+ T cell suppression, ADCC, and NK cell activity. Non-cytolytic CD8+ T cells inhibit HIV replication via soluble factors like CC-chemokines (RANTES, MIP-1α, MIP-1β) that block the CCR5 co-receptor for R5 strains of HIV-1. ADCC involves NK cells killing HIV-expressing cells with specific antibodies against HIV antigens. NK cells can also directly kill HIV-infected cells by recognizing altered class I MHC molecules.
keyPoints: Non-cytolytic CD8+ T cell-mediated suppression does not require HLA compatibility.
CC-chemokines (RANTES, MIP-1α, MIP-1β) block the CCR5 co-receptor for R5 HIV-1 strains.
ADCC involves NK cells armed with antibodies against HIV antigens.
NK cells can directly kill HIV-infected cells by recognizing altered class I MHC molecules.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.title: Advancements in HIV Screening and Monitoring Techniques
items.content: The development of sensitive screening tests for HIV began in 1984, with antibody testing starting in March 1985. The p24 antigen capture assay was added in 1996 to identify early infections before antibodies develop, reducing the window period from 22 days (antibody testing) to 16 days. In 2002, nucleic acid testing (NAT) further reduced this window period to 12 days. Monitoring plasma viremia and CD4+ T lymphocyte levels is crucial for managing HIV progression.
keyPoints: Antibody testing had a window period of 22 days.
p24 antigen capture assay reduced the window period to 16 days.
Nucleic acid testing (NAT) further reduced the window period to 12 days.
Monitoring plasma viremia and CD4+ T lymphocyte levels is essential for HIV management.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Confirmatory Testing for HIV Diagnosis
items.content: A positive fourth-generation EIA test should be confirmed with a more specific assay, such as an HIV-1 or HIV-2-specific antibody immunoassay or plasma HIV RNA level, due to potential false positives.
keyPoints: Fourth-generation assays have high sensitivity (>99.5%) but may yield false positives in low-risk populations (up to 10%).
Factors contributing to false-positive EIA results include recent vaccinations, infections, and certain medical conditions.
CDC recommends confirmation of a positive fourth-generation assay with an HIV-1 or HIV-2-specific immunoassay or plasma HIV RNA level.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Diagnosis in Early and Acute HIV Infection
items.content: Use of a 'detuned' assay can help estimate recent HIV-1 infection by comparing results with a standard EIA, aiding in the diagnosis of acute infections.
keyPoints: Antibodies to HIV typically appear 3–12 weeks post-infection.
A detuned assay is less sensitive and helps identify recent HIV-1 infections when compared to a standard EIA.
Acute HIV-1 infection can be diagnosed with positive NAT results in the presence of negative antibody tests.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Guidelines for Serologic Testing and Diagnosis
items.content: A structured approach using fourth-generation assays followed by confirmatory testing ensures accurate diagnosis of HIV infection.
keyPoints: Initial testing should be a fourth-generation HIV-1/2 antigen antibody immunoassay.
Indeterminate or positive results require repeat testing; if negative twice, consider initial result as technical error.
If repeat tests are indeterminate or positive, proceed to HIV-1/HIV-2 antibody differentiation and NAT for final diagnosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Confirmatory Testing for Positive Rapid HIV Tests
items.content: Rapid point-of-care tests, including home-use kits like OraQuick, provide quick results but require confirmation with standard laboratory testing due to potential false positives.
keyPoints: Rapid test kits can give results in 1-60 minutes.
Positive rapid test results must be confirmed by standard lab testing.
OraQuick is an FDA-approved home-use kit providing results in ~20 minutes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Use of p24 Antigen Capture Assay
items.content: The p24 antigen capture assay is useful for detecting acute HIV infection before antibodies develop, particularly in the context of the acute HIV syndrome.
keyPoints: p24 antigen levels are detectable in ~30% of untreated individuals.
Levels increase to ~50% with weak acid treatment to dissociate complexes.
Fourth-generation assays now combine p24 antigen and antibody testing for initial screening.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: HIV RNA Measurement in Diagnosis
items.content: Measuring HIV RNA levels aids in diagnosing acute or neonatal infections and monitoring ART response, with high sensitivity but potential for false positives.
keyPoints: Commercial RNA tests detect 20-50 copies/mL of plasma.
Research assays can detect as low as one copy/mL.
Positive EIA combined with positive HIV RNA assay is the 'gold standard' for diagnosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.title: Use of RT-PCR in Diagnosing HIV When Serology is Indeterminate
items.content: RT-PCR can be employed to diagnose HIV infection by quantifying HIV RNA or detecting proviral DNA, particularly when standard serologic tests are inconclusive. This technique involves creating cDNA from HIV RNA using tRNA $ Lys_{3} $ as a primer, followed by amplification and characterization via PCR.
keyPoints: RT-PCR is useful for diagnosing HIV in cases of indeterminate EIA or Western blot results.
It quantifies HIV RNA in plasma or detects proviral DNA in peripheral blood mononuclear cells.
Applicable when serologic tests are unreliable, such as in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia or advanced HIV disease.
RT-PCR should be used only after standard serologic testing fails to provide a definitive result.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.title: Role of RT-PCR in Prognosis and Research
items.content: Beyond diagnosis, RT-PCR serves as a prognostic tool and aids in research by amplifying specific regions of the HIV genome for sequence analysis. This helps study sequence diversity and microbial resistance to antiretroviral agents.
keyPoints: RT-PCR is diagnostic and prognostic for HIV infection.
It facilitates sequence analysis of the HIV genome, aiding studies on sequence diversity.
Helps in researching microbial resistance to antiretroviral treatments.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: CD4+ T Cell Counts as Prognostic and Therapeutic Guides
items.content: CD4+ T cell counts are crucial for assessing the immunologic competence of patients with HIV. They guide prophylactic treatments based on specific thresholds.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell count <200/μL indicates high risk for P. jirovecii; prophylaxis is recommended.
CD4+ T cell count <50/μL signifies high risk for CMV, MAC, and/or T. gondii; primary prophylaxis for MAC unless ART is initiated.
Measurements should be repeated before significant management changes based on CD4+ counts alone.
Routine measurements: at diagnosis, every 3–6 months for the first 2 years, more frequently if declining.
For stable patients on ART with HIV RNA <50 copies/mL and CD4 300–500/μL, annual monitoring may suffice.
CD4 count >500/μL makes further monitoring optional; CD4+ T cell percentage can be a better indicator in certain conditions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: HIV RNA Determinations for Monitoring and Prognosis
items.content: Quantitative measurement of HIV RNA levels is essential in monitoring the progression and treatment response in HIV patients.
keyPoints: RT-PCR assay used to quantify HIV RNA, detecting as few as 20 copies/mL with commercial assays.
Levels below detection thresholds should not be termed 'undetectable' due to potential residual viral presence.
More sensitive techniques can detect down to one copy per milliliter; tissue studies reveal virus in nearly all patients.
Exceptions include patients post-cytoreductive therapy and CCR5 Δ32 donor transplants.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Situations Affecting CD4+ T Cell Count Reliability
items.content: Certain clinical conditions can lead to misleading CD4+ T cell counts, necessitating alternative measures of immune function.
keyPoints: HTLV-1/HIV co-infection may result in elevated CD4+ counts not reflecting true immune competence.
In hypersplenism or post-splenectomy patients, and those on bone marrow-suppressing medications like IFN-α, CD4+ T cell percentage is more reliable.
A CD4+ T cell percentage of 15 is comparable to a count of 200/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Monitoring HIV RNA Levels for Treatment Effectiveness
items.content: HIV RNA levels should be measured at diagnosis and monitored approximately every 4 weeks until a new steady-state level is achieved. Effective antiretroviral therapy typically reduces plasma HIV RNA to <50 copies/mL within 6 months.
keyPoints: Measurements of changes in HIV RNA levels are crucial for assessing disease progression, immune system activation, and drug resistance development.
HIV RNA levels should be monitored every 4 weeks initially until a steady-state level is reached.
Effective ART usually reduces plasma HIV RNA to <50 copies/mL within 6 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: HIV Resistance Testing for Antiretroviral Therapy
items.content: Resistance testing is recommended at initial diagnosis and upon virologic failure. It helps in selecting effective antiretroviral agents by identifying resistance profiles.
keyPoints: Resistance testing should be done at initial diagnosis and during virologic failure.
Genotypic assays compare patient HIV sequences with known resistant strains; phenotypic assays assess viral growth in the presence of drugs.
Effective resistance testing requires an HIV-1 RNA level above 500–1000 copies/mL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.title: Co-Receptor Tropism Assays for Maraviroc Use
items.content: Maraviroc, a CCR5 antagonist, requires determination of viral tropism. Phenotypic assays or genotypic algorithms predict whether the virus is CCR5-tropic.
keyPoints: Maraviroc is effective only against CCR5-tropic viruses.
Phenotypic assays (e.g., Profile assay) are used to determine co-receptor tropism.
Genotypic assays of the V3 region can predict viral tropism using computer algorithms, though validation data is limited.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.title: Markers of Disease Activity in Acute HIV Infection
items.content: Various laboratory tests have been studied as potential markers for monitoring disease activity in acute HIV infection. These include quantitative culture of replication-competent HIV, levels of specific proteins and cytokines (e.g., $ \beta_{2} $-microglobulin, soluble IL-2 receptor), and activation markers on T cells (CD38, HLA-DR, PD-1). Despite their value in understanding pathogenesis, these tests are not currently central to patient monitoring.
keyPoints: Quantitative culture of replication-competent HIV from plasma or cells
Circulating levels of $ \beta_{2} $-microglobulin, soluble IL-2 receptor, IgA, acid-labile endogenous IFN, TNF-$ \alpha $
Activation markers such as CD38, HLA-DR, PD-1 on T cells
Non-specific serologic markers like IL-6, p-dimer, sCD14 correlate with all-cause mortality
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of Acute HIV Infection
items.content: Acute HIV infection occurs in 50–70% of individuals, typically presenting with symptoms ~3–6 weeks post-infection. Symptoms include fever, skin rash, pharyngitis, and myalgia, resembling acute infectious mononucleosis. These symptoms are less frequent in those infected via injection drug use compared to sexual contact.
keyPoints: 50–70% of individuals experience an acute clinical syndrome
Symptoms appear ~3–6 weeks after primary infection
Common symptoms: fever, skin rash, pharyngitis, myalgia
Less frequent in injection drug use compared to sexual contact
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.title: Immunologic Changes During Acute HIV Infection
items.content: Acute HIV infection is characterized by immunologic changes, including reduced total lymphocytes and T cell subsets initially. An inversion of the CD4+/CD8+ ratio occurs due to a rise in CD8+ T cells. Lymphadenopathy occurs in ~70% of cases, with most patients recovering spontaneously but some experiencing prolonged low disease activity.
keyPoints: Initial reduction in total lymphocytes and T cell subsets
Inversion of the CD4+/CD8+ T cell ratio due to increased CD8+ cells
Lymphadenopathy occurs in ~70% of individuals
Most recover spontaneously with stable or slightly rebounding CD4+ counts
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.title: Correlation Between HIV RNA Levels and Disease Progression Rate
items.content: During the asymptomatic stage of HIV, known as clinical latency, disease progression is directly correlated with plasma HIV RNA levels. Patients with higher HIV RNA levels progress to symptomatic disease more rapidly than those with lower levels.
keyPoints: Median time from initial infection to clinical disease in untreated patients is ~10 years.
Rate of disease progression correlates with HIV RNA levels.
Patients with high plasma HIV RNA levels progress faster to symptomatic disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.title: Significance of CD4+ T Cell Count in Predicting Opportunistic Infections Risk
items.content: During the asymptomatic stage, untreated patients experience an average decline in CD4+ T cell count of ~50/μL per year. A CD4+ T cell count falling below 200/μL indicates severe immunodeficiency, significantly increasing the risk for opportunistic infections and neoplasms.
keyPoints: Average rate of CD4+ T cell decline is ~50/μL per year in untreated patients during asymptomatic stage.
CD4+ T cell count <200/μL marks severe immunodeficiency, increasing risk for opportunistic infections and neoplasms.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Importance of CD4+ T Cell Count in HIV Disease Progression
items.content: The severity and type of illnesses associated with HIV infection are closely linked to the patient's CD4+ T cell count. As this count declines, particularly below 200/μL, patients experience more severe complications, including those defining AIDS.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL indicate severe and life-threatening complications of HIV infection.
A diagnosis of AIDS is made when CD4+ T cell count falls below 200/μL in individuals aged 6 years and older, or if they develop specific opportunistic diseases.
Severe opportunistic infections are more common with lower CD4+ T cell counts.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Impact of ART on HIV-Related Illnesses
items.content: The introduction and widespread use of antiretroviral therapy (ART) have significantly reduced the incidence of opportunistic infections in HIV patients, shifting the disease burden towards non-AIDS-related illnesses.
keyPoints: ART has dramatically decreased the incidence of secondary infections in HIV patients.
Non-AIDS events now dominate the disease burden for patients on ART.
~25% of deaths in treated HIV patients are due to AIDS-related illnesses, with a similar percentage from non-AIDS malignancies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Management and Prognosis of Pulmonary Disease in HIV
items.content: Pulmonary disease is a frequent complication in HIV patients, with pneumonia being the most common manifestation. The management includes addressing both primary infections and opportunistic pathogens.
keyPoints: Pneumonia is one of the top 10 AIDS-defining illnesses.
Bacterial pneumonia occurs at a rate of 0.8–2.0 cases per 100 person-years in HIV patients.
Common pathogens include S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae, with increased susceptibility due to altered immune function.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Increased Incidence of Pneumococcal Disease in HIV
items.content: Patients with untreated HIV have significantly higher risks for pneumococcal pneumonia and bacteremia, necessitating specific prophylactic measures.
keyPoints: 6-fold increase in incidence of pneumococcal pneumonia in untreated HIV patients.
100-fold increase in incidence of pneumococcal bacteremia.
Baseline CD4+ T cell count for first episode of pneumococcal pneumonia ~300/μL.
Prophylactic immunization with conjugated and 23-valent pneumococcal vaccines recommended, especially when CD4+ >200/μL.
Incidence of bacterial pneumonia reduced by half upon smoking cessation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Pneumocystis Pneumonia in HIV Patients
items.content: PCP remains a common cause of pneumonia among HIV patients, with specific risk factors and diagnostic criteria.
keyPoints: PCP is the most common cause of pneumonia in U.S. HIV patients.
Incidence of PCP about 1 case per 100 person-years.
30% of cases occur in unaware HIV-positive individuals.
79% of PCP patients have CD4+ <100/μL; 95% have <200/μL.
Prophylaxis recommended for CD4+ <200/μL or CD4 percentage <15.
Primary and secondary PCP occur at median CD4+ counts of 36/μL and 10/μL, respectively.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.title: Diagnosis and Presentation of Pneumocystis Pneumonia
items.content: PCP presents with specific symptoms and diagnostic findings in HIV patients, guiding treatment decisions.
keyPoints: Common PCP symptoms: fever, nonproductive cough, retrosternal chest pain.
Chest x-ray may show normal film or faint bilateral interstitial infiltrate; dense perihilar infiltrate is unusual.
Upper lobe cavitary disease can mimic TB in patients on prophylactic aerosolized pentamidine.
CT may show patchy ground-glass appearance.
Arterial blood-gases indicate hypoxemia with decreased PaO2 and increased a-a gradient.
Definitive diagnosis requires organism detection via induced sputum, bronchoalveolar lavage, or biopsy; PCR can be used if histologic exams fail.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Management and Prophylaxis of Pneumocystis jirovecii Pneumonia in HIV
items.content: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) is a significant opportunistic infection in HIV patients. The standard treatment for PCP involves trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX), with alternatives like dapsone/trimethoprim, clindamycin/primaquine, and atovaquone for mild to moderate cases. For severe disease or intolerance to TMP-SMX, IV pentamidine is recommended. Glucocorticoid therapy should be added if PaO2 < 70 mmHg or a-a gradient >35 mmHg. Prophylaxis with TMP-SMX is indicated for CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL or <15%, unexplained fever over two weeks, or history of oropharyngeal candidiasis.
keyPoints: Standard treatment: TMP-SMX
Alternatives: dapsone/trimethoprim, clindamycin/primaquine, atovaquone
Severe disease treatment: IV pentamidine
Glucocorticoid therapy for PaO2 < 70 mmHg or a-a gradient >35 mmHg
Prophylaxis indications: CD4+ T cell count <200/μL or <15%, unexplained fever, history of candidiasis
Preferred prophylaxis regimen: TMP-SMX one double-strength tablet daily
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Tuberculosis in HIV-Infected Patients
items.content: Mycobacterium tuberculosis has resurged due to the HIV epidemic, with TB being a leading cause of death among AIDS patients. In untreated HIV patients, active TB is more likely by a factor of 100 compared to HIV-negative individuals. The risk of reactivation TB in asymptomatic HIV-positive patients with a positive PPD skin test is 7–10% per year. Active TB often develops early in HIV infection and can accelerate the progression of HIV. Discontinuation of PCP prophylaxis is possible with effective ART maintaining CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL for at least three months.
keyPoints: TB resurgence linked to HIV epidemic
One-third of AIDS-related deaths associated with TB
Active TB risk in untreated HIV patients: 100x higher than HIV-negative
Reactivation TB risk: 7–10% per year for positive PPD skin test in HIV-positive
Early development of active TB in HIV infection
Discontinuation criteria for PCP prophylaxis: CD4+ T cell count >200/μL with effective ART
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.title: Clinical Manifestations of TB in HIV Patients
items.content: The clinical presentation of tuberculosis (TB) in HIV-infected individuals varies based on CD4+ T cell counts. Higher CD4+ counts typically present with pulmonary reactivation symptoms like fever, cough, and cavitary apical disease. Lower CD4+ counts are associated with disseminated TB. The median CD4+ T cell count at TB presentation is 326/μL.
keyPoints: Higher CD4+ counts: typical pulmonary reactivation
Lower CD4+ counts: more common disseminated disease
Median CD4+ T cell count at TB presentation: 326/μL
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: Screening for Tuberculosis in HIV Patients
items.content: All patients with HIV should be screened for TB as part of their initial evaluation due to the high prevalence and potential asymptomatic nature of TB in advanced HIV.
keyPoints: 60–80% of people with HIV have pulmonary TB, 30–40% have extrapulmonary disease
Screening is crucial because some patients may be asymptomatic
Respiratory isolation and negative-pressure rooms are recommended when considering a diagnosis of pulmonary TB
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: Managing TB in HIV Patients: Timing ART Initiation
items.content: ART initiation should be carefully timed relative to TB treatment to minimize the risk of IRIS, particularly in patients with different CD4+ T cell counts.
keyPoints: Delay ART for 2–8 weeks after starting TB treatment if CD4 count ≥50 cells/μL
Start ART immediately for CD4 counts <50 cells/μL due to higher risk of IRIS
IRIS reactions can occur as early as 1 week post-ART initiation
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.title: Preventing Active TB in HIV Patients
items.content: Aggressive screening and treatment for latent or active TB are essential to prevent progression to active disease, especially in patients with low CD4+ T cell counts.
keyPoints: Use PPD skin test or IFN-γ release assay for TB detection
Retest if CD4+ T cell count rises above 200 cells/μL after initially being <200 cells/μL
Annual testing recommended for those at continued risk of TB exposure
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.title: MAC Infection in HIV: Clinical Presentation and Management
items.content: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) is a common atypical mycobacterial infection in HIV patients, particularly those with CD4+ T cell counts <50/μL. It presents as disseminated disease with fever, weight loss, night sweats, and often mycobacteremia. Diagnosis involves culture of blood or tissue, with treatment including macrolides like clarithromycin combined with ethambutol.
keyPoints: MAC infection is prevalent in HIV patients with CD4+ T cell counts <50/μL.
Average CD4+ T cell count at diagnosis is 10/μL.
85% of MAC-infected patients are mycobacteremic.
Chest x-ray abnormalities occur in ~25%, often showing bilateral, lower-lobe infiltrates.
Therapy includes clarithromycin and ethambutol; add rifabutin, ciprofloxacin, or amikacin for extensive disease.
Continue therapy until clinical resolution, negative cultures, and CD4+ T cell counts >100/μL for 3–6 months with ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.title: Pulmonary Cryptococcal Disease in AIDS Patients
items.content: Cryptococcal disease is a significant fungal infection in AIDS patients, presenting with fever, cough, dyspnea, and often hemoptysis. Radiographically, >90% show interstitial infiltrates; other findings include lobar or cavitary disease. CNS involvement is common, occurring in 90% of cases.
keyPoints: Fever, cough, dyspnea are common symptoms.
>90% have a focal or diffuse interstitial infiltrate on chest x-ray.
CNS infection occurs concomitantly in 90% of patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.title: Histoplasmosis Reactivation in HIV Patients
items.content: Reactivation of histoplasmosis is common in HIV patients with disseminated disease, typically presenting with minimal respiratory symptoms. Chest x-ray abnormalities are seen in ~50% of cases, showing interstitial infiltrates or nodules. Urine often tests positive for Histoplasma antigen.
keyPoints: Respiratory symptoms occur in 10–30% of patients.
~50% have abnormal chest x-rays with diffuse interstitial infiltrate or small nodules.
Urine is often positive for Histoplasma antigen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.title: Idiopathic Interstitial Pneumonitis in HIV: LIP vs. NIP
items.content: Lymphoid interstitial pneumonitis (LIP) and nonspecific interstitial pneumonitis (NIP) are two forms of idiopathic interstitial pneumonia identified in patients with HIV infection. LIP is more common in children, seen in about 1% of untreated adult HIV patients, characterized by benign lung infiltrate linked to polyclonal lymphocyte activation due to HIV and EBV infections. NIP affects up to half of untreated HIV patients, presenting with fever and nonproductive cough. Both conditions are generally self-limited; LIP may require transbronchial or open-lung biopsy for diagnosis.
keyPoints: LIP is seen in ~1% of adult untreated HIV patients.
NIP can affect up to 50% of untreated HIV patients.
Transbronchial biopsy diagnostic in 50% LIP cases; open-lung biopsy needed otherwise.
No specific treatment for LIP or NIP, except ART management and brief glucocorticoids for severe LIP.
Chest x-ray usually normal or shows faint interstitial pattern.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.title: HIV-Associated Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension
items.content: Pulmonary arterial hypertension in HIV (HIV-PAH) occurs in ~0.5% of individuals with HIV, presenting with symptoms like shortness of breath and signs of right-sided heart failure. Diagnosis may show dilated pulmonary vessels on chest x-ray and right ventricular hypertrophy on ECG. ART does not significantly benefit prognosis, which is poor with a median survival of 2 years.
keyPoints: HIV-PAH occurs in ~0.5% of HIV patients.
Symptoms include shortness of breath, fatigue, syncope, chest pain.
Chest x-ray shows dilated pulmonary vessels; ECG reveals right ventricular hypertrophy.
ART does not clearly benefit prognosis; median survival is 2 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.title: HIV-Associated Cardiomyopathy and Heart Disease
items.content: Heart disease, including coronary heart disease and HIV-associated cardiomyopathy, is common in HIV patients. Coronary heart disease has a myocardial infarction rate of 3.5/1000 patient-years with 28% fatality. HIV-related immune activation and ART side effects contribute to cardiovascular risks. HIV-associated cardiomyopathy often presents as congestive heart failure and may be linked directly to HIV or other infections like toxoplasmosis.
keyPoints: Heart disease is common in HIV, found in 25–75% of autopsy cases.
Myocardial infarction rate: 3.5/1000 patient-years; 28% fatal.
CD4+ T cell count <500/μL is an independent cardiovascular risk factor.
HIV-associated cardiomyopathy presents with CHF symptoms and may involve direct HIV presence in cardiac tissue.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.title: Pericardial Effusions in Advanced HIV
items.content: Patients with advanced HIV may develop pericardial effusions, often due to TB, CHF, mycobacterial infection, cryptococcal infection, pulmonary infection, lymphoma, or KS. Pericarditis is rare but significant.
keyPoints: 5% of patients with HIV have moderate or severe pericardial effusions.
Tamponade and death can occur due to acute hemorrhage from pericardial KS.
Nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis should be considered in unexplained embolic phenomena.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.title: Oral Lesions as Indicators of HIV Progression
items.content: Thrush and oral hairy leukoplakia are common in untreated HIV, indicating advanced immunologic decline with CD4+ T cell counts <300/μL. These lesions suggest significant immune suppression.
keyPoints: 59% of patients with oral candidiasis develop AIDS within a year.
Thrush appears as white, cheesy exudate on erythematous mucosa.
Oral hairy leukoplakia is associated with EBV and not premalignant.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.title: Increased Risk of Enteric Infections in HIV
items.content: HIV-infected patients have a heightened risk for enteric infections like Salmonella, Shigella, and Campylobacter, with more severe presentations and higher relapse rates.
keyPoints: 20-fold increased risk of S. typhimurium infection in untreated HIV.
Shigella spp., particularly S. flexneri, can cause severe disease and bacteremia up to 50% in GI cases.
Long-term ciprofloxacin is recommended for Salmonella infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.title: Cryptosporidiosis in HIV: Presentation and Management
items.content: In patients with untreated HIV infection, particularly those with CD4+ T cell counts <300/μL, cryptosporidiosis presents as a significant cause of diarrhea. It can range from self-limited to severe, life-threatening diarrhea. The diagnosis is confirmed by stool examination or biopsy.
keyPoints: Incidence in untreated HIV patients with CD4+ <300/μL: ~1% per year
75% of cases present with crampy abdominal pain; 25% have nausea/vomiting
Noninflammatory diarrhea, oocysts stain with acid-fast dyes
Supportive therapy and ART improve outcomes
Nitazoxanide (2000 mg/d) may reduce symptoms or shedding in ~50% of patients
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.title: Microsporidia Infection in HIV: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Microsporidiosis, caused by Enterocytozoon bieneusi, presents similarly to cryptosporidiosis. Diagnosis is challenging due to the small size of organisms but can be confirmed with electron microscopy. ART improves outcomes; albendazole may benefit some patients.
keyPoints: Clinical manifestations: abdominal pain, malabsorption, diarrhea, cholangitis
Diagnosis via electron microscopy of stool or biopsy
Extraintestinal involvement possible (eye, brain, sinuses, muscle, liver)
ART is crucial for immune restoration
Albendazole (400 mg bid) may be beneficial
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.title: Isospora belli Infection in HIV: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Isospora belli causes diarrhea, similar to cryptosporidiosis, but is treatable with TMP-SMX. Relapses are common, necessitating a thrice-weekly regimen for prevention.
keyPoints: Cysts appear as large, acid-fast structures in stool
Diagnosis based on size, shape, and sporocyst count of cysts
Treatable with TMP-SMX; relapses are common
Thrice-weekly TMP-SMX regimen prevents recurrence
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.title: Management and Diagnosis of Chronic Diarrhea in HIV Patients
items.content: In patients with HIV, chronic diarrhea may be due to specific infections or AIDS enteropathy. Initial stool examinations should include culture, ova/parasites, and Clostridium difficile toxin tests. If these are negative, endoscopy with biopsy can identify microsporial or mycobacterial infection in ~30% of cases. A presumptive diagnosis of HIV enteropathy is made if diarrhea persists for >1 month without other findings.
keyPoints: Initial stool workup identifies pathogenic infections ~50% of the time.
Endoscopy with biopsy can diagnose microsporial or mycobacterial infection in ~30% of cases when initial tests are negative.
Presumptive diagnosis of HIV enteropathy if diarrhea persists >1 month without other findings.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.title: Hepatobiliary Complications in HIV Patients
items.content: Liver disease is a significant issue in HIV patients, contributing to ~15% of deaths. This is often due to co-infection with hepatitis B or C, but also includes hepatic steatosis and immune reconstitution-related injuries from ART.
keyPoints: ~15% of deaths in HIV patients are related to liver disease.
Liver complications include co-infections (hepatitis B/C) and ART-related issues like hepatic steatosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.title: Impact of HIV on Hepatitis Co-infections
items.content: HIV significantly influences the course and severity of hepatitis virus infections. It increases the prevalence of persistent HBsAg in co-infected individuals, alters liver disease presentation, and impacts mortality rates.
keyPoints: 90% of people with HIV in the U.S. have evidence of HBV infection; 6–14% have chronic HBV.
5–50% of patients are co-infected with HCV; among IV drug users, this ranges from 70 to 95%.
HIV is associated with a ~3-fold increase in persistent hepatitis B surface antigenemia.
Co-infection leads to decreased inflammatory liver disease markers due to HIV's immunosuppressive effects.
4- to 10-fold increases in liver-related mortality rates are noted in patients with both HIV and active HBV compared to those with either infection alone.
Slight increase in overall mortality rate for HBsAg-positive individuals with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.title: Treatment Considerations for Hepatitis B and C in HIV Co-infection
items.content: Effective management of hepatitis B and C in HIV co-infected patients requires integrated antiretroviral therapy to prevent drug resistance and manage both infections simultaneously.
keyPoints: IFN-α is less effective for HBV treatment in HIV co-infection; use lamivudine, emtricitabine, adefovir/tenofovir/entecavir, or telbivudine with ART.
HCV infection severity increases in HIV patients; HCV levels are ~10-fold higher than in HIV-negative individuals.
50% higher overall mortality and five-fold increased risk of death due to liver disease in co-infected patients.
Directly acting agents for HCV achieve cure rates near 100%, even with HIV co-infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.title: Preventive and Diagnostic Measures for Hepatitis in HIV Patients
items.content: Vaccination against hepatitis A and B is recommended for all HIV patients without prior infection. Diagnosis of liver involvement may include various infections, necessitating comprehensive evaluation.
keyPoints: Recommend immunization with hepatitis A and/or B vaccines for HIV patients lacking natural immunity.
Hepatitis G virus (GBV-C) co-infection in ~50% of HIV patients is associated with slower AIDS progression.
Granulomatous hepatitis, hepatic masses, and biliary tract disease may arise from opportunistic infections like MAC, TB, or fungal pathogens.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.title: Hepatic Injury from Antiretrovirals
items.content: Antiretroviral drugs, including nucleoside analogues and protease inhibitors like nevirapine, can cause liver injury. Nucleoside analogues inhibit DNA synthesis, potentially leading to mitochondrial toxicity, hepatic steatosis, lactic acidosis, and fulminant liver failure. Nevirapine is associated with fatal hepatitis and hepatic necrosis.
keyPoints: Nucleoside analogues can cause mitochondrial toxicity, leading to hepatic issues.
Hepatic injury from antiretrovirals is potentially reversible if diagnosed early.
Nevirapine may cause fatal fulminant and cholestatic hepatitis.
Indinavir and atazanavir can cause mild to moderate elevations in serum bilirubin (10–15% of patients).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.title: HIV-Associated Nephropathy (HIVAN) Characteristics
items.content: HIV-associated nephropathy is a direct complication of HIV infection, characterized by proteinuria and more prevalent in African-American or Hispanic individuals. It can occur even with CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL and was once the third leading cause of end-stage renal failure among African Americans aged 20–64 years.
keyPoints: Microalbuminuria is seen in ~20% of untreated HIV patients; significant proteinuria in ~2%.
HIVAN can be an early manifestation, prevalent in African-American or Hispanic individuals.
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis present in 80% of cases.
Proteinuria is the hallmark of HIVAN.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.title: Renal Injury from Antiretroviral Drugs
items.content: Certain antiretrovirals, including pentamidine and tenofovir, can cause renal damage. Switching from TDF to TAF may reduce renal injury. TMP-SMX and cobicistat can increase serum creatinine levels without affecting glomerular filtration rate.
keyPoints: Pentamidine, adefovir, cidofovir, tenofovir, foscarnet are associated with renal damage.
Switching from TDF to TAF may decrease renal injury.
TMP-SMX and cobicistat can increase serum creatinine levels without affecting GFR.
Adequate hydration is crucial for preventing renal calculi formation by indinavir or atazanavir.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.title: Neurosyphilis in HIV Patients
items.content: Patients with HIV infection have an increased risk of neurosyphilis, which can present in various forms including asymptomatic cases or acute meningitis. Due to immunologic abnormalities associated with HIV, standard serologic tests for syphilis may be unreliable.
keyPoints: Neurosyphilis rate may be as high as 1% in patients with HIV infection.
Consider lumbar puncture for neurosyphilis in all patients with HIV and secondary syphilis.
Standard serologic tests (VDRL) can yield false positives due to polyclonal B cell activation or delayed positivity.
Dark-field examination should be performed if syphilis is suspected, even with a negative VDRL.
Patients with positive serum VDRL test, neurologic findings, and abnormal spinal fluid should be treated for neurosyphilis regardless of CSF VDRL result.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.title: Vulvovaginal Candidiasis in HIV Patients
items.content: Women with HIV infection frequently experience vulvovaginal candidiasis, presenting symptoms include pruritus and discomfort. Diagnosis involves microscopic examination of vaginal discharge.
keyPoints: Symptoms: pruritus, discomfort, dyspareunia, dysuria.
Vulvar infection may present as a morbilliform rash extending to the thighs.
Vaginal infection typically presents with white discharge and erythematous plaques on vaginal wall.
Diagnosis by microscopic examination of discharge for pseudohyphal elements in 10% potassium hydroxide solution.
Mild disease treated with topical therapy; more serious cases treated with fluconazole.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.title: Understanding Lipodystrophy in HIV Patients
items.content: Lipodystrophy is a syndrome seen in patients with HIV receiving ART, characterized by fat redistribution and metabolic changes. It can be induced by thymidine analogues or protease inhibitors.
keyPoints: 33% to 75% of patients on thymidine analogues or protease inhibitors develop lipodystrophy.
Symptoms include truncal obesity, peripheral wasting, hypertriglyceridemia, hypercholesterolemia, and hyperinsulinemia.
20% meet criteria for metabolic syndrome per International Diabetes Federation or NCEP guidelines.
Avoid thymidine analogues like stavudine and zidovudine; consider statin therapy for those aged 40-75 unless cardiovascular risk <5%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.title: Managing Hyponatremia in Advanced HIV
items.content: Hyponatremia in advanced HIV can result from SIADH or adrenal insufficiency, with potential causes including medications like trimethoprim and pentamidine.
keyPoints: SIADH often accompanies pulmonary or CNS disease in HIV patients.
Adrenal insufficiency may present with low serum sodium and high serum potassium.
Hyperkalemia can be due to adrenal insufficiency, HIV nephropathy, or medications like trimethoprim.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.title: Thyroid Dysfunction in HIV Patients
items.content: HIV patients may experience thyroid dysfunction, including subclinical hypothyroidism and immune-reconstitution Graves' disease.
keyPoints: 10–15% of HIV patients have altered thyroid function, with subclinical hypothyroidism being predominant.
ART can lead to elevated TSH levels in up to 10%, indicating immune reconstitution effects.
Immune-reconstitution Graves' disease may occur 9-48 months after ART initiation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.title: Drug Allergies in HIV: Management and Considerations
items.content: Patients with HIV are prone to drug allergies, particularly as the disease progresses. TMP-SMX for PCP is a common culprit, affecting up to 65% of patients. While reactions often manifest as erythematous, morbilliform eruptions, they do not necessarily require discontinuation unless severe. Desensitization regimens can be effective, except in cases involving abacavir and the HLA-B5701 haplotype.
keyPoints: Drug allergies occur in up to 65% of patients receiving TMP-SMX for PCP.
Erythematous, morbilliform eruptions are common manifestations.
~33% of patients can continue therapy despite reactions.
Anaphylaxis is rare; cutaneous reactions do not preclude future use except with abacavir and HLA-B5701.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.title: Autoimmune Phenomena in HIV: Differential Considerations
items.content: HIV infection can mimic autoimmune diseases due to polyclonal B cell activation and increased antiphospholipid antibodies. However, systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis are not more common in HIV patients. A variant of primary Sjögren's syndrome, termed DILS, is noted for its distinct characteristics in HIV patients.
keyPoints: HIV shares similarities with autoimmune diseases due to polyclonal B cell activation.
Increased incidence of antiphospholipid antibodies like anticardiolipin and lupus-like anticoagulants.
Systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis are not more common in HIV patients.
DILS is characterized by parotid gland enlargement, dry eyes, and mouth; composed of CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.title: Reactive Arthritis in HIV: Incidence and Management
items.content: Approximately one-third of HIV patients experience arthralgias, with 5-10% diagnosed with reactive arthritis. These conditions are more frequent as immune function declines and respond well to standard treatments. Methotrexate should be used cautiously due to the risk of opportunistic infections.
keyPoints: One-third of HIV patients experience arthralgias; 5–10% have reactive arthritis.
Reactive arthritis includes Reiter's syndrome, psoriatic arthritis, and undifferentiated spondyloarthropathy.
Standard treatments are generally effective for reactive arthritis in HIV patients.
Methotrexate use should be cautious due to increased risk of opportunistic infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.title: Understanding HIV-Associated Arthropathy
items.content: HIV-associated arthropathy includes subacute oligoarticular arthritis and painful articular syndrome. The former develops over 1–6 weeks, lasts 6 weeks to 6 months, involves large joints like knees and ankles, and shows a mild inflammatory response with nonrevealing X-rays. Painful articular syndrome affects up to 10% of AIDS patients, presenting as acute severe joint pain lasting 2–24 hours.
keyPoints: Subacute oligoarticular arthritis: develops over 1-6 weeks, lasts 6 weeks to 6 months, involves knees and ankles, mild inflammatory response, nonrevealing X-rays
Painful articular syndrome affects up to 10% of AIDS patients, presents as acute severe joint pain lasting 2–24 hours
Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are marginally helpful; intraarticular glucocorticoids provide relief
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.title: Osteonecrosis in HIV Patients on ART
items.content: Patients with HIV treated with antiretroviral therapy (ART) show an increased incidence of osteonecrosis. A study found that 4.4% of asymptomatic patients had evidence of osteonecrosis on MRI, potentially linked to lipid-lowering agents, systemic glucocorticoids, testosterone, bodybuilding exercise, alcohol consumption, and anticardiolipin antibodies.
keyPoints: Increased incidence of osteonecrosis in HIV patients on ART
4.4% of asymptomatic patients had evidence of osteonecrosis on MRI
Associated with lipid-lowering agents, systemic glucocorticoids, testosterone, bodybuilding exercise, alcohol consumption, anticardiolipin antibodies
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.title: Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome (IRIS) in HIV
items.content: Following effective ART initiation, IRIS can cause a paradoxical worsening of pre-existing infections or new autoimmune conditions. It is seen in 10–30% of patients, especially those with CD4+ T cell counts <50 cells/μL and rapid HIV RNA level drops. Symptoms may appear 2 weeks to 2 years post-ART initiation and include lymphadenitis, fever, pulmonary infiltrates, hepatitis, increased intracranial pressure, uveitis, sarcoidosis, and Graves' disease.
keyPoints: IRIS seen in 10–30% of patients post-ART initiation
Common in patients with CD4+ T cell counts <50 cells/μL and rapid HIV RNA level drops
Symptoms: lymphadenitis, fever, pulmonary infiltrates, hepatitis, increased intracranial pressure, uveitis, sarcoidosis, Graves' disease
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.title: Lymphadenopathy in HIV: Clinical Significance and Management
items.content: Persistent generalized lymphadenopathy is an early clinical manifestation of HIV, characterized by enlarged lymph nodes (>1 cm) at two or more extrainguinal sites for over three months without an obvious cause. It results from follicular hyperplasia due to HIV infection. While not directly linked to AIDS progression, a decrease in lymph node size outside ART initiation may signal disease progression.
