keyPoints: Lymphadenopathy defined as enlarged nodes (>1 cm) at two or more sites for >3 months without obvious cause.
May occur at any stage of immune dysfunction; not directly linked to increased likelihood of developing AIDS.
Decrease in lymph node size outside ART may indicate disease progression.
Differential diagnosis includes TB, KS, Castleman's disease, and lymphoma with CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL.
Biopsy indicated for CD4+ <200/μL or if systemic illness signs (fever, weight loss) are present.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.title: Anemia in HIV: Causes and Management
items.content: Anemia is the most common hematologic abnormality in HIV patients, often mild but can be severe. It's associated with poor prognosis if no specific cause is identified. Reversible causes include drug toxicity, infections, nutritional deficiencies, and parvovirus B19 infection. Zidovudine may block erythroid maturation, leading to elevated MCV. Treatment options include addressing the underlying cause, erythropoietin for some cases, and IVIg for parvovirus B19-induced anemia.
keyPoints: Anemia is common in HIV and linked with poor prognosis if untreated.
Reversible causes: drug toxicity (e.g., zidovudine), infections, nutritional deficiencies, parvovirus B19.
Zidovudine can cause elevated MCV by blocking erythroid maturation.
Erythropoietin treatment may increase hemoglobin levels; effective in some cases of HIV-associated anemia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.title: Neutropenia in HIV: Implications and Treatment
items.content: Neutropenia occurs in about half of HIV patients, mostly mild but can be severe. It increases the risk for spontaneous bacterial infections and rare diseases like aspergillosis or mucormycosis. Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) and GM-CSF are effective in increasing neutrophil counts regardless of neutropenia cause.
keyPoints: Neutropenia seen in ~50% of HIV patients, can be mild to severe.
Increases risk for spontaneous bacterial infections and rare diseases like aspergillosis or mucormycosis.
G-CSF and GM-CSF effectively increase neutrophil counts.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.title: Thrombocytopenia in HIV: Incidence and Management
items.content: Thrombocytopenia is an early consequence of HIV infection, with incidence rates influenced by CD4+ T cell counts. It generally responds well to antiretroviral therapy (ART). In severe cases (platelet count <20,000/μL), a combination of IVIg or anti-Rh Ig and ART may be used.
keyPoints: Approximately 3% of patients with untreated HIV and CD4+ T cell counts ≥400/μL have thrombocytopenia.
Incidence increases to 10% for those with CD4+ T cell counts <400/μL.
Thrombocytopenia is more common in patients with hepatitis C co-infection, cirrhosis, and/or ongoing high-level HIV replication.
ART is the most effective treatment; severe cases may require IVIg or anti-Rh Ig plus ART.
Rituximab can be used for refractory cases.
Splenectomy is rarely needed and should follow vaccination against S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, and H. influenzae type b.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.title: Venous Thromboembolic Disease in HIV: Incidence and Risk Factors
items.content: HIV patients have a higher incidence of venous thromboembolic disease, approximately 1% per year. This is influenced by factors such as age >45, opportunistic infections, lower CD4 count, and estrogen use.
keyPoints: Incidence of venous thromboembolic disease in HIV patients is about 1% per year.
This rate is approximately 10 times higher than that seen in an age-matched population without HIV.
Risk factors include age >45, history of opportunistic infection, lower CD4 count, and estrogen use.
Abnormalities like decreased protein S activity, increased factor VIII, anticardiolipin antibodies, PAR-1 expression on T cells, or lupus-like anticoagulant are seen in over 50% of HIV patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.title: Seborrheic Dermatitis in HIV: Prevalence and Management
items.content: Seborrheic dermatitis is common among HIV patients, with prevalence increasing as CD4+ T cell counts decline. It may be aggravated by Pityrosporum infection.
keyPoints: Occurs in up to 50% of patients with HIV compared to 3% in the general population.
Prevalence and severity increase as CD4+ T cell count declines.
May be exacerbated by concomitant infection with Pityrosporum.
Topical antifungal agents are recommended for cases refractory to standard treatment.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.title: Folliculitis in HIV: Prevalence, Risk Factors, and Treatment
items.content: Folliculitis is a common dermatologic condition in patients with HIV, affecting approximately 20% of this population. It is more prevalent among those with CD4+ T cell counts below 200 cells/μL.
keyPoints: Prevalence: ~20% of HIV patients
Risk Factor: CD4+ T cell count <200 cells/μL
Eosinophilic pustular folliculitis may respond to topical anthelmintics
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.title: Herpes Zoster Reactivation in HIV: Indicators and Treatment
items.content: Reactivation of herpes zoster occurs in 10-20% of patients with HIV, indicating a modest decline in immune function. It may be the first sign of clinical immunodeficiency.
keyPoints: Prevalence: 10–20% of HIV patients
Indicators: Severe pain, extensive skin involvement, cranial or cervical dermatome involvement
AIDS development rate post-zoster: ~1% per month
Treatment: Valacyclovir, famciclovir, acyclovir; IV acyclovir for severe cases
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.title: Herpes Simplex Virus Reactivation in HIV: Clinical Features and Management
items.content: HIV patients experience recurrent herpes simplex virus infections, which become more frequent and severe as CD4+ T cell counts decline. These infections can significantly impact patient quality of life.
keyPoints: Clinical features: Beefy red, painful lesions; perirectal involvement may cause proctitis
Subclinical reactivation linked to increased plasma HIV RNA levels
Treatment: Valacyclovir, famciclovir, acyclovir
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.title: HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders (HAND) spectrum
items.content: HAND encompasses a range of cognitive impairments from asymptomatic to severe dementia, impacting many HIV-infected patients.
keyPoints: HAND ranges from Asymptomatic Neurocognitive Impairment (ANI) to Minor Neurocognitive Disorder (MND), and Clinically Severe Dementia (HIV-associated dementia or AIDS dementia complex).
Even with suppressive ART, ~50% of HIV patients show mild to moderate neurocognitive impairment.
HAND is an AIDS-defining illness when severe.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.title: CNS involvement and CSF findings in untreated HIV
items.content: The majority of untreated HIV patients exhibit abnormal CNS involvement, evidenced by specific CSF abnormalities.
keyPoints: ~90% of untreated HIV patients have abnormal CSF findings during the asymptomatic phase.
CSF pleocytosis occurs in 50–65% of these patients.
Viral RNA is detected in ~75%, elevated CSF protein in 35%, and anti-HIV antibodies are intrathecally synthesized in 90%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.title: Cryptococcal meningitis as a leading cause of fungal meningitis in AIDS
items.content: Cryptococcal meningitis is prevalent among patients with advanced HIV, particularly those with low CD4+ T cell counts.
keyPoints: Fungal meningitis is the leading infectious cause of meningitis in AIDS patients.
~2% of AIDS patients have cryptococcal meningitis as an initial AIDS-defining illness.
Occurs primarily in patients with CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL, and ~5% prevalence in untreated African patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Cryptococcal Meningitis in HIV
items.content: Cryptococcal meningitis is a significant opportunistic infection in patients with HIV, characterized by fever, nausea, vomiting, altered mental status, headache, and meningeal signs. Diagnosis involves identifying organisms in spinal fluid or detecting cryptococcal antigen. Initial treatment includes IV amphotericin B and flucytosine, followed by fluconazole.
keyPoints: Cryptococcal meningitis can present with fever, nausea, vomiting, altered mental status, headache, and meningeal signs.
CSF profile may show modest elevations in WBC or protein levels, decreases in glucose, and elevated opening pressure.
Diagnosis is made by identification of organisms in spinal fluid or detection of cryptococcal antigen.
Initial treatment: IV amphotericin B 0.7 mg/kg daily or liposomal amphotericin 3–4 mg/kg daily with flucytosine 25 mg/kg qid for at least 2 weeks.
Follow-up therapy includes fluconazole 800 mg/d PO for 8 weeks, then 200 mg/d until CD4+ T cell count >200 cells/μL for 6 months in response to ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.title: Understanding HIV-associated Dementia
items.content: HIV-associated dementia is a late complication of HIV infection, characterized by cognitive decline and motor/behavioral abnormalities. It can occur even when CD4+ T cell counts are >350 cells/μL. Diagnosis involves recognizing subcortical dementia features such as short-term memory defects and executive function issues.
keyPoints: HIV-associated dementia is a late complication, generally seen in patients with CD4+ T cell counts >350 cells/μL.
Characterized by cognitive decline, impaired concentration, forgetfulness, difficulty reading, and performing complex tasks.
Motor abnormalities include unsteady gait, poor balance, tremor, and difficulty with rapid alternating movements.
Behavioral changes may involve apathy, irritability, lack of initiative, agitation, or mild mania.
Clinically significant encephalopathy develops in ~25% of untreated patients with AIDS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.title: Pathophysiology and Histologic Features of HIV-associated Dementia
items.content: HIV-associated dementia results from direct effects of HIV on the CNS and immune activation. Histologically, changes are seen in subcortical areas, including pallor, gliosis, multinucleated giant cell encephalitis, and vacuolar myelopathy.
keyPoints: HIV-associated dementia is thought to result from direct effects of HIV on the CNS and associated immune activation.
Histologic changes are primarily in subcortical areas, including pallor, gliosis, multinucleated giant cell encephalitis, and vacuolar myelopathy.
80–90% of patients with HIV infection have histologic evidence of CNS involvement at autopsy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.title: Diagnosis and Monitoring of HIV-associated Dementia
items.content: HIV-associated dementia diagnosis relies on demonstrating cognitive decline, often assessed using the Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE). Baseline MMSE scores are recommended for all HIV patients. MRI may show cerebral atrophy or increased density in T2-weighted images, while lumbar puncture helps rule out opportunistic infections.
keyPoints: No specific criteria exist for diagnosing HIV-associated dementia; differentiation from other CNS diseases is necessary.
MMSE is used to objectively demonstrate cognitive decline; baseline scores are advisable.
MRI may show cerebral atrophy or increased density on T2-weighted images.
Lumbar puncture helps rule out opportunistic infections, showing nonspecific CSF findings in HIV encephalopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.title: Management of HIV-associated Dementia
items.content: Combination antiretroviral therapy improves neuropsychiatric test scores and cognitive function, indicating some reversible components. However, patients are sensitive to neuroleptic drugs' side effects, necessitating careful monitoring.
keyPoints: Antiretroviral therapy benefits patients with HIV-associated dementia by improving cognitive function.
Rapid improvement in cognition suggests a role for soluble mediators in pathogenesis.
Patients have increased sensitivity to neuroleptic drug side effects, requiring close monitoring.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.title: Seizures in HIV-infected Patients
items.content: Seizures may result from opportunistic infections, neoplasms, or HIV encephalopathy. They are common in cerebral toxoplasmosis and primary CNS lymphoma. Anticonvulsant therapy is indicated for patients with multiple seizures unless a correctable cause is identified.
keyPoints: Seizures occur in 15–40% of patients with cerebral toxoplasmosis, 15–35% with primary CNS lymphoma, and 7–50% with HIV encephalopathy.
HIV encephalopathy may present as seizures; anticonvulsant therapy is recommended for multiple seizures unless a correctable cause exists.
Drug-drug interactions between antiseizure medications and antiretrovirals necessitate careful monitoring of drug levels.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.title: Cerebral Toxoplasmosis: Incidence, Risk Factors, and Screening
items.content: Toxoplasmosis is a common secondary CNS infection in AIDS patients, especially prevalent among those from the Caribbean and France. It typically occurs when CD4+ T cell counts fall below 200/µL. Seroprevalence of T. gondii varies by region (50% in the Caribbean and France, 15% in the US). HIV-infected individuals should be screened for IgG antibodies to T. gondii during initial evaluation.
keyPoints: Toxoplasmosis is most common when CD4+ T cell counts <200/µL
Seroprevalence of T. gondii: ~50% in the Caribbean and France, ~15% in the US
Screen for IgG antibodies to T. gondii during initial HIV workup
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Cerebral Toxoplasmosis
items.content: Cerebral toxoplasmosis typically presents with fever, headache, focal neurologic deficits, or altered mental status. MRI findings often show multiple lesions with ring enhancement; however, brain biopsy is definitive but reserved for those not responding to empiric therapy. Treatment includes sulfadiazine and pyrimethamine with leucovorin for 4-6 weeks.
keyPoints: Common presentation: fever, headache, focal neurologic deficits
MRI findings: multiple lesions with ring enhancement
Empiric treatment failure warrants brain biopsy
Standard treatment: sulfadiazine and pyrimethamine with leucovorin for 4-6 weeks
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.title: Prophylaxis Strategies for Toxoplasmosis in HIV Patients
items.content: Primary prophylaxis is recommended for patients with CD4+ T cell counts <100/µL and IgG antibody to Toxoplasma. TMP-SMX, used for Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis, also protects against toxoplasmosis. Secondary prophylaxis can be discontinued if ART is effective and CD4+ T cell counts rise above 200/µL for 6 months.
keyPoints: Primary prophylaxis indicated for CD4+ <100/µL with IgG to Toxoplasma
TMP-SMX provides dual protection against P. jirovecii and toxoplasmosis
Discontinue secondary prophylaxis if CD4+ >200/µL for 6 months on effective ART
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.title: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML) in AIDS
items.content: PML is a late manifestation of AIDS caused by JC virus, seen in ~1–4% of patients. It involves demyelination primarily in subcortical white matter and can affect multiple brain regions. Diagnosis is supported by MRI findings and JC virus DNA levels in CSF (sensitivity 76%, specificity ~100%). ART improves survival but only ~50% show neurologic improvement.
keyPoints: PML occurs in ~1–4% of AIDS patients
JC virus DNA in CSF: sensitivity 76%, specificity ~100%
ART increases median survival to 2 years, with some surviving >15 years
~50% show neurologic improvement with ART
Prognosis better with CD4+ T cell count >100/μL and HIV viral load <500 copies/mL
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.title: Reactivation of American Trypanosomiasis (Chagas' Disease) as an AIDS-defining Condition
items.content: In patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200 cells/μL, reactivation of Chagas' disease can present as acute meningoencephalitis. Diagnosis is supported by the presence of antibodies to T. cruzi and radiographic findings. Treatment involves benzimidazole or nifurtimox for at least 60 days, with maintenance therapy during immunodeficiency.
keyPoints: Occurs in patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200 cells/μL
Diagnosis supported by antibodies to T. cruzi and radiographic findings
Treatment: benzimidazole (2.5 mg/kg bid) or nifurtimox (2 mg/kg qid) for ≥60 days
Maintenance therapy during immunodeficiency
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.title: Stroke Risk and Presentation in HIV-infected Individuals
items.content: HIV infection increases stroke risk, especially in women aged 18–29. Stroke symptoms are sudden onset, differing from other neurologic deficits in HIV. Classic stroke risk factors like smoking and diabetes are prevalent. Secondary causes include vasculitis and septic embolism.
keyPoints: Increased stroke risk in HIV, particularly in women aged 18–29
Symptoms of stroke are sudden onset
Classic stroke risk factors: smoking, diabetes
Secondary causes: vasculitis, septic embolism
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.title: Understanding Spinal Cord Disease in AIDS
items.content: Spinal cord disease, or myelopathy, is prevalent in approximately 20% of patients with AIDS and often coexists with HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder. Three primary types are identified: vacuolar myelopathy, pure sensory ataxia, and a sensory syndrome presenting as paresthesias and dysesthesias.
keyPoints: Spinal cord disease occurs in ~20% of AIDS patients.
90% of HIV-associated myelopathy patients have evidence of dementia.
Vacuolar myelopathy presents with gait disturbances, ataxia, spasticity, and may progress to bladder/bowel dysfunction.
Pure sensory ataxia affects the dorsal columns; paresthesias/dysesthesias affect lower extremities.
Spinal cord syndromes do not respond well to antiretroviral drugs.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.title: CMV Myelopathy and Polyradiculopathy in Advanced HIV
items.content: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection can lead to a fulminant myelopathy and polyradiculopathy, typically seen late in HIV progression. It presents with rapid onset of lower extremity paresthesias, walking difficulty, areflexia, ascending sensory loss, and urinary retention.
keyPoints: CMV myelopathy is fulminant and rapidly progressive over weeks.
CSF shows predominantly neutrophilic pleocytosis; CMV DNA detectable by PCR.
Intravenous ganciclovir or foscarnet can lead to rapid improvement.
Prompt initiation of therapy minimizes permanent neurologic damage.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.title: Peripheral Neuropathies in HIV: Types and Management
items.content: Peripheral neuropathies are common across all stages of HIV infection. They include acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP), mononeuritis multiplex, and distal sensory polyneuropathy (DSPN).
keyPoints: Peripheral neuropathies occur at all stages of HIV.
Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy resembles Guillain-Barré syndrome.
CIDP presents with progressive weakness, areflexia, and minimal sensory changes; CSF shows mononuclear pleocytosis.
DSPN is common in AIDS, often due to HIV or ART side effects like dideoxynucleosides.
Two-thirds of AIDS patients show peripheral nerve disease via electrophysiologic studies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.title: HIV-associated Myopathy: Recognizing Severity and Causes
items.content: Myopathy in HIV patients can range from asymptomatic to severe, characterized by proximal muscle weakness and myalgias. It may be caused by the infection itself or medications like zidovudine.
keyPoints: Creatine kinase levels may rise significantly even without symptoms, especially post-exercise.
Zidovudine-induced myopathy is dose-dependent and reversible upon discontinuation; red ragged fibers are a histologic hallmark.
Pathological findings in severe cases include myofiber necrosis with inflammatory cells and mitochondrial abnormalities.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.title: Ophthalmologic Complications in Advanced HIV: Monitoring and Diagnosis
items.content: Approximately 50% of patients with advanced HIV experience ophthalmologic issues, with cotton-wool spots being common. CMV retinitis is a severe consequence requiring regular monitoring for high-risk patients.
keyPoints: Cotton-wool spots are indicative of retinal ischemia and appear in ~50% of advanced HIV cases.
CMV retinitis risk increases with CD4+ T cell count <100/μL, particularly <50/μL; ART has reduced incidence from 25–30% to near 2%.
Regular ophthalmologic exams every 3-6 months are advised for patients at high risk of CMV retinitis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.title: Management and Complications of CMV Retinitis in HIV
items.content: CMV retinitis presents as progressive vision loss, often bilateral. Treatment includes antiviral therapy, with specific regimens for induction and maintenance.
keyPoints: Characteristic symptoms include painless vision loss, blurred vision, floaters, and scintillations.
Therapy options are oral valganciclovir, IV ganciclovir, or foscarnet; combination therapy is slightly more effective for relapsed cases.
Maintenance therapy continues until CD4+ T cell count >100 μL for over 6 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.title: Ocular Manifestations of HIV
items.content: Patients with HIV may experience ocular problems due to secondary infections. Pneumocystis jirovecii can cause choroidal lesions, while toxoplasmosis and syphilis are associated with chorioretinitis and uveitis respectively.
keyPoints: P. jirovecii causes bilateral choroidal lesions, appearing as slightly elevated yellow-white plaques, typically asymptomatic.
Chorioretinitis due to toxoplasmosis may occur alone or with CNS involvement.
Syphilis can lead to uveitis highly associated with neurosyphilis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.title: Bartonella Infections in Advanced HIV
items.content: In patients with advanced HIV, Bartonella infections are linked to severe cell-mediated immunity defects and present as bacillary angiomatosis, cat-scratch disease, or trench fever.
keyPoints: Commonly seen when CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL.
Bacillary angiomatosis is painful, vascular proliferation due to B. henselae, often confused with KS lesions.
Diagnosis involves biopsy with Warthin-Starry stain or PCR; treatment includes doxycycline or erythromycin for at least 3 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.title: Histoplasmosis as an Opportunistic Infection
items.content: Histoplasmosis is a late manifestation of HIV, often presenting as disseminated disease. It's endemic in specific geographic areas and may be the initial AIDS-defining condition.
keyPoints: Occurs in ~0.5% of AIDS cases in the U.S., more common in Mississippi and Ohio River valleys.
Median CD4+ T cell count for patients with histoplasmosis is 33/μL.
Disseminated disease presents with fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy; CNS involvement in 15% of cases.
Diagnosis via silver staining or culturing; treatment includes liposomal amphotericin B followed by itraconazole.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.title: Talaromyces marneffei infection as an AIDS-defining condition
items.content: In regions like southeast Asia, Talaromyces marneffei is recognized as a complication of HIV and is considered an AIDS-defining illness. It ranks third in prevalence behind TB and cryptococcosis in Thailand. The clinical presentation includes fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and skin lesions resembling Molluscum contagiosum.
keyPoints: Talaromyces marneffei is the third most common AIDS-defining illness in Thailand.
More frequently diagnosed during the rainy season.
Treatment involves amphotericin B followed by itraconazole until CD4+ T cell count >100 cells/μL for at least 6 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.title: Visceral leishmaniasis co-infection in HIV patients
items.content: Visceral leishmaniasis is increasingly recognized among HIV-infected individuals, particularly those from or traveling to endemic regions. It presents with hepatosplenomegaly, fever, and hematologic abnormalities. A chronic relapsing course occurs in two-thirds of co-infected patients.
keyPoints: Clinical presentation includes hepatosplenomegaly, fever, and hematologic abnormalities.
Chronic, relapsing course seen in two-thirds of co-infected patients.
Standard therapy involves amphotericin B or pentavalent antimony compounds; however, eradication is difficult with common relapses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.title: Impact of ART on neoplastic diseases in HIV
items.content: ART has significantly reduced the incidence of Kaposi's sarcoma, a prominent AIDS-defining condition. However, non-AIDS-defining malignancies now account for more morbidity and mortality among HIV patients.
keyPoints: Introduction of ART led to a marked reduction in KS incidence.
Non-AIDS-defining malignancies account for ~10% of deaths in HIV patients.
ART has had little effect on HPV-associated malignancies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.title: Presentation and Prognosis of Kaposi Sarcoma (KS) in HIV
items.content: Kaposi Sarcoma can present at any stage of HIV infection, even with normal CD4+ T cell counts. Lesions may appear as small nodules or discolorations on the skin, mucosa, or lymph nodes, often in sun-exposed areas or sites of trauma. Lymph node involvement early in KS is not indicative of poor prognosis and can occur in patients with relatively intact immune function.
keyPoints: KS lesions range from a few millimeters to several centimeters.
Lesions may appear as raised macules, papules, or confluent masses.
Lymph node involvement early in KS does not imply poor prognosis.
Pulmonary KS presents with shortness of breath and bilateral lower lobe infiltrates on chest x-ray.
Pleural effusions are seen in 70% of pulmonary KS cases.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.title: Diagnostic and Differential Considerations for Kaposi Sarcoma
items.content: Diagnosis of KS is confirmed by biopsy showing spindle cells, endothelial cells, extravasated red blood cells, and hemosiderin-laden macrophages. Differentials include lymphoma, bacillary angiomatosis, and cutaneous mycobacterial infections.
keyPoints: Biopsy reveals proliferation of spindle and endothelial cells.
Extravasation of red blood cells is a histological feature.
Differential diagnosis includes lymphoma, bacillary angiomatosis, and cutaneous mycobacterial infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.title: Management Strategies for Kaposi Sarcoma
items.content: Effective ART can control KS, sometimes leading to spontaneous regression. Treatment is indicated for lesions causing discomfort or cosmetic issues, using localized radiation, intralesional vinblastine, topical 9-cis-retinoic acid, or cryotherapy. Avoid treatments that further suppress the immune system due to high risk of secondary infections.
keyPoints: ART can lead to spontaneous regression of KS lesions.
Localized treatment options include radiation, intralesional vinblastine, and cryotherapy.
Fewer than 10% of AIDS patients with KS die from malignancy; death is more common from secondary infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.title: Role of CD4+ T Cell Count in KS Treatment Response
items.content: The response to Kaposi's Sarcoma (KS) treatment, particularly IFN-$\alpha$, is significantly influenced by the patient's baseline CD4+ T cell count. Higher counts correlate with better outcomes.
keyPoints: IFN-α response rate ~80% for CD4+ T cell counts >600/$\mu$L
<10% response rate for CD4+ T cell counts <150/$\mu$L
CD4+ T cell count is a critical determinant of treatment efficacy
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.title: Incidence and Risk Factors for Lymphoma in AIDS Patients
items.content: AIDS patients have a significantly increased risk of developing lymphomas, with incidence rates influenced by CD4+ T cell counts and duration of HIV infection.
keyPoints: 6% of AIDS patients develop lymphoma during their illness
10- to 20-fold increase in incidence compared to the general population
Lymphoma risk increases exponentially with lower CD4+ T cell counts (<200/$\mu$L) and longer HIV duration (2.6% per year at 8 years post-infection)
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.title: Types of Lymphoma in HIV Patients
items.content: HIV patients predominantly develop certain types of lymphomas, often associated with viral infections like EBV and KSHV.
keyPoints: Three main categories: immunoblastic lymphoma (60%), Burkitt's lymphoma, primary CNS lymphoma
90% are B cell phenotype; more than half contain EBV DNA
Primary effusion lymphoma linked to KSHV as the driving force behind oncogenesis
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.title: Burkitt's Lymphoma and HIV: Incidence and Characteristics
items.content: Burkitt's lymphoma is significantly more prevalent in patients with HIV, occurring over 1000 times more frequently than in the general population. It often involves c-myc translocations from chromosome 8 to chromosomes 14 or 22.
keyPoints: Accounts for ~20% of lymphomas in AIDS patients
Incidence >1000-fold higher in HIV infection compared to general population
Characteristic c-myc translocations involve chromosome 8 to 14 or 22
50% are EBV-positive, unlike African Burkitt's lymphoma (97%)
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.title: Primary CNS Lymphoma: Presentation and Prognosis
items.content: Primary CNS lymphomas are common in HIV patients, often presenting at a later stage of infection. They typically occur when CD4+ T cell counts are around 50/μL.
keyPoints: Accounts for ~20% of lymphomas in HIV patients
Usually EBV-positive (100% incidence in one study)
Median CD4+ T cell count at diagnosis is ~50/μL
Prognosis is poorer due to later stage presentation
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.title: Systemic Lymphoma: Diagnosis and Treatment in HIV
items.content: Systemic lymphoma occurs earlier in the course of HIV infection, often when CD4+ T cell counts are around 226/μL. It can involve multiple organ systems.
keyPoints: Seen at earlier stages of HIV (mean CD4+ count ~226/μL)
Commonly involves lymph nodes, GI tract (~25%), bone marrow (~20%), liver and lung (~10% each)
Treatment with combination chemotherapy has improved outcomes
Higher CD4+ counts correlate with better survival
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.title: Increased Incidence and Management of Multicentric Castleman's Disease in HIV
items.content: Multicentric Castleman's disease (MCD) is more common in patients with HIV, especially under ART. It shares features with lymphoma but isn't a true malignancy.
keyPoints: Increased frequency of MCD in HIV patients on ART.
75–82% of cases present with Kaposi's sarcoma (KS).
Median survival pre-ART was 14 months; now over 90% survive more than 2 years with ART.
Treatment involves chemotherapy, but Rituximab may worsen KS and corticosteroids increase KS risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.title: HPV Infection and Cancer Risk in HIV Patients
items.content: HIV patients have a higher incidence of HPV-related dysplasia, leading to increased risk for cervical and anal cancers.
keyPoints: HPV infection is twice as common in people with HIV compared to the general population.
Abnormal Pap smears are 20–80% in men with HIV; 30–60% in women with HIV.
Invasive cervical cancer is an AIDS-defining condition.
Relative risk of HPV-related cancers is 10- to 100-fold higher in HIV patients compared to non-HIV individuals, decreased with ART.
2-year survival rate for invasive cervical cancer: 64% in HIV-positive women vs. 79% in those without HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.title: HPV Vaccination and Screening Recommendations for HIV Patients
items.content: Despite concerns about vaccine effectiveness, HPV vaccination is recommended for HIV patients due to their higher risk of HPV-related cancers.
keyPoints: High rates of dysplasia necessitate comprehensive gynecologic and rectal exams at initial evaluation and 6 months later.
Yearly follow-ups if initial and repeat Pap smears are negative; retest in 3 months if severe inflammation is present.
Colposcopic examination with biopsies for squamous intraepithelial lesions.
HPV genotypes like 58 and 53 are more common in HIV patients, raising concerns about vaccine effectiveness.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Epidemiology of ICL
items.content: Idiopathic CD4+ lymphocytopenia (ICL) is defined by an absolute CD4+ T cell count <300/μL or <20% of total T cells on two occasions at least 6 weeks apart, with no evidence of HIV-1, HIV-2, HTLV-1, or HTLV-2. It was first recognized in 1992 and is very rare, identified through studies of blood donors and cohorts of HIV-seronegative men who have sex with men.
keyPoints: ICL defined by CD4+ T cell count <300/μL or <20% on two occasions at least 6 weeks apart
No evidence of HIV-1, HIV-2, HTLV-1, or HTLV-2
First recognized in 1992 and is very rare
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Autoimmune Associations of ICL
items.content: Patients with ICL may develop opportunistic diseases such as cryptococcosis (24%), nontuberculous mycobacterial infections (5%), and HPV-associated disease (29%). Approximately 10–30% exhibit autoimmune diseases, more common in those with higher CD4 counts. Unlike HIV/AIDS, ICL is demographically diverse and does not show progressive deterioration.
keyPoints: Opportunistic diseases: cryptococcosis (24%), nontuberculous mycobacterial infections (5%), HPV-associated disease (29%)
10–30% exhibit autoimmune diseases, more common with higher CD4 counts
Demographically diverse and does not show progressive deterioration
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.title: Management and Prognosis of ICL
items.content: No specific therapy is initiated for ICL if no underlying cause is detected. Opportunistic diseases should be treated appropriately, and prophylaxis may be given based on CD4+ T cell count levels. Approximately 15% experience spontaneous reversal of CD4+ T lymphocyteopenia.
keyPoints: No specific therapy if no underlying cause
Treat opportunistic diseases as they occur
Prophylaxis for opportunistic infections based on CD4+ T cell count
Approximately 15% experience spontaneous reversal of CD4+ T lymphocyteopenia
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.title: Routine HIV Testing and Pretest Counseling
items.content: The CDC recommends routine HIV testing as part of standard medical care, with patients given the option to 'opt out.' This approach aims to identify undiagnosed individuals, as 13% of the 1.2 million people in the U.S. with HIV are unaware of their status. Pretest counseling is crucial for preparing patients emotionally for a potential positive diagnosis.
keyPoints: CDC guidelines recommend routine HIV testing
13% of 1.2 million individuals with HIV in the U.S. are undiagnosed
Pretest counseling helps prepare patients emotionally
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.title: Post-Diagnosis Support and ART Initiation
items.content: Upon diagnosing HIV, immediate activation of support systems is essential. This includes emotional support and initiating antiretroviral therapy (ART) to improve patient outcomes. Community HIV support centers can be instrumental in providing necessary assistance.
keyPoints: Immediate support system activation post-diagnosis
Initiation of ART is crucial for treatment
Community HIV support centers provide valuable resources
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.title: Comprehensive Baseline Evaluation Post-HIV Diagnosis
items.content: After diagnosing HIV, a comprehensive set of examinations and laboratory studies should be conducted to assess disease extent and establish baselines. This includes CD4+ T cell count, plasma HIV RNA level, resistance testing, and screenings for other infections like hepatitis.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell count and plasma HIV RNA level are essential
HIV resistance test and serologies for hepatitis A, B, C should be performed
Baseline examinations include chest x-ray, urinalysis, and Pap smear
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.title: Immunization Strategies for HIV Patients
items.content: HIV patients should receive specific vaccinations to prevent opportunistic infections and other diseases. This includes pneumococcal polysaccharide, annual influenza, COVID-19 shots, and vaccines for HPV, hepatitis A, and B if seronegative.
keyPoints: Immunizations: pneumococcal polysaccharide, influenza (annual), COVID-19
Additional vaccines if seronegative: HPV, hepatitis A, hepatitis B
Assess hepatitis C status
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.title: Counseling and Management Updates in HIV Care
items.content: HIV patients should receive counseling on sexual practices and needle sharing. They should also be informed about notifying others who may be at risk. Medical management strategies must be regularly updated based on the latest information.
keyPoints: Counseling: sexual practices, needle sharing
Inform and counsel potential contacts of infection risk
Develop short- and long-term medical management plans
Stay updated with rapidly changing HIV medicine through reliable online resources
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Initiation Timing for Antiretroviral Therapy
items.content: ART should be initiated as soon as possible after an HIV diagnosis, except in cases of cryptococcal meningitis or TB where delaying ART for several weeks may reduce severe IRIS risk.
keyPoints: ART initiation is crucial immediately following HIV diagnosis to improve patient outcomes and reduce transmission risks.
Delay ART by several weeks in cases of cryptococcal meningitis or TB to decrease the risk of severe IRIS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Regimen Selection and Adherence
items.content: Selecting an effective ART regimen involves choosing initial drugs, knowing when to change them, and ensuring strict adherence for HIV replication suppression.
keyPoints: Strict adherence to prescribed regimens is essential for adequate HIV replication suppression.
Coformulations and longer dosing intervals (e.g., once-daily or monthly) improve adherence.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.title: Drug Categories and Resistance Testing
items.content: ART drugs fall into five categories, with a typical regimen including two nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors plus another agent. Drug resistance testing is crucial before therapy initiation or changes.
keyPoints: Available ART drugs include those inhibiting reverse transcriptase, protease, integrase, viral entry, and capsid.
A common regimen includes tenofovir-based drugs with 3TC/FTC plus another agent like a nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor or protease inhibitor.
Perform drug resistance testing before starting therapy or changing due to treatment failure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Combination Therapy is Essential for HIV Treatment
items.content: Antiretroviral therapy for HIV should always be administered as part of combination regimens to prevent rapid development of drug resistance. Monotherapy with reverse transcriptase inhibitors or protease inhibitors is not recommended.
keyPoints: None of the FDA-approved reverse transcriptase inhibitors (e.g., zidovudine, lamivudine) should be used alone due to risk of drug resistance.
Protease inhibitors like ritonavir and darunavir are also part of combination regimens for efficacy and safety.
Preferred initial regimen includes integrase strand transfer inhibitors with reverse transcriptase inhibitors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Monitoring and Adjustments in Antiretroviral Therapy
items.content: When treating HIV patients coinfected with hepatitis B, ensure additional antiretroviral medications are used if lamivudine, emtricitabine, or tenofovir is prescribed. Discontinuation requires vigilance for hepatitis B flare-ups.
keyPoints: Lamivudine, emtricitabine, and tenofovir require additional antiretroviral medication when treating hepatitis B in HIV patients.
Monitor for hepatitis B flare upon discontinuation of these medications.
Tenofovir disoproxil is limited to patients with creatinine clearance >70; tenofovir alafenamide for CrCl >30.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.title: Preferred Antiretroviral Regimens and Considerations
items.content: The DHHS Panel recommends specific antiretroviral drugs for combination therapy based on efficacy and side-effect profiles. Ritonavir-boosted darunavir with emtricitabine and tenofovir is preferred.
keyPoints: Preferred nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors: lamivudine, emtricitabine, abacavir, tenofovir disoproxil, tenofovir alafenamide.
Preferred nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors: efavirenz, rilpivirine (for ART-naïve patients with HIV RNA <10,000 copies/mL), doravirine.
Ritonavir-boosted darunavir with emtricitabine and tenofovir is the preferred protease inhibitor strategy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.title: Use of Maraviroc in HIV Therapy
items.content: Maraviroc, a CCR5 antagonist, is effective only against CCR5-tropic viruses. Therefore, a co-receptor tropism assay should be performed before its use to ensure the virus's susceptibility.
keyPoints: Maraviroc targets CCR5-tropic HIV strains.
A co-receptor tropism assay is necessary prior to using maraviroc.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.title: Lenacapavir for Multidrug-Resistant HIV
items.content: Lenacapavir, a capsid inhibitor, is used in individuals with multidrug-resistant HIV who are heavily treatment-experienced and not suppressed on current regimens. It requires administration every 6 months after an initial loading regimen.
keyPoints: Indicated for heavily treatment-experienced patients with multidrug-resistant HIV.
Administered subcutaneously every 6 months following a loading regimen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.title: Initiation and Continuity of ART in HIV Treatment
items.content: ART should be initiated as soon as possible after HIV diagnosis, with life-long adherence to prevent rapid increases in viral load and declines in CD4+ counts. Intermittent therapy has been linked to serious adverse events.
keyPoints: ART initiation is recommended immediately post-diagnosis unless contraindicated.
Life-long adherence to ART is crucial for preventing disease progression.
Intermittent ART regimens are associated with increased risk of serious adverse events.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Viral Genotyping at Initiation of ART
items.content: Viral genotyping is recommended at the time of initiation of antiretroviral therapy to ensure that appropriate drugs are selected, especially given the potential for pre-existing drug resistance mutations.
keyPoints: HIV can rapidly develop resistance to single agents; hence, multidrug combinations are necessary.
Viral genotyping helps in selecting effective initial regimen by identifying any existing drug resistance mutations.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.title: Goals and Expectations for Initial ART
items.content: The primary goal of initial antiretroviral therapy is maximal suppression of viral replication, which reduces the likelihood of drug-resistant quasispecies development.
keyPoints: Maximal suppression of viral replication should be achieved to prevent resistance.
Expect a rapid 1-log reduction in plasma HIV RNA levels within 1-2 months and <50 copies/mL within 6 months.
CD4+ T cell count should rise by 100–150 cells/μL during the same period.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.title: Indications for Changing Antiretroviral Therapy
items.content: Antiretroviral therapy should be changed if there is less than a 1-log drop in plasma HIV RNA by 4 weeks, significant increase in viral load not due to other factors, persistently declining CD4+ T cell numbers, or clinical deterioration.
keyPoints: Change ART if <1-log reduction in plasma HIV RNA within 4 weeks of initiation.
Consider change with a threefold or greater increase from nadir viral load, excluding intercurrent infections or vaccinations.
Persistently declining CD4+ T cells and clinical deterioration are also indications for changing therapy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.title: Indications for Changing HIV Therapy
items.content: Therapy change considerations include failure to achieve expected CD4+ T cell count increases, persistently declining CD4+ counts, rising HIV RNA levels (>200 copies/mL), clinical deterioration, or drug toxicity.
keyPoints: CD4+ T cell count should increase by 50–100 cells/year after initial therapy.
Change therapy if CD4+ T cell count does not increase as expected, declines persistently, or HIV RNA levels rise to >200 copies/mL.
Consider drug toxicity and ensure patient adherence before changing regimen.
Resistance testing is crucial for confirming therapy choices in high-risk settings.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.title: Monitoring and Adjusting Antiretroviral Therapy
items.content: Plasma HIV RNA levels should be monitored within 2–4 weeks after ART initiation or regimen change, every 4–8 weeks until suppressed to <200 copies/mL, then every 3–6 months.
keyPoints: Monitor plasma HIV RNA levels at specified intervals: 2-4 weeks post-initiation/change, 4-8 weeks until suppression, and 3-6 months during therapy.
Use resistance testing to guide regimen changes in virologic failure.
Next-generation sequencing can detect resistant quasispecies down to 1% frequency.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.title: Emerging Therapies and Strategies for HIV
items.content: Experimental agents and immune-based therapies are being explored, including new antiviral drugs and strategies to eliminate the reservoir of infected cells.
keyPoints: Experimental agents include nucleoside analogues, protease inhibitors, fusion inhibitors, receptor antagonists, integrase inhibitors, long-acting injectables, antisense nucleic acids, and maturation inhibitors.

Immune-based therapies under evaluation: monoclonal antibodies, bone marrow transplantation, adoptive transfer of modified lymphocytes, active immunotherapy with inactivated HIV or its components, IL-7, and IL-15.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.title: Risk of Occupational HIV Transmission to Health Care Workers
items.content: Health care workers face a small but definite risk of HIV infection due to occupational exposures, primarily through percutaneous injuries. The risk is quantified at approximately 0.23% for percutaneous exposure and 0.09% for mucous membrane exposure without postexposure prophylaxis (PEP).
keyPoints: 58 documented seroconversions in U.S. health care workers since the start of case investigations.
85% of infections from occupational exposures are due to percutaneous injuries.
Risk of HIV infection following percutaneous exposure without PEP is ~0.23%; mucous membrane exposure risk is ~0.09%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.title: Prevention and Management of Needle-Stick Injuries
items.content: Most health care worker seroconversions result from needle-stick injuries, often due to improper disposal or handling. Adhering to standard guidelines for sharp object management can significantly reduce these incidents.
keyPoints: 27% of needle-stick injuries are due to improper disposal (over half from recapping needles).
23% occur during IV line attempts; 22% during blood drawing.
Immediate cleansing and antiseptic application are crucial after an exposure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.title: Postexposure Prophylaxis (PEP) for HIV
items.content: Occupational exposures to HIV should be treated as medical emergencies, with PEP initiated promptly. U.S. guidelines recommend a three-drug regimen for 4 weeks, considering factors like injury depth and source patient's disease stage.
keyPoints: PEP initiation delay beyond 72 hours may prevent infection prevention.
Risk factors include deep injury, visible blood on the instrument, and advanced HIV in the source patient.
U.S. guidelines recommend a three-drug PEP regimen for 4 weeks.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.title: Prevention of Occupational HIV Infection in Health Care Workers
items.content: Health care workers should follow CDC guidelines to minimize the risk of occupational HIV infection. This includes adhering to universal precautions, using barriers like gloves and goggles during potential contact with blood or body fluids, washing hands immediately after such contact, and safely handling and disposing of sharp instruments.
keyPoints: Follow CDC guidelines from June 2015 for minimizing occupational HIV risk.
Assume all patient blood and body fluids are potentially infectious.
Use barriers (gloves/goggles) when anticipating contact with blood/body fluids.
Wash hands immediately after contact with blood/body fluids.
Carefully handle and dispose of sharp instruments.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.title: Prevention and Management of HBV and TB in Health Care Workers
items.content: Health care workers should be immunized with the HBV vaccine due to the higher risk of HBV infection from needle-stick injuries compared to HIV. Additionally, they should monitor their PPD status annually and receive isoniazid treatment if necessary. Patients suspected of having active pulmonary TB should be placed in respiratory isolation until diagnostic results are available.
keyPoints: Immunize all health care workers dealing with HIV patients against HBV due to higher infection risk from needle-stick injuries.
Know and check PPD status yearly; receive 6 months of isoniazid treatment if skin test converts positive.
Place suspected active pulmonary TB patients in respiratory isolation pending diagnostic results.
Be aware of the increased risk of drug-resistant TB, especially for health care workers with pre-existing HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.title: Routine HIV Testing and Its Impact on Prevention
items.content: Routine HIV testing is recommended for individuals aged 13 to 64 as part of standard medical care, without requiring written consent. This approach aims to identify those unaware of their HIV status, who contribute significantly to new infections.
keyPoints: CDC recommends routine HIV testing for ages 13-64 at least once.
~13% of ~1.2 million persons in the U.S. with HIV are unaware of their status.
Routine testing helps identify and manage undiagnosed cases, reducing transmission.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.title: Effectiveness of ART in Preventing HIV Transmission
items.content: Antiretroviral therapy (ART) significantly reduces the risk of HIV transmission when viral load is suppressed. Studies show no transmissions occurred with a suppressed viral load, supporting the concept 'undetectable equals untransmittable' (U=U).
keyPoints: HPTN 052 trial showed a 96% reduction in transmission risk with immediate ART initiation.
No HIV transmissions when partner's viral load <400 copies/mL.
Studies included >500 heterosexual and >1100 male couples, demonstrating U=U.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.title: Condom Use and Its Limitations in HIV Prevention
items.content: While condoms significantly reduce HIV transmission risk, they are not foolproof. The ~10% failure rate is mainly due to breakage or improper use. Latex condoms are preferred over natural skin ones.
keyPoints: Condoms have a ~10% failure rate for contraception.
Most failures result from breakage or improper usage.
Latex condoms are preferable; avoid petroleum-based lubricants.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.title: Effectiveness of PrEP Regimens in HIV Prevention
items.content: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) with antiretroviral medications is highly effective for preventing HIV acquisition among at-risk populations. High adherence to a daily regimen of emtricitabine + tenofovir disoproxil fumarate can prevent HIV acquisition by 99%. Cabotegravir injections every two months and lenacapavir injections every six months have shown similar or greater efficacy in preventing HIV infection.
keyPoints: PrEP with emtricitabine + tenofovir disoproxil fumarate is 99% effective if adherence is strict.
Cabotegravir injections every two months show similar efficacy to daily PrEP.
Lenacapavir injections every six months demonstrated greater efficacy in cisgender women.
CDC estimates approximately 1.2 million people in the U.S. are at substantial risk for HIV and should be counseled about PrEP.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.title: Strategies to Prevent HIV Transmission Among Injection Drug Users
items.content: The most effective way to prevent HIV transmission among injection drug users is cessation of injectable drug use. For those unable or unwilling to stop, avoiding needle sharing and using virucidal solutions like sodium hypochlorite for cleaning paraphernalia are crucial steps. Needle exchange programs have proven successful in reducing HIV transmission without increasing drug use.
keyPoints: Stopping injectable drug use is the most effective prevention method.
Avoiding needle sharing and cleaning with virucidal solutions (e.g., sodium hypochlorite) reduces transmission risk.
Needle exchange programs decrease HIV transmission among IDUs without increasing drug usage.
Oral PrEP is also effective in preventing HIV acquisition among IDUs.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.title: Why is there no effective HIV vaccine?
items.content: The absence of an approved safe and effective HIV vaccine is due to the unique challenges posed by the virus. Unlike other diseases where natural infection leads to clearance and lifelong immunity, HIV persists in the body despite a natural immune response. Additionally, superinfection with different strains can occur.
keyPoints: No safe and effective HIV vaccine approved for prevention
Natural immune response fails to clear HIV from the body
Superinfections with HIV are not rare
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.title: Challenges in developing an HIV vaccine
items.content: Developing a preventive HIV vaccine is particularly challenging due to the virus's high mutability. This characteristic makes it difficult for a vaccine to effectively mimic the natural immune response and provide protection against diverse strains.
keyPoints: High mutability of HIV complicates vaccine development
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.question: Which CD4+ T lymphocyte count category is used to classify advanced HIV disease (AIDS) in individuals over 6 years of age?
items.explanation: Advanced HIV disease (AIDS) is classified as stage C with a CD4+ T lymphocyte count below 200 cells/μL in individuals over 6 years of age.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.question: Which family does HIV belong to?
items.explanation: HIV belongs to the family of human retroviruses, Retroviridae.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.question: Which subfamily within Retroviridae does HIV belong to?
items.explanation: HIV belongs to the subfamily of lentiviruses within Retroviridae.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.question: Which virus is primarily responsible for the global AIDS pandemic?
items.explanation: The AIDS pandemic is primarily caused by HIV-1 M group viruses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.question: Which proteins form the trimeric heterodimer that constitutes the external spikes of the HIV virion?
items.explanation: The external spikes of the HIV virion are formed by the trimeric heterodimer consisting of gp120 and gp41.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.question: What is the role of the CD4 molecule in the replication cycle of HIV?
options: It acts as a receptor for gp120 binding.
It integrates proviral DNA into host chromosomes.
items.explanation: The CD4 molecule is the primary receptor on the host cell surface to which HIV's gp120 protein binds.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.question: Which cellular proteins are major co-receptors for HIV-1 entry into the host cell?
items.explanation: The major co-receptors for HIV-1 entry into the host cell are CCR5 and CXCR4.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.question: What is the function of Vif in the context of HIV infection?
options: It integrates proviral DNA into host chromosomes.
It facilitates fusion with the host cell membrane.
It binds to the CD4 molecule.
items.explanation: The viral protein Vif targets APOBEC3 for proteasomal degradation, protecting HIV from hypermutation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.question: Which viral protein counteracts the effect of tetherin to facilitate the release of budding HIV particles?
items.explanation: The HIV accessory protein vpu interacts with tetherin to counteract its effect and facilitate virion release.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.question: What is the role of the viral protease in the HIV replication cycle?
options: It catalyzes the cleavage of gag-pol precursor proteins.
It integrates viral DNA into the host genome.
It transcribes viral RNA from the provirus.
items.explanation: The HIV protease enzyme cleaves the gag-pol precursor to produce mature virion components during or soon after budding.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a gene product encoded by the HIV-1 genome?
items.explanation: The vpx gene is not found in HIV-1 but is present in HIV-2.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary cause of HIV sequence diversity?
options: The limited fidelity of reverse transcriptase
High mutation rate in host DNA
items.explanation: HIV sequence diversity arises directly from the limited fidelity of the reverse transcriptase, leading to copying errors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.question: Which region of the HIV genome is most variable due to immune selective pressure?
items.explanation: The envelope protein, exposed on the virion surface and under immune selective pressure, is extremely variable.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.question: Which group of HIV-1 is responsible for most infections worldwide?
items.explanation: Group M (major) is responsible for most infections in the world.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.question: What mechanism contributes to the generation of circulating recombinant forms (CRFs) of HIV?
options: Infection with two subtypes leading to recombination
items.explanation: Inter-subtype recombinants are generated by infection of an individual with two subtypes that then recombine.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.question: Which factor contributes to the challenge in developing a broadly useful HIV vaccine?
options: The presence of multiple subtypes and circulating recombinant forms.
The stability of viral strains over time.
The uniformity of transmission routes for HIV.
The limited diversity of antiretroviral drugs.
items.explanation: HIV's molecular heterogeneity, including multiple subtypes and recombinant forms, complicates vaccine development.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a primary route for HIV transmission?
items.explanation: HIV is not transmitted by airborne particles; it is primarily transmitted through sexual contact, blood, and from infected mothers to infants.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.question: What has been observed about the transmission of HIV over four decades?
options: HIV can be transmitted through casual contact.
New modes of transmission have emerged.
There is no evidence for other transmission modalities besides those known.
HIV transmission rates are decreasing significantly.
items.explanation: Over four decades, there has been no evidence supporting HIV transmission through any modality other than the established ones.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: Which mode of HIV transmission is most common worldwide?
items.explanation: HIV infection is predominantly a sexually transmitted infection, with heterosexual transmission being the most common mode worldwide.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: What is the estimated per-act risk of HIV transmission during unprotected receptive anal intercourse?
items.explanation: The per-act risk for HIV transmission via unprotected receptive anal intercourse has been estimated to be ~1.4%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.question: Which factor is NOT associated with an increased efficiency of heterosexual HIV transmission?
options: Effective antiretroviral therapy in the infected partner
items.explanation: The risk of HIV transmission approaches zero when the infected partner is on effective antiretroviral therapy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
