items.question: What is a reason for the higher risk of HIV acquisition during unprotected receptive anal intercourse compared to vaginal intercourse?
options: Lower viral concentration in seminal fluid
items.explanation: The risk is higher because only a thin, fragile rectal mucosal membrane separates the deposited semen from susceptible cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: What is the plasma level of HIV RNA below which transmission is rare even when genital ulcer disease is present?
items.explanation: Transmission was rare when the infected partner had a plasma level of <1700 copies/mL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: What is the viral load threshold below which there is essentially no chance of sexual transmission of HIV?
items.explanation: If the viral load is reduced to <20 copies/mL, there is essentially no chance of sexual transmission.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: Which intervention has been shown to reduce the risk of HIV acquisition in heterosexual men?
items.explanation: Multiple studies have indicated that male circumcision is associated with a lower risk of acquisition of HIV infection for heterosexual men.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.question: Which factor increases the susceptibility of uncircumcised men to ulcerative STIs and potentially HIV?
items.explanation: The highly vascularized inner layer of foreskin tissue contains a high density of Langerhans cells, increasing susceptibility to ulcerative STIs.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.question: Which of the following injection drug use behaviors can transmit HIV?
items.explanation: HIV can be transmitted through subcutaneous ('skin popping') and intramuscular ('muscling') injections, not just IV puncture.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.question: What is the estimated per-act risk of HIV transmission from injection drug use with a contaminated needle?
items.explanation: The per-act risk of HIV transmission from injection drug use with a contaminated needle is approximately 0.6%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.question: Which blood product has not been associated with the transmission of HIV infection?
items.explanation: Hyperimmune gamma globulin has not been associated with the transmission of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.question: What measure is NOT used to reduce the risk of HIV transmission by transfused blood in developed countries?
options: Screening for antibodies to HIV-1 and HIV-2
Health history questionnaires for potential donors
Universal screening of all donated food products
items.explanation: Universal screening of all donated food products is not a measure used to reduce the risk of HIV transmission by transfused blood.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.question: What is the estimated risk of HIV transmission following a percutaneous injury from a needle contaminated with blood from an individual with documented HIV infection?
items.explanation: The risk of HIV transmission following skin puncture is approximately 0.23%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.question: Which type of exposure has a higher risk of HIV transmission to health care workers?
items.explanation: The risk for mucous membrane exposure (~0.09%) is higher than nonintact skin exposure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.question: What fluids are considered potentially infectious for occupational transmission of HIV?
items.explanation: Cerebrospinal fluid, synovial fluid, and other similar fluids are considered potentially infectious.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.question: What is the effectiveness of postexposure prophylaxis with hepatitis B immune globulin and HBV vaccine in preventing HBV infection?
items.explanation: Postexposure prophylaxis for HBV is more than 90% effective.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.question: Which of the following is a rare occurrence that can result in the transmission of HIV among health care workers?
items.explanation: Reuse of contaminated syringes is a rare occurrence that can result in the transmission of HIV among health care workers.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.question: What is the most common time for maternal transmission of HIV to occur?
items.explanation: Maternal transmission of HIV occurs most commonly during birth.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.question: What is the probability range of mother-to-child transmission of HIV in developing countries without antiretroviral therapy?
items.explanation: The probability of mother-to-child transmission of HIV in developing countries without antiretroviral therapy ranges from 25% to 35%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.question: Which factor is best documented to increase the risk of mother-to-child transmission of HIV?
options: High maternal levels of plasma viremia
Low maternal CD4+ T cell counts
Prolonged interval between membrane rupture and delivery
items.explanation: The presence of high maternal levels of plasma viremia is the best documented factor to increase the risk of mother-to-child transmission of HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.question: Which factor found in saliva is associated with a decreased risk of HIV transmission through breast milk?
items.explanation: Higher salivary levels of SLPI in breast-fed infants were associated with decreased risk of HIV transmission through breast milk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.question: Which body fluid is mentioned as having no convincing evidence for transmitting HIV?
items.explanation: There is no convincing evidence that saliva can transmit HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.question: What is suggested as a mechanism by which submandibular saliva reduces HIV infectivity?
options: Stripping gp120 from the surface of virions
items.explanation: Submandibular saliva is suggested to reduce HIV infectivity by stripping gp120 from the surface of virions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.question: As of 2023, what percentage of people living with HIV/AIDS reside in low- and middle-income countries?
items.explanation: An estimated 95% of people living with HIV/AIDS reside in low- and middle-income countries.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.question: What is the global prevalence of HIV infection among persons aged 15–49 years as of 2023?
items.explanation: The overall global prevalence of HIV infection among persons 15–49 years of age is ~0.7%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.question: Which region has the highest adult HIV prevalence globally?
items.explanation: Eswatini, in the Eastern and Southern Africa region, has the highest adult HIV prevalence globally (25.1%).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.question: What was the estimated number of new HIV infections worldwide in 2023?
items.explanation: An estimated 1.3 million new cases of HIV infection occurred worldwide in 2023.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.question: What percentage of people living with HIV in Eastern and Southern Africa knew their infection status as of 2023?
items.explanation: As of 2023, 93% of people living with HIV in the region knew their infection status.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.question: What was the percentage decrease in AIDS-related deaths in Eastern and Southern Africa from 2010 to 2023?
items.explanation: AIDS-related deaths decreased by 57% in that period.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.question: What was the HIV prevalence among adults across Western and Central Africa as of 2023?
items.explanation: HIV prevalence among adults in this region stands at 1.2%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.question: Which country reported the highest number of new HIV infections in Asia and the Pacific in 2023?
items.explanation: While specific numbers are not given, Thailand is noted for having an adult seroprevalence rate that reaches 1%, indicating significant HIV presence.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.question: Which group accounted for almost half of new HIV infections in the Caribbean region in 2023?
options: People from key populations and their sex partners
Men who have sex with men
items.explanation: People from key populations and their sex partners accounted for almost half of new infections in the Caribbean region in 2023.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.question: What percentage of people living with HIV in the United States are Black/African American?
items.explanation: Nearly 40% of people living with HIV in the United States are Black/African American.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.question: By how much did the estimated annual number of new HIV infections in the United States fall from its peak in the late 1980s?
items.explanation: The estimated annual number of new HIV infections has fallen by more than two-thirds since its height in the late 1980s.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.question: What was a significant factor contributing to the decline in HIV-related deaths in the United States?
options: Improved prophylaxis and treatment of opportunistic infections
items.explanation: The most influential factor contributing to the decline in HIV-related deaths was the increased use—and continued refinement—of antiretroviral therapy (ART).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which molecule serves as the primary cellular receptor for HIV on helper T cells?
items.explanation: The CD4 molecule is present on the surface of helper T cells and serves as the primary receptor for HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which co-receptors are primarily used by HIV-1 for fusion and entry into target cells?
items.explanation: HIV-1 uses the co-receptors CCR5 and CXCR4 for fusion and entry into target cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What is a major mechanism responsible for CD4+ T cell depletion in HIV infection?
options: Direct viral destruction of B cells
items.explanation: One of the mechanisms for CD4+ T cell depletion is immune clearance of infected cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.question: Which cells are primarily responsible for the initial amplification of HIV infection?
items.explanation: Both partially resting and activated CD4+ T cells serve as early amplifiers of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.question: What is the term used for the period when HIV is not detected in plasma after initial exposure?
items.explanation: The period when HIV is typically not detected in the plasma following initial infection is called the 'eclipse' phase.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.question: What must occur for HIV infection to become established?
options: The basic reproductive rate (R0) must be less than 1.
The virus must infect only CD8+ T cells.
The basic reproductive rate (R0) must be equal to or greater than 1.
The virus must remain in the plasma.
items.explanation: For HIV infection to become established, the basic reproductive rate (R0) must become equal to or greater than 1.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.question: Which type of HIV strains are typically transmitted during sexual transmission?
items.explanation: Transmitting viruses, often referred to as 'founder viruses,' are almost exclusively R5 strains.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the typical duration of acute viremia during primary HIV infection?
items.explanation: Acute viremia can last for several weeks during primary HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What correlates well with the presence of high levels of plasma viremia during primary HIV infection?
items.explanation: Acute mononucleosis-like symptoms are well correlated with high levels of plasma viremia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the median duration of chronic infection before a patient becomes clinically ill?
items.explanation: The median duration of chronic HIV infection before clinical illness is ~10 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the hallmark of HIV disease?
options: Establishment of a chronic, persistent infection
items.explanation: The establishment of a chronic, persistent infection is the hallmark of HIV disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: Which mechanism is paramount for HIV's evasion of immune control?
options: Establishment of a sustained level of replication with viral diversity via mutation and recombination
Immediate production of neutralizing antibodies by the humoral immune system
Downregulation of HLA class I molecules on uninfected cells
Sequestration in immunologically privileged sites without any mutations
items.explanation: HIV evades immune control primarily through sustained replication and viral diversity via mutation and recombination.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What is a consequence of the high rate of HIV virus replication?
options: Inability of antibodies to neutralize and/or clear the autologous virus
Immediate production of classic neutralizing antibodies
Complete control of viral replication by CD8+ CTLs
items.explanation: The high rate of virus replication leads to mutations that prevent antibody neutralization of the autologous virus.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What is a feature of HIV-specific CD8+ T cells during prolonged immune activation?
options: Upregulation of inhibitory receptors leading to immune exhaustion
Enhanced ability to recognize and kill infected target cells
items.explanation: Prolonged immune activation leads to upregulation of inhibitory receptors in HIV-specific CD8+ T cells, causing exhaustion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: Which viral proteins contribute to the downregulation of HLA class I molecules on infected cells?
items.explanation: The viral proteins Nef, Tat, and Vpu downregulate HLA class I molecules on infected cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: What is a significant consequence of HIV preferentially infecting activated CD4+ T cells?
options: Enhanced viral-specific helper T cell responses
Loss of viral-specific helper T cell responses
Reduction in the size of the viral reservoir
items.explanation: HIV infection leads to a loss of viral-specific helper T cell responses, negatively impacting immunologic control.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: During chronic HIV viremia, what evidence suggests CD4+ T cells are exhausted?
options: Decrease in memory CD4+ T cells
Elimination of all latently infected CD4+ T cells
items.explanation: Exhaustion is reflected by the upregulation of CTLA-4, a member of the B7-CD28 family.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: What characterizes the viral reservoir in HIV infection?
options: It consists only of replication-competent virus
It is eliminated by ART and CTLs
It includes latently infected CD4+ T cells with integrated HIV DNA
It is composed solely of preintegration latency cells
items.explanation: The viral reservoir comprises latently infected CD4+ T cells carrying integrated HIV DNA.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: Why is the 'shock and kill' strategy for eliminating HIV from the latent reservoir considered unsuccessful?
options: It increases viral replication beyond control
It fails to activate resting CD4+ T cells
It does not eliminate the persistent reservoir of infected cells
It causes systemic activation without reinitiating viral expression
items.explanation: 'Shock and kill' has been unsuccessful in eliminating the persistent reservoir of HIV-infected cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.question: What percentage of circulating HIV-1 virus originates from recently infected, rapidly turning over CD4+ T cells?
items.explanation: 93–99% of the circulating virus originated from recently infected, rapidly turning over CD4+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.question: What is the half-life of a productively infected cell in HIV infection?
items.explanation: The half-life of a productively infected cell was determined to be ~1 day.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.question: What is the main site of HIV replication and the primary source of plasma viremia?
items.explanation: Lymphoid tissue is confirmed as the main site of HIV replication and the main source of plasma viremia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.question: What is the critical CD4+ T cell count level below which a patient with untreated HIV becomes highly susceptible to opportunistic diseases?
items.explanation: A CD4+ T cell count below <200/μL is considered critical for susceptibility to opportunistic diseases.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.question: What was the median survival time from presentation with a life-threatening opportunistic infection prior to the availability of antiretroviral therapy?
items.explanation: Prior to ART, median survival was 26 weeks from presentation with a life-threatening opportunistic infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.question: Which HLA class I haplotypes are overrepresented in elite controllers of HIV?
items.explanation: Elite controllers often have the HLA-B*57:01 and HLA-B*27:05 haplotypes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.question: What percentage of people with HIV were estimated to remain free of clinical AIDS for more than 20 years prior to effective ART?
items.explanation: Approximately ~13% of homosexual/bisexual men infected at an early age were estimated to remain free of clinical AIDS for more than 20 years before effective ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Where does the majority of HIV replication occur?
items.explanation: Virus replication occurs mainly in lymphoid tissue, not in the blood.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What is a common feature of lymphadenopathy in HIV infection?
items.explanation: Lymphadenopathy reflects cellular activation and immune response, characterized by follicular or germinal center hyperplasia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Where does the earliest burst of virus replication occur during acute HIV infection?
items.explanation: The earliest burst of virus replication occurs in the GALT during acute HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What role do follicular dendritic cells (FDCs) play in HIV pathogenesis?
options: They trap extracellular virions on their surfaces
They eliminate infected CD4+ T cells
items.explanation: FDCs trap extracellular virions, serving as a reservoir and activator of CD4+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which cytokine is decreased and contributes to the disruption of lymphoid tissue architecture in advanced HIV disease?
items.explanation: Decreased levels of IL-7 are associated with the disruption of lymphoid tissue architecture in HIV disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: What is a consequence of advanced HIV disease on lymph node structure?
items.explanation: In advanced stages, HIV leads to complete disruption of lymphoid architecture, resulting in 'burnt out' lymph nodes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: What factor is increased and contributes to fibrosis in the context of HIV pathogenesis?
items.explanation: Increased levels of transforming growth factor (TGF) β contribute to fibrosis in HIV pathogenesis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Which cells does HIV replicate most efficiently in?
items.explanation: HIV replicates most efficiently in activated CD4+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What is a proposed mechanism of persistent immune activation in HIV patients?
options: Disruption of the mucosal barrier leading to microbial translocation
items.explanation: Persistent immune activation is proposed to occur due to disruption of the mucosal barrier, resulting in microbial translocation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Which condition has been shown to increase HIV viral load when co-infected with HIV?
items.explanation: Tuberculosis increases HIV viral load in dually infected individuals.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What effect does deworming have on HIV replication?
options: Has no effect on plasma viremia
items.explanation: Deworming has been shown to decrease plasma viremia in some studies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a marker associated with immune activation in HIV infection?
items.explanation: TNF-alpha is not specifically mentioned as an elevated marker of immune activation in the passage.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: What role does LAG-3 play in advanced HIV disease?
options: It inhibits the proliferation of regulatory T cells.
It reduces immune checkpoint receptor expression.
items.explanation: LAG-3 is overexpressed on exhausted T cells and shown to inhibit the proliferation of T-regs.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which form of cell death involves the release of proinflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18?
items.explanation: Pyroptosis is described as an inflammatory form of cell death involving the release of IL-1β and IL-18.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which viral gene product is associated with antiapoptotic properties in HIV?
items.explanation: Nef has been shown to possess antiapoptotic properties.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following medical conditions are associated with chronic immune activation and inflammation in HIV disease, even after years of ART-induced viral suppression?
items.explanation: Bone fragility is associated with chronic immune activation and inflammation in HIV disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: What phenomenon is characterized by a paradoxical deterioration of clinical condition after initiating ART, particularly in individuals with low CD4+ T cell counts?
items.explanation: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) is characterized by a paradoxical deterioration of clinical condition after initiating ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: Which type of antibodies in HIV disease may cross-react with host proteins, contributing to autoimmune phenomena?
options: Antibodies against the HIV envelope protein gp41
items.explanation: Antibodies against the HIV envelope protein gp41 may cross-react with host proteins.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which cytokine is one of the first induced during primary HIV infection and plays a significant role in activating immune responses?
items.explanation: IFN-α is one of the first cytokines induced during primary HIV infection and plays a significant role in activating immune responses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which cytokine's plasma levels are predictive of disease progression in HIV?
items.explanation: Plasma levels of IP-10 are predictive of disease progression in HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which cytokines inhibit infection by R5 strains of HIV-1?
items.explanation: RANTES (CCL5), MIP-1α (CCL3), and MIP-1β (CCL4) inhibit infection by R5 strains of HIV-1.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which cytokine is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease in people with HIV, even when receiving ART?
items.explanation: IL-6 is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease in people with HIV, even when receiving ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.question: Which lymphocyte types exhibit increased turnover in patients with HIV infection?
options: CD4+, CD8+ T cells, and B lymphocytes
Neither CD4+ nor CD8+ T cells
items.explanation: Increased turnover is observed in CD4+, CD8+ T cells, and B lymphocytes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.question: What effect does effective antiretroviral therapy (ART) have on lymphocyte turnover in HIV patients?
options: It has no impact on lymphocyte turnover.
It only affects CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: Effective ART immediately reduces lymphocyte turnover in HIV patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.question: What is the relationship between lymphocyte turnover and plasma viremia in HIV infection?
options: Lymphocyte turnover only affects CD8+ T cells.
items.explanation: A tight correlation exists between the degree of lymphocyte turnover and plasma viremia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which chemokine is the natural ligand for CXCR4 and can block entry of X4 viruses?
items.explanation: SDF-1 is the natural ligand for CXCR4 and blocks entry of X4 viruses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What type of virus predominates during the early stages of HIV disease?
items.explanation: The transmitting virus is almost invariably an R5 virus that predominates during the early stages of HIV disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: What role does the integrin $\alpha4\beta7$ play in HIV infection?
options: It is essential for binding and infecting CD4+ T cells.
It facilitates efficient cell-to-cell spread of HIV at mucosal surfaces.
It blocks entry of R5 viruses.
It has no role in the transmission or pathogenesis of HIV.
items.explanation: The integrin $\alpha4\beta7$ plays an important role in the transmission of HIV at mucosal surfaces and contributes to the pathogenesis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.question: Which viral envelope region interacts with co-receptors CCR5 or CXCR4?
items.explanation: The third variable region (V3 loop) of gp120 interacts with the co-receptors CCR5 or CXCR4.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.question: Which cells are the principal targets of HIV and can be productively infected?
options: CD4+ T lymphocytes and to a lesser extent CD4+ cells of the myeloid lineage
items.explanation: CD4+ T lymphocytes and to a lesser extent CD4+ cells of the myeloid lineage are the principal targets of HIV that can be productively infected.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.question: What factor limits HIV replication in myeloid (mDC) and plasmacytoid (pDC) dendritic cells?
options: High expression of the restriction factor SAMHD1
items.explanation: The high expression of the restriction factor SAMHD1 in myeloid (mDC) and plasmacytoid (pDC) dendritic cells limits HIV replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.question: Which cells are more likely to bind and transfer virus to activated CD4+ T cells rather than be productively infected themselves?
items.explanation: Langerhans cells are more likely to bind and transfer virus to activated CD4+ T cells than be productively infected themselves.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which CD4+ T cell subset is primarily involved in host defense against extracellular pathogens in the intestinal mucosa and helps maintain gut epithelium integrity?
items.explanation: T_H17 cells are important for host defense against extracellular pathogens in the intestinal mucosa and help maintain gut epithelium integrity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.question: In HIV infection, which CD4+ T cell subset is increased in lymph nodes of viremic individuals?
items.explanation: The number of TF_H cells is increased in lymph nodes of people with HIV, especially those who are viremic.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which cytokine is most important for TF_H cells to provide help to antigen-specific B cells?
items.explanation: TF_H cells provide help to antigen-specific B cells through secretion of cytokines, the most important being IL-21.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.question: Which CD4+ T cell subset is depleted early in HIV disease due to direct and indirect effects of viral replication?
items.explanation: T_H17 cells are depleted early in HIV disease due to direct and indirect effects of viral replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.question: Which viral protein binds with high affinity to CD4 molecules on uninfected T cells and monocytes, contributing to immunodeficiency in HIV?
items.explanation: Soluble viral proteins, particularly gp120, bind with high affinity to CD4 molecules on uninfected T cells and monocytes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.question: What is a major predictor of the restoration of CD4+ T cell counts upon initiation of therapy in HIV patients?
options: The level of gp120 binding to chemokine receptors
The degree and duration of decline of CD4+ T cells before therapy
The amount of IL-7 produced during lymphopenia
items.explanation: The degree and duration of decline of CD4+ T cells at the time of initiation of therapy is an important predictor of their restoration.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.question: Which signaling pathways are associated with immunopathogenic processes due to gp120 binding in HIV?
options: T cell receptor-phosphoinositide pathway and p56ck activation
MAP kinase and ras signaling pathways only
items.explanation: Intracellular signals transduced by gp120 include dysregulation of the T cell receptor-phosphoinositide pathway, p56ck activation, and others.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.question: Which phenotype of CD8+ T cells following seroconversion is associated with stabilization of CD4+ T cell counts in people with HIV?
items.explanation: Individuals with an HLA-DR+/CD38- phenotype of CD8+ T cells following seroconversion have stabilization of their CD4+ T cell counts.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of elite controllers in HIV disease?
options: Reduced proliferative capacity of HIV-specific CD8+ T cells
Increased expression of inhibitory receptors like PD-1 and LAG-3 on CD8+ T cells
High proliferative capacity of HIV-specific CD8+ T cells with increased perforin expression
Skewed expansion of a less differentiated CD8+ T cell subset
items.explanation: Elite controllers maintain high proliferative capacity and increased perforin expression in their HIV-specific CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.question: What is associated with the emergence of HIV escape mutants in people not receiving ART?
options: Functional exhaustion of CD8+ T cells
Increased expression of IL-2 receptor on CD8+ T cells
items.explanation: The emergence of HIV escape mutants is associated with the functional exhaustion of CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.question: Which markers are expressed on CD8+ T cells that assume an abnormal phenotype due to chronic immune activation in HIV disease?
items.explanation: CD8+ T cells with an abnormal phenotype express HLA-DR and CD38 due to chronic immune activation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.question: What is the predominant defect in B cells from people with HIV?
options: Increased propensity to terminal differentiation and immunoglobulin secretion
Enhanced ability to undergo cell signaling ex vivo
Reduced incidence of EBV-related B cell lymphomas
items.explanation: The predominant defect in B cells from people with HIV is aberrant cellular activation, leading to increased propensity to terminal differentiation and immunoglobulin secretion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.question: Which receptor do B cells from patients with high levels of HIV viremia use to bind virions?
items.explanation: B cells from patients with high levels of viremia bind virions via the CD21 complement receptor.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.question: What is a consequence of increased B cell turnover in people with HIV?
options: Increased incidence of EBV-related B cell lymphomas
items.explanation: There is an increased incidence of EBV-related B cell lymphomas in people with HIV due to defective T cell immune surveillance and increased B cell turnover.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.question: What is a characteristic of the memory B cells from people with HIV?
options: Attrition and replacement by aberrant subpopulations
Enhanced capacity to respond to further stimulation
items.explanation: Memory B cells from people with HIV undergo attrition and are replaced by aberrant memory and differentiated B cell subpopulations.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.question: Which biomarker remains elevated in individuals with suppressed plasma viremia due to ART, indicating residual immune activation?
items.explanation: Levels of sCD14 can remain elevated even when plasma viremia is suppressed by ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.question: What type of monocytes expand in the peripheral blood, especially in individuals with HIV who are viremic?
items.explanation: Expansion of intermediate and nonclassical CD16+ monocytes is observed in viremic individuals.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.question: Which cells are an important source of HIV during the inflammatory response associated with opportunistic infections?
items.explanation: Tissue macrophages serve as persistent reservoirs and are an important source of HIV during inflammatory responses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.question: Which cells are not productively infected with HIV due to expression of host restriction factors like APOBEC3G and SAMHD1?
items.explanation: Dendritic cells are not productively infected with HIV due to expression of host restriction factors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.question: Which subset of NK cells is associated with an increased expression of inhibitory NK cell receptors and decreased expression of natural cytotoxicity receptors in HIV infection?
items.explanation: The CD56-/CD16+ NK cell subset is associated with increased expression of inhibitory NK cell receptors and decreased expression of natural cytotoxicity receptors in HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.question: What is the impact of polymorphisms in iNKR and NCR alleles on HIV-1 disease outcomes?
options: "They are linked to HIV-1 disease outcomes"
"They enhance NK cell cytotoxicity against HIV-infected cells"
"They prevent the downregulation of HLA-A and B molecules"
items.explanation: Polymorphisms in iNKR and NCR alleles have been linked to HIV-1 disease outcomes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.question: Which of the following factors is NOT mentioned as a source of HIV-inhibitory soluble factors produced by NK cells?
items.explanation: IL-2 is not mentioned as a source of HIV-inhibitory soluble factors produced by NK cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: Which polymorphism is associated with resistance to acquiring HIV infection?
options: HLA class I histocompatibility antigen protein P5
items.explanation: The CCR5 Δ32 allele encodes a truncated protein that is not expressed on the cell surface, conferring resistance to HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What is the effect of being homozygous for the CCR5 Δ32 allele?
options: Increased risk of acquiring HIV infection
Lack of CCR5 surface expression and high resistance to HIV
Higher viral load after HIV acquisition
No impact on HIV disease progression
items.explanation: Individuals homozygous for the CCR5 Δ32 allele lack CCR5 surface expression and are highly resistant to acquiring HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: In which population is the CCR5 Δ32 allele most commonly found at a homozygous state?
items.explanation: Approximately 1% of individuals of European ancestry are homozygous for the CCR5 Δ32 allele.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.question: What is a speculated reason for the emergence of the CCR5 Δ32 allele in European populations?
options: Secondary to an ancestral pandemic, such as the plague
items.explanation: The evolutionary pressure that resulted in the emergence of the CCR5 Δ32 allele is speculated to be secondary to an ancestral pandemic, such as the plague.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which CCR5 haplotype is associated with higher gene expression levels?
items.explanation: The CCR5-HHE haplotype is associated with higher CCR5 gene expression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which genotype provides strong resistance to HIV infection?
items.explanation: The CCR5 $ \Delta32/\Delta32$ genotype is associated with strong resistance to HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which haplotype pairing is associated with a lower risk of acquiring HIV infection?
items.explanation: The HHC/HHG'2 genotype is associated with a lower risk of acquiring HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.question: Which CCR5 haplotype is more common in persons of African ancestry?
items.explanation: The ancestral HHA haplotype is more common in persons of African ancestry.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: Which HLA-B allele is associated with faster progression to AIDS and higher viral load?
items.explanation: The HLA-B $ ^{35} $ allele is associated with faster progression to AIDS and higher viral load.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following HLA-B alleles are linked with slower disease progression in HIV infection?
items.explanation: Carriage of HLA-B $ ^{57} $ is associated with slower disease progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: In which population is the protective allele HLA-B $ ^{51} $ associated with a protective phenotype?
items.explanation: In Japanese, where HLA-B $ ^{57} $/HLA-B $ ^{27} $ alleles are absent, HLA-B $ ^{51} $ is associated with a protective phenotype.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.question: What effect does higher HLA-A levels have on HIV control?
options: Higher viral load and poorer control of HIV
items.explanation: Higher HLA-A levels associate with poorer control of HIV as well as higher viral load.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.question: Which SNV is associated with higher HLA-C expression and better HIV outcomes?
items.explanation: The SNV rs9264942 (T→C) is associated with higher HLA-C expression and better HIV outcomes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.question: Which allele's protective effect may be related to its linkage with the HLA-B*57:01 allele?
items.explanation: The protective effect of the HCP5 allele may be related to its linkage with the HLA-B*57:01 allele.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.question: What is the association between the rs1015164A allele and CCR5AS expression?
items.explanation: The rs1015164A allele is associated with higher expression of CCR5AS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.question: Which gene polymorphism is strongly associated with HIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN) in persons of African descent?
items.explanation: Polymorphisms in the MYH9 gene and neighboring APOL1 gene are a strong determinant of susceptibility to HIVAN.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.question: What genetic factor is associated with an increased risk of abacavir-associated hypersensitivity reactions?
items.explanation: The HLA-B*57:01 allele is associated with a higher risk of abacavir-associated hypersensitivity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.question: Which genetic variation influences the risk of developing HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder (HAND)?
options: Variations in CCL2 and CCL3 chemokines
items.explanation: Variations in the chemokines CCL2 and CCL3 influence the risk of developing HAND.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.question: Which genetic variant is associated with a significantly lower viral load set point and slower progression to AIDS?
options: A common haplotype in APOBEC3F gene
items.explanation: A common haplotype derived from SNVs in the APOBEC3F gene is associated with a lower viral load set point and slower progression to AIDS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which cell types are primarily infected by HIV in the brain?
items.explanation: The main cell types infected in the brain are perivascular macrophages and microglial cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is a proposed mechanism by which HIV enters the brain?
options: Induction of adhesion molecules on brain endothelium
Infection of B lymphocytes in the brain
items.explanation: HIV-infected and immune-activated macrophages induce adhesion molecules such as E-selectin and VCAM-1 on brain endothelium.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the nature of white matter lesions in people with HIV?
options: Resulting from axonal injury and disruption of the blood-brain barrier
Caused by direct infection of neurons
items.explanation: White matter lesions are due to axonal injury and a disruption of the blood-brain barrier, not demyelination.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Which monocyte-derived cytokine levels in the brain and CSF correlate best with the presence and degree of HIV encephalopathy in ART-naive patients?
items.explanation: Elevated levels of MCP-1 (CCL-2) in the brain and CSF correlate best with HIV encephalopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What role do astrocytes play in HIV neuropathogenesis?
options: Astrocytes may downregulate macrophage-produced neurotoxins and induce HIV expression in infected cells.
items.explanation: Astrocytes can both downregulate macrophage-produced neurotoxins and induce HIV expression in infected cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is suggested to occur weeks or a few months after initiation of ART in individuals with low CD4+ T cell counts?
options: Development of subacute encephalitis due to an IRIS reaction.
items.explanation: Subacute encephalitis due to an IRIS reaction can occur weeks or months after ART initiation in individuals with low CD4+ T cell counts.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.question: Which form of Kaposi's Sarcoma (KS) is associated with organ transplantation?
options: The classic form that occurs in older men of Mediterranean or eastern European Jewish backgrounds.
The form associated with organ transplantation and its attendant iatrogenic immunosuppressed state.
The form associated with HIV-1 infection.
items.explanation: KS associated with organ transplantation occurs due to the immunosuppressed state induced by the transplantation process.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.question: Which virus is universally found in the lesions of Kaposi's Sarcoma (KS)?
items.explanation: Sequences of Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (HHV-8) are found universally in the lesions of KS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.question: What is the role of HHV-8 in the pathogenesis of Kaposi's Sarcoma?
options: HHV-8 is not linked to KS.
HHV-8 transforms endothelial cells and has genes that can potentially transform host cells.
HHV-8 solely causes immunosuppression without transforming cells.
HHV-8 infection leads directly to the formation of neoplastic sarcoma.
items.explanation: HHV-8 possesses genes that can potentially transform endothelial cells and is linked to KS pathogenesis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.question: Which immune responses are involved in the body's reaction to HIV infection?
items.explanation: The immune response involves both humoral and cell-mediated immunity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.question: What is the median duration before clinically apparent disease develops in untreated individuals with HIV?
items.explanation: In untreated individuals, the development of clinically apparent disease is delayed for a median of 10 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.question: What is an early consequence of HIV infection on CD4+ T cells?
options: Increase in helper T cell population
No effect on CD4+ T cells
Interference with and decrease of the helper T cell population
items.explanation: An early consequence is interference with and a decrease in the helper T cell population.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: When do antibodies to HIV usually first appear after primary infection?
items.explanation: Antibodies to HIV usually appear within 3–6 weeks and almost invariably within 12 weeks of primary infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: Which viral proteins elicit neutralizing antibodies in HIV?
options: Pol gene products p31 and p66
items.explanation: The only viral proteins that elicit neutralizing antibodies are the envelope proteins gp120 and gp41.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: What mechanism allows HIV to escape from initial neutralizing antibodies?
items.explanation: One important mechanism of immune escape is the addition of N-linked glycosylation sites, forming a glycan shield.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of patients develop antibodies capable of neutralizing highly diverse HIV strains?
items.explanation: Approximately 20% of patients develop antibodies capable of neutralizing highly diverse strains.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: Which component of the immune system consists of approximately 30 proteins found in blood or associated with cell membranes?
items.explanation: The complement system is described as consisting of ~30 proteins circulating in blood or associated with cell membranes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: What role do HIV-specific CD4+ T cells play in the immune response to HIV?
options: Provide help to B and CD8+ T cells
items.explanation: HIV-specific CD4+ T cells provide help to HIV-specific B cells and CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: What is a characteristic of MHC class I-restricted, HIV-specific CD8+ T cells in patients with HIV-1 infection?
options: They bind to and lyse target cells presenting HIV antigens
They are involved in antibody-mediated enhancement
They inhibit viral replication without cytotoxic activity
items.explanation: CD8+ T cells bind to and cause the lytic destruction of target cells bearing MHC class I molecules presenting HIV antigens.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.question: What type of activity can be demonstrated by CD8+ T cells in the peripheral blood or lymph node mononuclear cells of people with HIV without prior stimulation?
items.explanation: CD8+ T cells can demonstrate spontaneous CTL activity, directly lysing target cells in culture without prior stimulation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.question: Which of the following mechanisms involves CD8+ T cells inhibiting HIV replication without killing infected targets?
options: Non-cytolytic CD8+ T cell-mediated suppression of HIV replication
ADCC involving NK cells with specific antibodies
NK cell activity directed at altered class I MHC molecules
Blockade of the HIV co-receptor by neutralizing antibodies
items.explanation: Non-cytolytic CD8+ T cell-mediated suppression refers to inhibition without killing.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.question: Which chemokines are involved in the non-cytolytic CD8+ T cell-mediated suppression of HIV replication?
items.explanation: RANTES, MIP-1α, and MIP-1β are the chemokines involved in this suppression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.question: What was added to blood donor screening in 1996 to help identify early HIV infections?
items.explanation: The p24 antigen capture assay was added in 1996.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the sensitivity of the ELISA test for detecting antibodies to HIV?
items.explanation: The ELISA test has a sensitivity of >99.5%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: Which assay is no longer used for confirming an HIV diagnosis?
items.explanation: The Western blot is no longer used for confirming an HIV diagnosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What should be done if a fourth-generation assay result is positive or inconclusive?
options: Consider the patient as definitively diagnosed with HIV
Confirm the result with an HIV-1-specific antibody immunoassay
Disregard the result and retest immediately
items.explanation: A positive or inconclusive fourth-generation assay should be confirmed with a more specific assay such as an HIV-1-specific antibody immunoassay.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the recommended initial test for patients suspected of having HIV infection?
items.explanation: The appropriate initial test for suspected HIV infection is a fourth-generation HIV-1/2 antigen antibody immunoassay.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: Which FDA-approved rapid test kit is available for home use to detect HIV?
items.explanation: The OraQuick test kit is the only FDA-approved rapid test kit available for home use in the United States.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What should be done if a point-of-care test for HIV returns a positive result?
items.explanation: Any positive results from point-of-care tests should be confirmed with standard laboratory testing.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What is the primary use of the p24 antigen capture assay in HIV diagnosis?
items.explanation: The p24 antigen capture assay is primarily used as a screening test for patients suspected of having the acute HIV syndrome.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What does a positive EIA combined with a positive HIV RNA assay indicate?
options: The gold standard for diagnosing HIV infection
