items.explanation: A positive EIA with a positive HIV RNA assay is considered the 'gold standard' for diagnosing HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: Which primer is typically used during the RT-PCR technique for making a cDNA copy of HIV RNA?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that tRNA $ Lys_{3} $ is used as the primary primer.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: What does RT-PCR result in when applied to HIV RNA?
options: Production of DNA copies of the HIV genome
items.explanation: RT-PCR results in the production of DNA copies of the HIV genome.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.question: In which patients are tests for quantitating HIV RNA or detecting proviral DNA particularly valuable?
options: Patients with a definitive positive EIA test
Patients with indeterminate serologic testing results
Patients in the early stages of HIV infection
items.explanation: These tests are valuable for patients with indeterminate serologic testing or unreliable results.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: Which CD4+ T cell count threshold indicates a high risk for disease from P. jirovecii in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: Patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL are at high risk of disease from P. jirovecii.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: At what CD4+ T cell count should primary prophylaxis for MAC infection be initiated unless the patient is immediately started on ART?
items.explanation: Primary prophylaxis for MAC infection is indicated when the CD4+ T cell count is <50/μL unless ART is started immediately.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: For patients on ART with persistently low HIV RNA levels and CD4 counts of 300–500/μL, how often should monitoring occur?
items.explanation: Monitoring may be decreased to every year for patients on ART with HIV RNA levels <50 copies/mL and CD4 counts of 300–500/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.question: In which clinical situation might the CD4+ T cell count be misleading?
options: Patients on ART for less than 2 years
items.explanation: In patients with HTLV-1/HIV co-infection, the CD4+ T cell count may not accurately reflect their degree of immune competence.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: When should HIV RNA levels be monitored approximately every 4 weeks?
options: After ART initiation until a new steady-state level of HIV RNA is determined
Only at the time of initial diagnosis
At the time of virologic failure
items.explanation: HIV RNA levels should be monitored every 4 weeks after ART initiation until a new steady-state level is determined.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What plasma HIV RNA level indicates effective antiretroviral therapy within 6 months?
items.explanation: Effective antiretroviral therapy typically reduces plasma HIV RNA to <50 copies/mL within 6 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: When is resistance testing indicated in the context of virologic failure?
options: While still on the failing regimen
Only after switching to a new drug
items.explanation: Resistance testing is indicated while the patient is still on the failing regimen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.question: What is required for an accurate resistance determination in HIV-1 RNA levels?
items.explanation: An HIV-1 RNA level above 500–1000 copies/mL is needed for accurate resistance determination.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically used as a marker for monitoring disease activity in patients with HIV infection?
options: Quantitative culture of replication-competent HIV from plasma
Presence or absence of activation markers such as CD38 on CD4+ T cells
items.explanation: Routine blood glucose monitoring is not mentioned as a marker for HIV disease activity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.question: What percentage of individuals with primary HIV infection experience an acute clinical syndrome?
items.explanation: It is estimated that 50–70% of individuals with HIV infection experience an acute clinical syndrome.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.question: Which symptom is less frequently observed in individuals infected by injection drug use compared to those infected by sexual contact during acute HIV infection?
items.explanation: Symptoms such as fever are less frequently observed in individuals infected by injection drug use compared to those infected by sexual contact.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.question: What is the typical course of CD4+ T cell levels after an acute HIV infection?
options: They decrease and do not recover.
They may experience a slight rebound toward normal before declining.
They increase to twice their original count.
items.explanation: CD4+ T cell levels usually remain somewhat depressed, although there may be a slight rebound toward normal.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.question: Which factor is directly correlated with the rate of HIV disease progression during the asymptomatic stage?
items.explanation: The rate of disease progression is directly correlated with HIV RNA levels.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.question: What characterizes patients known as elite nonprogressors?
options: They have high CD4+ T cell counts.
Their HIV RNA levels are <50 copies/mL.
They show rapid progression to symptomatic disease.
They maintain normal HIV RNA levels.
items.explanation: Elite nonprogressors exhibit extremely low levels of HIV RNA, specifically <50 copies/mL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.question: At what CD4+ T cell count level does a patient become at high risk for opportunistic infections?
items.explanation: When the CD4+ T cell count falls to <200/μL, the patient is at high risk for opportunistic infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which CD4+ T cell count threshold is used to define AIDS in individuals aged 6 years and older?
items.explanation: A diagnosis of AIDS is made when the CD4+ T cell count falls below <200/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which organism is commonly associated with sinusitis in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: Sinusitis in HIV-infected patients is often due to infections with encapsulated organisms like H. influenzae.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of deaths in treated HIV patients in developed countries are attributed to AIDS-related illnesses?
items.explanation: In developed countries, AIDS-related illnesses account for approximately ~25% of deaths in patients with HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which treatment is recommended for mucormycosis infections of the sinuses in HIV-infected patients?
options: Local and systemic amphotericin B with debridement
items.explanation: Mucormycosis of the sinuses in HIV-infected patients may be treated effectively with aggressive, frequent local debridement and local and systemic amphotericin B.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended CD4+ T cell count threshold for administering pneumococcal vaccines in patients with HIV?
items.explanation: Immunization is most effective if given while the CD4+ T cell count is >200/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: Which prophylactic measure is recommended for patients with HIV to prevent pneumococcal disease?
options: Conjugated pneumococcal vaccine followed by the 23-valent polysaccharide vaccine
items.explanation: Immunization with the conjugated pneumococcal vaccine followed by booster immunization with the 23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine is recommended.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What is the most common cause of pneumonia in patients with HIV infection in the United States?
items.explanation: Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) is the single most common cause of pneumonia in patients with HIV infection in the United States.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.question: What percentage of patients with PCP have CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL?
items.explanation: Approximately 79% of patients with PCP have CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: Which prophylactic treatment for PCP also provides protection against toxoplasmosis and some bacterial respiratory pathogens?
items.explanation: The preferred regimen for prophylaxis is TMP-SMX, which also provides protection against toxoplasmosis and some bacterial respiratory pathogens.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: In patients with HIV infection, what is the incidence range of side effects seen with TMP-SMX treatment for PCP?
items.explanation: A high (20–85%) incidence of side effects, particularly skin rash and bone marrow suppression, is seen with TMP-SMX in patients with HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: For which CD4+ T cell count threshold is prophylaxis for PCP indicated in individuals with HIV?
items.explanation: Prophylaxis for PCP is indicated for any patient with a CD4+ T cell count of <200/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.question: What is the treatment of choice for severe PCP in patients unable to tolerate TMP-SMX?
items.explanation: IV pentamidine is the treatment of choice for severe disease in patients unable to tolerate TMP-SMX.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: Which diagnostic test provides a definitive diagnosis for TB in patients with HIV?
options: Culture of the organism from an involved site
items.explanation: Culture of the organism provides a definitive diagnosis for TB.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: What is recommended to limit nosocomial and community spread when considering a diagnosis of pulmonary TB in HIV patients?
options: Respiratory isolation and a negative-pressure room
items.explanation: Respiratory isolation and a negative-pressure room are recommended to limit spread.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: In patients with HIV, when should ART be delayed in antiretroviral-naïve individuals with CD4 counts ≥50 cells/μL?
options: 2–8 weeks following the initiation of TB treatment
items.explanation: ART should be delayed for 2–8 weeks after starting TB treatment in these patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.question: What is the recommended treatment duration with once-weekly isoniazid and rifapentin for HIV patients at risk of active TB?
items.explanation: The recommended treatment duration is 12 weeks.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: Which mycobacterial infection is most commonly associated with HIV patients in the United States?
items.explanation: The passage states that MAC is the most common atypical mycobacterial infection in HIV patients in the United States.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: What is the typical CD4+ T cell count range for patients when Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infections occur?
items.explanation: The passage notes that MAC infection occurs predominantly in patients with CD4+ T cell counts of <50/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: What is the most common radiographic finding in patients with Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the most common chest x-ray pattern in MAC infections is a bilateral, lower-lobe infiltrate suggestive of miliary spread.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.question: Which treatment regimen is typically used for Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that therapy for MAC consists of a macrolide, usually clarithromycin, with ethambutol.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.question: Which form of idiopathic interstitial pneumonia is more commonly identified in children with HIV?
items.explanation: Lymphoid interstitial pneumonitis (LIP) is a common finding in children with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.question: What diagnostic procedure has a 50% success rate for diagnosing lymphoid interstitial pneumonitis?
items.explanation: Transbronchial biopsy is diagnostic in 50% of the cases for LIP.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.question: What percentage of people with HIV are seen to have HIV-related pulmonary arterial hypertension?
items.explanation: HIV-related pulmonary arterial hypertension (HIV-PAH) is seen in ~0.5% of people with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.question: Which condition associated with HIV infection has a median survival range of approximately 2 years?
items.explanation: The prognosis for HIV-related pulmonary arterial hypertension is poor with a median survival of about 2 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.question: Which of the following conditions is not commonly associated with oral lesions in untreated HIV infection?
items.explanation: Oral candidiasis is another term for thrush, which is commonly associated with oral lesions in untreated HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.question: What is the typical CD4+ T cell count threshold below which thrush and oral hairy leukoplakia generally occur?
items.explanation: Thrush and oral hairy leukoplakia generally occur in patients with CD4+ T cell counts of <300/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.question: Which condition is associated with florid replication of EBV in HIV-infected patients?
items.explanation: Oral hairy leukoplakia is associated with florid replication of EBV in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.question: Which type of esophagitis is typically associated with multiple small ulcers?
items.explanation: HSV infection in the esophagus is more often associated with multiple small ulcers.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with causing diarrhea in patients from tropical and subtropical regions?
items.explanation: Isospora belli is most commonly found as a cause of diarrhea in patients from tropical and subtropical regions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.question: What is the recommended treatment for Isospora infection?
items.explanation: Infection with Isospora belli is generally easy to treat with TMP-SMX.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.question: Which diagnostic method is definitive for microsporidia infection?
items.explanation: Definitive diagnosis of microsporidia generally depends on electron-microscopic examination.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.question: What is the typical presentation of CMV colitis in patients with AIDS?
options: Nonbloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, and anorexia
items.explanation: CMV colitis presents as nonbloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, and anorexia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.question: What is a common histologic finding in the small bowel of patients with HIV enteropathy?
options: Low-grade mucosal atrophy with decreased mitotic figures
items.explanation: Histologic examination reveals low-grade mucosal atrophy with a decrease in mitotic figures, suggesting a hyporegenerative state.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.question: Which of the following treatments is typically effective for perirectal ulcers due to HSV reactivation in HIV patients?
items.explanation: Perirectal ulcers due to HSV reactivation typically respond well to treatment with valacyclovir, famciclovir, or foscarnet.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.question: What percentage of deaths in patients with HIV infection is related to liver disease?
items.explanation: Approximately 15% of the deaths of patients with HIV infection are related to liver disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.question: Which hepatitis virus is most commonly co-infected with HIV in the United States?
items.explanation: In the U.S., ~90% of people with HIV have evidence of infection with HBV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.question: What is the impact of HIV on hepatitis B surface antigenemia?
options: It decreases the development of persistent hepatitis B surface antigenemia.
There is no significant impact on hepatitis B surface antigenemia.
It is associated with a ~3-fold increase in the development of persistent hepatitis B surface antigenemia.
It completely prevents the development of persistent hepatitis B surface antigenemia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.question: Which treatment should not be used alone for HBV in patients co-infected with HIV?
items.explanation: These drugs should not be used alone in patients with HIV to avoid resistance, and treatment should always be done in the setting of ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.question: What is the effect of HCV infection on overall mortality rates in people with HIV?
options: It significantly increases overall mortality rates.
There is no significant impact on overall mortality rates when other variables are considered.
It only affects mortality rates in patients without ART.
items.explanation: HCV infection does not appear to affect overall mortality rates in people with HIV when age, baseline CD4+ T cell count, and use of ART are taken into account.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.question: Which antiretroviral drug is associated with fatal fulminant and cholestatic hepatitis, hepatic necrosis, and hepatic failure?
items.explanation: Nevirapine has been associated with fatal fulminant and cholestatic hepatitis, hepatic necrosis, and hepatic failure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.question: Which condition is a hallmark of HIV-associated nephropathy?
items.explanation: Proteinuria is the hallmark of HIV-associated nephropathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.question: Which drug may cause renal calculi formation in patients with HIV?
items.explanation: Indinavir or atazanavir may form renal calculi.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.question: What is the mainstay of treatment and prevention for renal calculi caused by indinavir or atazanavir?
items.explanation: Adequate hydration is the mainstay of treatment and prevention for renal calculi caused by indinavir or atazanavir.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.question: Which clinical presentation is most common for syphilis in patients with HIV?
items.explanation: The most common presentation of syphilis in patients with HIV is condylomata lata.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.question: What should be considered in all patients with HIV infection and secondary syphilis to diagnose neurosyphilis?
items.explanation: A lumbar puncture should be considered in all patients with HIV infection and secondary syphilis to diagnose neurosyphilis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.question: Which test may yield false-positive results due to polyclonal B cell activation in HIV-infected patients?
items.explanation: The VDRL test may yield false-positive results due to polyclonal B cell activation in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.question: Which combination of drugs is most commonly associated with HIV-associated lipodystrophy?
items.explanation: Lipodystrophy is often associated with thymidine analogues and protease inhibitors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.question: What percentage of patients on ART develop HIV-associated lipodystrophy?
items.explanation: Between 33% and 75% of patients on ART develop HIV-associated lipodystrophy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.question: Which medication is suggested to be avoided due to severe lipoatrophy changes?
items.explanation: Severe lipoatrophy changes are particularly noted with thymidine analogues like stavudine.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.question: Which condition is associated with the use of local glucocorticoids in patients on ritonavir or cobicistat?
items.explanation: Iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome can occur with local glucocorticoids in patients on ritonavir or cobicistat.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.question: Which drug is associated with fatal hypersensitivity reactions upon rechallenge in patients with HIV?
items.explanation: Fatal hypersensitivity reactions to abacavir are strongly associated with the HLA-B5701 haplotype.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.question: What is a common characteristic of drug allergies in HIV patients?
items.explanation: Drug reactions are characterized by erythematous, morbilliform eruptions that are pruritic and often associated with fever.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.question: What is the primary difference in lymphocytic infiltrates between Sjögren's syndrome and HIV-associated diffuse infiltrative lymphocytosis syndrome (DILS)?
options: Infiltrates are composed predominantly of CD8+ T cells in DILS
Infiltrates are composed predominantly of B cells in DILS
No difference, both have similar compositions
items.explanation: In Sjögren's syndrome, infiltrates are mainly CD4+ T cells; in HIV-associated DILS, they are predominantly CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.question: Which autoimmune disease may occur with increased frequency in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: A variant of primary Sjögren's syndrome is more common in patients with HIV, characterized by parotid gland enlargement and lymphocytic infiltrates.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.question: Which joints are most commonly affected by HIV- or AIDS-associated arthropathy?
options: Large joints, predominantly knees and ankles
items.explanation: HIV- or AIDS-associated arthropathy generally involves large joints, predominantly the knees and ankles.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.question: What is a characteristic feature of painful articular syndrome in HIV patients?
options: Requires narcotic analgesics for severe pain
items.explanation: Painful articular syndrome can be severe enough to require narcotic analgesics.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.question: What is the incidence of fibromyalgia in a cohort of people with HIV containing 55% IDUs?
items.explanation: 11% of a cohort of people with HIV containing 55% IDUs were diagnosed as having fibromyalgia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with septic arthritis in HIV patients?
items.explanation: Septic arthritis when reported is usually due to Staphylococcus aureus.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.question: What is a common hematologic abnormality in patients with HIV that is independently associated with poor prognosis?
items.explanation: Anemia is the most common hematologic abnormality in patients with HIV and is independently associated with a poor prognosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.question: Which drug used to treat HIV can cause an elevated mean corpuscular volume (MCV) due to its effect on erythroid maturation?
items.explanation: The antiretroviral zidovudine may block erythroid maturation prior to its effects on other marrow elements, leading to an elevated MCV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.question: In patients with HIV infection and CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL, which of the following is NOT a differential diagnosis for lymphadenopathy?
items.explanation: In patients with CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL, toxoplasmosis is not a differential diagnosis for lymphadenopathy; it occurs in more advanced disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.question: What is the significance of a decrease in lymph node size outside the setting of ART in HIV patients?
options: It may be a prognostic marker of disease progression.
It suggests effective treatment with zidovudine.
It implies resolution of monoclonal gammopathy.
items.explanation: A decrease in lymph node size outside the setting of ART may paradoxically be a prognostic marker of disease progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.question: What is the incidence of thrombocytopenia in untreated HIV patients with CD4+ T cell counts <400/μL?
items.explanation: The incidence increases to 10% for untreated patients with CD4+ T cell counts <400/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.question: Which treatment is recommended for HIV patients with platelet counts <20,000/μL?
options: IVIg or anti-Rh Ig combined with ART
items.explanation: A more aggressive approach combining IVIg or anti-Rh Ig for an immediate response and ART for a lasting response is appropriate.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.question: What should be done before splenectomy in patients with HIV infection?
options: Immunize against S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, and H. influenzae type b
Conduct a CD4+ T cell percentage test
items.explanation: Patients should be immunized with vaccines to prevent disease from these organisms before splenectomy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.question: What is the approximate incidence of venous thromboembolic disease per year in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: The incidence is approximately 1% per year, which is about 10 times higher than that seen in an age-matched population.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.question: Which dermatologic disorder is more common in patients with HIV infection and CD4+ T cell counts <200 cells/μL?
items.explanation: Folliculitis is more common in patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200 cells/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.question: What is the treatment of choice for reactivation herpes zoster in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: Valacyclovir, famciclovir, or acyclovir are the treatments of choice for reactivation herpes zoster in HIV patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.question: What is a common symptom associated with pruritus in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: Pruritus can lead to prurigo nodularis in patients with HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.question: Which condition may be associated with an increase in plasma HIV RNA levels due to subclinical reactivation?
items.explanation: Subclinical reactivation of herpes simplex may be associated with increases in plasma HIV RNA levels.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following is considered an AIDS-defining illness?
items.explanation: HIV-associated dementia (HAD), also known as AIDS dementia complex or HIV encephalopathy, is considered an AIDS-defining illness.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: Which allele increases the risk for AIDS encephalopathy and peripheral neuropathy in people with HIV?
items.explanation: People with HIV who have the E4 allele for apoE are at increased risk for AIDS encephalopathy and peripheral neuropathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: What is the most common infectious cause of meningitis in patients with AIDS?
items.explanation: Fungal meningitis, primarily due to Cryptococcus neoformans, is the leading infectious cause of meningitis in patients with AIDS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: Which CSF finding is most commonly abnormal in untreated patients with HIV infection?
options: Evidence of intrathecal synthesis of anti-HIV antibodies
items.explanation: CSF findings are abnormal in ~90% of untreated patients, with evidence of intrathecal synthesis of anti-HIV antibodies being common.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: Which antifungal agent is recommended for initial treatment of cryptococcal meningitis in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: Initial treatment is with IV amphotericin B or liposomal amphotericin.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: What is a major feature of HIV-associated dementia?
items.explanation: A major feature of HIV-associated dementia is the development of dementia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: Which cell types are primarily involved in harboring HIV within the CNS?
items.explanation: Multinucleated giant cells, macrophages, and microglial cells appear to be the main cell types harboring virus in the CNS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: What is a common motor problem associated with HIV encephalopathy?
items.explanation: Among the motor problems are unsteady gait, poor balance, tremor, and difficulty with rapid alternating movements.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.question: Which imaging study often demonstrates evidence of cerebral atrophy in patients with HIV encephalopathy?
items.explanation: Both MRI and CT scans can demonstrate evidence of cerebral atrophy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.question: What is the role of lumbar puncture in evaluating patients with HIV infection and neurologic abnormalities?
options: It confirms the presence of HIV encephalopathy.
It helps rule out or diagnose opportunistic infections.
items.explanation: Lumbar puncture is helpful in ruling out or diagnosing opportunistic infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following factors have been noted in the CSF of patients with HIV encephalopathy and suggest involvement in its pathogenesis?
options: Increased levels of MCP-1, $ \beta_{2} $-microglobulin, neopterin, and quinolinic acid.
items.explanation: Increased levels of MCP-1, $ \beta_{2} $-microglobulin, neopterin, and quinolinic acid suggest involvement in the pathogenesis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.question: What is a common consequence of advanced HIV infection that affects seizure threshold?
options: Increased seizure threshold due to electrolyte balance.
Decreased seizure threshold due to electrolyte abnormalities.
Stable seizure threshold regardless of infection stage.
Seizure threshold unaffected by HIV status.
items.explanation: The seizure threshold is often lower than normal in patients with advanced HIV infection, partly due to electrolyte abnormalities.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.question: In patients with AIDS, what is the most common clinical presentation of cerebral toxoplasmosis?
options: Fever, headache, and focal neurologic deficits
items.explanation: The most common clinical presentation includes fever, headache, and focal neurologic deficits.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.question: Which diagnostic imaging finding is typically associated with cerebral toxoplasmosis in patients with HIV?
items.explanation: Cerebral toxoplasmosis typically shows multiple lesions with ring enhancement on MRI or contrast CT.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.question: What is the recommended duration for standard treatment of cerebral toxoplasmosis?
items.explanation: Standard treatment is recommended for a minimum of 4–6 weeks.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.question: When should primary prophylaxis for toxoplasmosis be considered in patients with HIV?
options: CD4+ T cell counts <100 cells/μL and IgG antibody to Toxoplasma present
In all patients regardless of CD4+ count
items.explanation: Primary prophylaxis is recommended for patients with CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL and IgG antibody to Toxoplasma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.question: Which of the following statements about Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML) is true?
options: PML is caused by a herpesvirus.
The majority of healthy adults show ongoing JC virus replication.
MRI typically reveals multiple, nonenhancing white matter lesions in PML.
Headache and fever are common symptoms in patients with PML.
items.explanation: PML is caused by the JC virus, not a herpesvirus. Ongoing viral replication is rare in healthy adults. MRI findings include nonenhancing white matter lesions; headache and fever are rarely seen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.question: What is the diagnostic sensitivity of measuring JC virus DNA levels in CSF for PML?
items.explanation: The measurement of JC virus DNA levels in CSF has a diagnostic sensitivity of 76% and specificity close to 100%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.question: Which factor does NOT independently predict survival in patients with PML?
options: Ability to maintain an HIV viral load <500 copies/mL
items.explanation: Baseline HIV-1 viral load does not have independent predictive value for survival in PML.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic radiographic finding in reactivation American trypanosomiasis?
options: Deep lesions similar to those seen in toxoplasmosis
Single or multiple hypodense areas with ring enhancement and edema
items.explanation: Reactivation American trypanosomiasis shows single or multiple hypodense areas, typically with ring enhancement and edema.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.question: Which spinal cord disease type is characterized by a subacute onset and often presents with gait disturbances, predominantly ataxia and spasticity?
options: Paresthesias and dysesthesias of the lower extremities
items.explanation: Vacuolar myelopathy is characterized by a subacute onset with gait disturbances, predominantly ataxia and spasticity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.question: Which condition associated with spinal cord disease in patients with HIV infection is fulminant in onset and involves lower extremity and sacral paresthesias?
items.explanation: CMV-associated myelopathy is fulminant in onset and involves lower extremity and sacral paresthesias.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.question: What type of peripheral neuropathy is most common in patients with HIV infection?
items.explanation: The most common peripheral neuropathy in patients with HIV infection is distal sensory polyneuropathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.question: Which treatment can lead to rapid improvement in CMV-associated myelopathy?
items.explanation: Intravenous therapy with ganciclovir or foscarnet can lead to rapid improvement in CMV-associated myelopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.question: Which histologic feature is a hallmark of zidovudine-induced myopathy?
items.explanation: Red ragged fibers are specifically mentioned as a histologic hallmark of zidovudine-induced myopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.question: What is the typical CD4+ T cell count threshold for patients at high risk of CMV retinitis?
items.explanation: Patients with a CD4+ T cell count <100/μL are at high risk for CMV retinitis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.question: What is the most common abnormal finding on funduscopic examination in patients with advanced HIV infection?
items.explanation: Cotton-wool spots are the most common abnormal findings on funduscopic examination in patients with advanced HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.question: Which therapy is generally avoided for CMV retinitis due to increased risk of uveitis and hypotony?
items.explanation: Intravitreal injections of cidofovir are generally avoided due to the increased risk of uveitis and hypotony.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.question: Which organism is most commonly associated with bacillary angiomatosis in patients with HIV?
items.explanation: Bacillary angiomatosis is usually due to infection with B. henselae.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.question: What is the typical CD4+ T cell count for patients presenting with histoplasmosis and AIDS?
items.explanation: The median CD4+ T cell count for patients with histoplasmosis and AIDS was 33/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.question: Which of the following is a common clinical manifestation of Bartonella infection?
items.explanation: Bacillary angiomatosis is a clinical manifestation of Bartonella infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.question: What diagnostic method is often used for identifying Bartonella organisms?
items.explanation: Diagnosis of Bartonella infection often relies on PCR and/or seroconversion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.question: Which condition is considered an AIDS-defining illness and more frequently diagnosed during the rainy season in southeast Asia?
items.explanation: Talaromyces marneffei infection is more frequently diagnosed during the rainy season in southeast Asia and is considered an AIDS-defining condition.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.question: What treatment is recommended for Talaromyces marneffei infection until CD4+ T cell count is >100 cells/μL for at least 6 months?
items.explanation: The treatment for Talaromyces marneffei infection is amphotericin B followed by itraconazole until the CD4+ T cell count is >100 cells/μL for at least 6 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.question: Which of the following neoplastic diseases has seen a marked reduction in incidence since the introduction of potent ART?
items.explanation: The incidence of Kaposi's sarcoma has markedly reduced since the introduction of potent ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.question: What is a common clinical feature of generalized wasting as an AIDS-defining condition?
options: Intermittent or constant fever and chronic diarrhea
items.explanation: Generalized wasting is defined by involuntary weight loss with intermittent or constant fever and chronic diarrhea lasting >30 days.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.question: Which organ system is NOT commonly affected by Kaposi's sarcoma (KS) according to the passage?
items.explanation: The heart is not listed as one of the most commonly affected organ systems by KS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.question: What percentage of patients with pulmonary Kaposi's sarcoma also have cutaneous lesions?
items.explanation: Approximately 80% of patients with pulmonary KS also have cutaneous lesions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.question: What is the typical radiographic finding in patients with pulmonary Kaposi's sarcoma?
options: Bilateral lower lobe infiltrates obscuring mediastinum and diaphragm margins
items.explanation: The chest x-ray characteristically shows bilateral lower lobe infiltrates that obscure the margins of the mediastinum and diaphragm.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.question: Which condition is NOT part of the differential diagnosis for Kaposi's sarcoma lesions?
items.explanation: Sclerosing cholangitis is not part of the differential diagnosis for KS lesions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.question: Which chemotherapeutic agent is approved by the FDA as first-line therapy for patients with advanced Kaposi's sarcoma despite ART?
items.explanation: Liposomal daunorubicin and pomalidomide are approved as first-line therapy for patients with advanced KS despite ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.question: What is the approximate response rate to IFN-$\alpha$ for patients with CD4+ T cell counts >600/$\mu$L?
items.explanation: The response rate to IFN-$\alpha$ for patients with CD4+ T cell counts >600/$\mu$L is ~80%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.question: Which type of lymphoma accounts for approximately 60% of the cases of lymphoma in patients with AIDS?
items.explanation: Immunoblastic lymphomas account for ~60% of the cases of lymphoma in patients with AIDS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.question: Which virus is considered to be the driving force behind the oncogenesis of primary effusion lymphoma?
items.explanation: KSHV is felt to be the driving force behind the oncogenesis of primary effusion lymphoma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.question: What is the approximate percentage of lymphoma cases in patients with AIDS that are accounted for by small noncleaved cell lymphoma (Burkitt's lymphoma)?
items.explanation: Small noncleaved cell lymphoma accounts for ~20% of the cases of lymphoma in patients with AIDS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.question: What is the median CD4+ T cell count at the time of diagnosis for primary CNS lymphoma?
items.explanation: The median CD4+ T cell count at the time of diagnosis for primary CNS lymphoma is ~50/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.question: Which of the following sites is most commonly involved by extranodal disease in patients with lymphoma and HIV infection?
items.explanation: The CNS is the most common extranodal site involved, accounting for approximately one-third of all patients with lymphoma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.question: What percentage of systemic lymphomas in patients with HIV have CNS disease in the form of leptomeningeal involvement?
items.explanation: Approximately 20% of patients with systemic lymphoma have CNS disease in the form of leptomeningeal involvement.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.question: Which of the following features are commonly seen in Multicentric Castleman's disease (MCD) associated with HIV infection?
items.explanation: Multicentric Castleman's disease shares features with lymphoma, including generalized lymphadenopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.question: What is the approximate percentage of patients with Multicentric Castleman's disease who present with pulmonary symptoms?
items.explanation: Pulmonary symptoms may be seen in approximately 50% of patients with Multicentric Castleman's disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.question: Which treatment for MCD has been associated with worsening of coexisting Kaposi's sarcoma?
items.explanation: Rituximab may be beneficial for MCD but has been associated with worsening of coexisting Kaposi's sarcoma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.question: What is the relative risk increase for cervical and anal cancers in people with HIV compared to those without?
items.explanation: The relative risk of cervical and anal cancers in people with HIV compared to those without is on the order of 10- to 100-fold.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic feature of idiopathic CD4+ lymphocytopenia (ICL)?
options: An absolute CD4+ T cell count of <300/μL or <20% of total T cells on two occasions at least 6 weeks apart.
Evidence of HIV-1, HIV-2, HTLV-1, or HTLV-2 infection.
Absence of any defined immunodeficiency or therapy associated with decreased levels of CD4+ T cells.
Development of opportunistic diseases such as cryptococcosis and nontuberculous mycobacterial infections.
items.explanation: ICL is characterized by the absence of evidence for HIV-1, HIV-2, HTLV-1, or HTLV-2 infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.question: Which opportunistic disease was most commonly observed in patients with ICL according to the passage?
items.explanation: Molluscum contagiosum was observed in 95% of patients with ICL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.question: What percentage of ICL patients may exhibit an autoimmune disease?
items.explanation: Approximately 10–30% of ICL patients may exhibit an autoimmune disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.question: Which statement best describes the immunologic abnormalities in ICL compared to HIV infection?
options: Increased CD8+ T cells and normal B cell levels.
Decreased CD4+ T cell activation with increased B cells.
Increases in CD4+ T cell activation with decreases in CD8+ T cells and B cells.
Normal immunoglobulin levels similar to HIV infection.
items.explanation: ICL patients often have increases in CD4+ T cell activation with decreases in CD8+ T cells and B cells, unlike the hypergammaglobulinemia seen in HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.question: According to CDC guidelines, what approach should be taken for HIV testing in routine medical care?
options: Testing should only occur if the patient requests it.
Patients must provide informed consent before being tested.
HIV testing should be a part of routine medical care with an 'opt out' option.
Routine HIV testing is not recommended.
items.explanation: The CDC recommends that HIV testing be included in routine medical care, allowing patients to opt out if they choose.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.question: What is the significance of pretest counseling for patients undergoing HIV testing?
options: It ensures that all patients receive ART immediately after diagnosis.
Pretest counseling prepares patients emotionally in case they test positive.
Counseling is unnecessary if the patient has prior knowledge about HIV.
The main purpose is to educate patients on how to prevent transmission.
items.explanation: Pretest counseling helps prepare patients emotionally for a potential diagnosis of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.question: What is the importance of ART in managing HIV infection?
options: ART can completely eradicate HIV from the body.
ART helps patients live longer and healthier lives despite HIV.
ART is only necessary for patients with advanced disease stages.
ART is primarily used to prevent transmission rather than treat the patient.
items.explanation: Appropriate use of ART provides patients with the best opportunity to live a long and healthy life with HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.question: Why should all patients with HIV, especially those with CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL, designate a proxy for medical decisions?
options: To ensure they receive the latest medications available.
Because they are likely to become incapacitated and unable to make decisions.
It is only necessary if they do not have access to regular healthcare.
Designating a proxy has no significant impact on their treatment.
items.explanation: Patients with low CD4+ T cell counts are at risk of incapacitation, necessitating a proxy for medical decisions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.question: Which vaccine should be administered to HIV patients who are seronegative for the virus?
options: HPV, hepatitis A, and hepatitis B vaccines
items.explanation: HIV patients who are seronegative for these viruses should receive HPV, hepatitis A, and hepatitis B vaccines.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended frequency of influenza vaccination for HIV patients?
items.explanation: HIV patients should receive annual influenza shots.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.question: What is an important aspect of counseling for HIV patients?
options: Counseling regarding sexual practices and needle sharing
items.explanation: Patients should be counseled with regard to sexual practices and needle sharing.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: When is it recommended to delay the initiation of antiretroviral therapy (ART) in HIV-infected patients?
options: In cases of cryptococcal meningitis or TB where specific antimicrobial therapy is needed first.
Immediately after diagnosis regardless of any other conditions.
Only when the patient has a low CD4 count.
When the patient shows no symptoms.
items.explanation: ART should be delayed in cases like cryptococcal meningitis or TB to reduce severe IRIS risk after specific antimicrobial therapy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: What is a typical regimen for ART?
options: One nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor plus two protease inhibitors.
Two nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors plus a nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor, an integrase inhibitor, or a boosted protease inhibitor.
Three CCR5 antagonists combined with a pharmacokinetic enhancer.
One fusion inhibitor and one CD4 antagonist.
items.explanation: A typical ART regimen includes two nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors plus another class of antiretroviral drug.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: Why is adherence to prescribed regimens crucial in ART?
options: To ensure the patient feels better immediately.
To minimize the risk of HIV transmission and improve quality of life by suppressing HIV replication.
Because it reduces the need for drug resistance testing.
Adherence is not important as long as drugs are taken occasionally.
items.explanation: Strict adherence to ART regimens ensures suppression of HIV replication, improving patient quality of life and minimizing transmission risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.question: Which drugs are not active against HIV-2?
options: Nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, enfuvirtide, and fostemsavir.
items.explanation: The nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors, enfuvirtide, and fostemsavir are not effective against HIV-2.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended use for zidovudine and stavudine in the United States?
options: For treatment of HIV infection due to side-effect profiles
For treating hepatitis B in coinfected patients
items.explanation: Zidovudine and stavudine are not recommended for use in the United States due to their side-effect profiles.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following is a reason why rilpivirine should be used with caution?
options: It can cause significant renal toxicity
It is contraindicated in patients taking proton pump inhibitors
It has high affinity for cytochrome P450 enzymes
It leads to rapid development of drug resistance
items.explanation: Rilpivirine is contraindicated in patients taking proton pump inhibitors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following integrase inhibitors requires cobicistat to boost its concentrations?
items.explanation: Elvitegravir is always given in combination with cobicistat to boost its concentrations.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following statements about tenofovir disoproxil and tenofovir alafenamide is correct?
options: Both should be used in patients with CrCl >30
Tenofovir disoproxil should be limited to use in patients with CrCl >70
Tenofovir alafenamide can be used without regard to renal function
Both are preferred for initial ART regimens regardless of renal function
items.explanation: Tenofovir disoproxil should be limited to use in patients with CrCl >70.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.question: Which drug is a CCR5 antagonist used in antiretroviral therapy?
items.explanation: Maraviroc is the CCR5 antagonist mentioned.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.question: What type of assay should be performed before using maraviroc?
items.explanation: A co-receptor tropism assay is needed to determine if the virus is CCR5-tropic.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.question: How often is lenacapavir administered after the initial regimen?
items.explanation: Lenacapavir is given by subcutaneous injection every 6 months.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.question: What is the recommended timing for initiating ART in patients with advanced HIV infection?
options: only if CD4+ count falls below 50 cells/μL
items.explanation: ART should be initiated as soon as possible for patients with advanced HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: What is the goal of therapy in terms of viral replication?
options: Increase in CD4+ T cell counts
items.explanation: The goal is maximal suppression of viral replication to reduce the likelihood of drug-resistant quasispecies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: What should be done at the time of initiation of therapy to ensure appropriate antiretroviral agents are selected?
options: Monitor CD4+ T cell counts only
Delay treatment until resistance is confirmed
items.explanation: A viral genotype should be done to ensure appropriate antiretroviral agents are selected.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: What is a recommended action if there is less than a 1-log drop in plasma HIV RNA by 4 weeks following the initiation of therapy?
options: Initiate at least two new effective drugs
Increase the dosage of current drugs
items.explanation: A change should involve the initiation of at least two drugs felt to be effective.
