items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.question: What is the expected change in CD4+ T cell count within 6 months after initiating therapy?
items.explanation: There should be a rise in the CD4+ T cell count of 100–150/cells μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: What is the anticipated increase in CD4+ T cell count per year after the first month of therapy?
items.explanation: The passage states that one should anticipate a CD4+ T cell count increase of 50–100 cells/year after the first month of therapy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: When is resistance testing recommended in the context of initial therapy choices?
options: Only when there is clinical progression
In settings with a high risk of transmission of resistant virus
After switching to a new regimen regardless of setting
items.explanation: Resistance testing is recommended in confirming initial therapy choices in settings where the risk of transmission of resistant virus is high.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: What should be monitored within 2–4 weeks after initiation or change in ART regimen?
items.explanation: The passage specifies that plasma HIV RNA levels should be monitored within 2–4 weeks after initiation of ART or following a change in regimen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.question: What is the inhibitory quotient used for?
items.explanation: The inhibitory quotient, defined as the trough blood level/IC50 of the patient's virus, is used to determine the adequacy of dosing of a given treatment regimen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.question: What percentage risk of HIV infection following a percutaneous exposure to HIV-contaminated blood in an individual who does not receive postexposure prophylaxis (PEP) is suggested by data from several large studies?
items.explanation: The risk of HIV infection following a percutaneous exposure to HIV-contaminated blood in an individual who does not receive PEP is ~0.23%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.question: Which type of health care worker exposure has the highest documented risk for HIV infection?
options: Exposure to body fluids other than blood
items.explanation: Approximately 85% of the exposures resulting in infection have been due to percutaneous (puncture/cut injury) exposures.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.question: What is a common cause for needle-stick injuries among health care workers?
items.explanation: 27% of needle-stick injuries resulted from improper disposal of the needle.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.question: What is recommended for all occupational exposures to HIV according to U.S. Public Health Service guidelines?
items.explanation: PEP regimens should contain three (or more) antiretroviral drugs administered for a 4-week duration.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.question: Which of the following precautions should be routinely used by healthcare workers to minimize the risk of occupational HIV infection?
options: Use barriers like gloves and/or goggles when anticipating contact with blood or body fluids.
Avoid washing hands after contact with blood or body fluids if gloves were worn.
Handle sharp instruments without any special precautions during use.
Assume that only patients known to be HIV-positive are potentially infectious.
items.explanation: Healthcare workers should routinely use barriers such as gloves and/or goggles when anticipating contact with blood or body fluids, according to CDC guidelines.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.question: What is the recommended action for healthcare workers dealing with patients who have both HBV and HIV?
options: They should avoid immunization as it poses a risk.
Immunization with the HBV vaccine is advisable due to high prevalence of HBV in people with HIV.
Only those exposed to needle-stick injuries need HBV vaccination.
Routine immunization for HBV is unnecessary if they are already vaccinated against HIV.
items.explanation: Healthcare workers dealing with patients who have both HBV and HIV should be immunized with the HBV vaccine due to the high prevalence of HBV in people with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.question: What is a key infection control measure for healthcare workers when dealing with patients suspected of having active pulmonary TB?
options: Healthcare workers should not be concerned about their PPD status.
Patients suspected of having active pulmonary TB should be placed in respiratory isolation pending diagnostic evaluation results.
Isoniazid treatment is unnecessary if the skin test remains negative.
Respiratory isolation is only required for confirmed cases, not suspected ones.
items.explanation: Patients in whom a diagnosis of active pulmonary TB is being entertained should be placed immediately in respiratory isolation pending results of the diagnostic evaluation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.question: What percentage of persons with HIV in the United States are estimated to be unaware of their infection status?
items.explanation: It is estimated that ~13% of persons with HIV do not know their HIV status.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.question: What age range does the CDC recommend for routine HIV testing in the United States?
items.explanation: The CDC recommends that all individuals between the ages of 13 and 64 years are tested at least once.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the failure rate of condoms used for contraceptive purposes?
items.explanation: There is a ~10% failure rate of condoms used for contraceptive purposes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.question: Which product has WHO recommended as an additional prevention choice for women at substantial risk of HIV infection?
items.explanation: WHO has recommended a vaginal ring that releases the antiretroviral drug dapivirine as an additional prevention choice.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.question: Which antiretroviral regimen is noted to be 99% effective in preventing HIV acquisition with high adherence?
items.explanation: High adherence to a PrEP regimen of emtricitabine + tenofovir disoproxil fumarate is 99% effective in preventing HIV acquisition.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.question: What is the most effective way to prevent transmission of HIV infection among injection drug users (IDUs)?
options: Stopping the use of injectable drugs
Sharing needles after cleaning with sodium hypochlorite
items.explanation: The most effective way to prevent transmission among IDUs is to stop the use of injectable drugs.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.question: What percentage reduction in HIV acquisition does adult male circumcision result in?
items.explanation: Adult male circumcision results in a 50–65% reduction in HIV acquisition.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.question: Which statement best describes why an effective HIV vaccine has not yet been developed?
options: The body can clear HIV infection naturally, providing lifelong immunity.
There is no natural immune response to HIV infection.
The high mutability of the virus and inability of the natural immune response to clear it make development challenging.
HIV vaccines are not necessary because superinfection does not occur.
items.explanation: An effective HIV vaccine has not been developed due to the high mutability of the virus and the failure of the natural immune response to clear it from the body.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.question: What is a common characteristic of successful vaccines for other diseases that is lacking in HIV?
options: They induce lifelong immunity against future exposure.
They prevent any form of infection from occurring.
They are based on the body's inability to clear the infectious agent.
They mimic the natural response to infection and provide protection.
items.explanation: Successful vaccines for other diseases mimic the natural immune response to infection, providing protection, which is not achievable with HIV due to its high mutability and inability of the body to clear it naturally.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.question: Why are cases of superinfection significant in the context of developing an HIV vaccine?
options: They indicate that the immune system can handle multiple strains of HIV.
They suggest that natural immunity to HIV is strong and effective.
They highlight the challenge due to the virus's ability to evade the immune response.
They demonstrate that current vaccines are partially effective.
items.explanation: Cases of superinfection underscore the difficulty in developing an HIV vaccine because they show the virus's capacity to evade the immune system, even when it has been previously exposed.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.statement: HIV-2 was first identified in the United States.
items.explanation: HIV-2 was first identified in West African patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.statement: Nononcogenic lentiviruses cause disease in other animal species.
items.explanation: Nononcogenic lentiviruses do indeed cause disease in various animal species.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.statement: The viral envelope protein gp41 facilitates the fusion of the HIV virion with the host cell membrane.
items.explanation: Gp41 undergoes conformational changes that allow it to penetrate and fuse the plasma membrane of the target cell.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.statement: SAMHD1 promotes reverse transcription by increasing dNTP pools.
items.explanation: SAMHD1 depletes dNTPs, thereby inhibiting reverse transcription.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.statement: Tetherin, an IFN-induced protein, promotes the release of budding HIV particles.
items.explanation: Tetherin interferes with virion detachment and inhibits the release of budding HIV particles.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME
items.statement: The gag gene in the HIV-1 genome encodes proteins that form the core of the virion.
items.explanation: The gag gene encodes structural proteins, including p24 antigen, forming the core of the HIV virion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 2) | HIV GENOME

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.statement: HIV-1 subtype C is the most common form globally, accounting for approximately 50% of infections.
items.explanation: Subtype C viruses are by far the most common form worldwide.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.statement: HIV-1 subtype B is predominant in sub-Saharan Africa.
items.explanation: In sub-Saharan Africa, most infections are caused by subtype C.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.statement: HIV can be transmitted by casual contact.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that HIV is transmitted through casual contact.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.statement: HIV's molecular heterogeneity affects vaccine development.
items.explanation: The diversity of HIV strains poses a challenge to developing a broadly effective vaccine.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.statement: HIV transmission via unprotected receptive anal intercourse carries a lower risk than vaginal intercourse.
items.explanation: The risk of HIV acquisition is higher during unprotected receptive anal intercourse compared to vaginal intercourse.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.statement: Treating STIs can help decrease the transmission of HIV, especially in populations with low prevalence.
items.explanation: Treating STIs and genital tract syndromes may help decrease HIV transmission, particularly where HIV prevalence is relatively low.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.statement: Oral sex is completely safe and cannot transmit HIV.
items.explanation: There have been well-documented reports of HIV transmission from oral sex, so it is not considered completely safe.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.statement: The use of antiretroviral therapy to reduce viral load can significantly decrease the risk of HIV transmission.
items.explanation: Antiretroviral therapy reduces plasma viremia and is associated with a dramatic reduction in the risk of transmission.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.statement: The majority of HIV infections from contaminated blood products in resource-rich countries occurred after the implementation of mandatory testing for HIV-1.
items.explanation: Most HIV infections via contaminated blood products in resource-rich countries occurred before mandatory HIV-1 testing was initiated.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.statement: The risk of HIV transmission through transfused blood in the United States is approximately 1 in 2 million units.
items.explanation: The estimated risk of infection with HIV via screened blood in the U.S. is about 1 in 2 million units.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.statement: Transmission of HIV through intact skin has been documented.
items.explanation: There is no documentation of HIV transmission through intact skin.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.statement: The risk for HIV infection following percutaneous injury is higher than the risk of HBV infection.
items.explanation: The risk for HBV infection (~6-30%) is significantly higher than that for HIV (~0.23%).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.statement: The rate of mother-to-child transmission of HIV has fallen to <1% in pregnant women receiving ART.
items.explanation: ART and cesarean section delivery have reduced the rate of mother-to-child transmission of HIV to less than 1%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.statement: Exclusive breast-feeding carries a higher risk of HIV transmission than mixed feeding.
items.explanation: Exclusive breast-feeding has been reported to carry a lower risk of HIV transmission compared to mixed feeding.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.statement: HIV transmission can occur through exposure to tears.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that HIV transmission can occur as a result of exposure to tears.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS
items.statement: Transmission of HIV by a human bite is common.
items.explanation: Transmission of HIV by a human bite can occur but is considered a rare event.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION OF HIV BY OTHER BODY FLUIDS

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.statement: The number of global AIDS deaths decreased by 65% since 2000.
items.explanation: Global AIDS deaths totaled 630,000 in 2023, a 65% decrease since 2000.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.statement: The majority of new HIV infections globally occur among people aged over 50 years.
items.explanation: About one-third of new infections were among people age 15–24 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.statement: The Middle East and North Africa region has the highest HIV prevalence rates in the world.
items.explanation: This region has one of the lowest HIV prevalence rates in the world (<0.1%).
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.statement: In Eastern Europe and Central Asia, key populations account for approximately 94% of new HIV infections.
items.explanation: Members of key populations and their sexual partners account for ~94% of new infections in the region.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.statement: In 2022, there were approximately 1750 new perinatal HIV infections in the United States.
items.explanation: In 2022, only 62 children were newly diagnosed with HIV infection in the United States.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.statement: The rate of new HIV infections among Blacks/African Americans from 2018 to 2022 was proportionate to their representation in the U.S. population.
items.explanation: Blacks/African Americans, who constitute only 12% of the U.S. population, accounted for 37% of new HIV infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Kaposi's sarcoma and certain neurologic abnormalities in AIDS can occur prior to severe immunologic impairment.
items.explanation: These complications may occur before the development of severe immunologic impairment.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: The pathogenic mechanisms of HIV disease are uniform and consistent throughout all stages.
items.explanation: Pathogenic mechanisms of HIV disease are multifactorial, multiphasic, and vary at different stages.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOPHYSIOLOGY AND PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.statement: The efficiency of sexual transmission of HIV is high due to the abundance of CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: The spatial dispersion of CD4+ T cells in the mucosa provides a significant obstacle, accounting for the low efficiency of sexual transmission.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.statement: The gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) is a significant target for HIV infection.
items.explanation: HIV infects and depletes CD4+ T cells in the GALT, contributing to its role as a major site of viral replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.statement: HIV is completely cleared from the body in most human viral infections if the host survives.
items.explanation: Unlike some other viruses, HIV is not completely cleared from the body.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.statement: The set point of plasma viremia after ~1 year correlates with the rate of disease progression in untreated patients.
items.explanation: The steady-state level of plasma viremia is correlated with disease progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.statement: Broadly neutralizing antibodies are effective in containing the virus early during HIV infection.
items.explanation: Broadly neutralizing antibodies generally require 2–3 years of infection to develop and are ineffective at that point.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.statement: NK cells effectively eliminate HIV-infected cells due to the downregulation of HLA class I molecules.
items.explanation: Despite HLA class I molecule downregulation, NK cells do not effectively remove HIV-infected cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.statement: The majority of latently infected cells contain replication-competent virus.
items.explanation: Most latently infected cells have defective proviruses incapable of full replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.statement: Preintegration latency involves the integration of HIV DNA into the host genome.
items.explanation: In preintegration latency, proviral DNA fails to integrate into the host genome.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.statement: The viral set point has no prognostic implications for the progression of HIV disease in untreated patients.
items.explanation: The level of steady-state viremia, or viral set point, at ~1 year following acquisition of HIV infection has important prognostic implications.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)
items.statement: Clinical latency in HIV means there is no virus replication occurring.
items.explanation: During clinical latency, varying levels of virus replication occur; it does not mean the absence of virus replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 4)

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.statement: Long-term nonprogressors are always long-term survivors.
items.explanation: While all long-term nonprogressors are long-term survivors, not all long-term survivors are nonprogressors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.statement: The survival of people with HIV has dramatically increased with the advent of effective ART.
items.explanation: Effective ART has significantly improved the life expectancy of individuals with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: The level of plasma viremia directly reflects virus production in blood.
items.explanation: Plasma viremia reflects virus production in lymphoid tissue, not directly in the blood.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: Follicular helper CD4+ T cells are highly susceptible to HIV infection.
items.explanation: CD4+ TF_H cells in germinal centers are highly susceptible and contribute to the HIV reservoir.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.statement: The destruction of lymphoid tissue architecture in advanced HIV disease is associated with increased levels of IL-7.
items.explanation: IL-7 levels decrease, not increase, contributing to the disruption of lymphoid tissue.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.statement: In advanced HIV disease, there is a complete preservation of FRC and FDC networks.
items.explanation: Advanced HIV disease leads to the dissolution of FRC and FDC networks.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: HIV replication is efficient even in quiescent CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: HIV replicates most efficiently in activated, not quiescent, CD4+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: Effective treatment of malaria can reverse increased viral load in individuals with HIV.
items.explanation: Treatment of malaria has been shown to reverse the increase in viral load in HIV-infected individuals.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.statement: Persistent immune activation in HIV is associated with increased secretion of proinflammatory cytokines.
items.explanation: The passage states that persistent immune activation leads to increased secretion of proinflammatory cytokines, particularly IL-6 and type I interferons.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.statement: Apoptosis in HIV infection is solely dependent on the stage of disease.
items.explanation: The intensity of apoptosis correlates with the general state of activation, not specifically with the stage of disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.statement: Autoimmune phenomena in HIV disease are exclusively associated with autoimmune diseases.
items.explanation: Autoimmune phenomena usually occur without full-blown autoimmune diseases.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.statement: The frequency of certain autoimmune diseases has decreased with the widespread use of ART.
items.explanation: Most autoimmune manifestations have decreased in frequency since the advent of ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: TNF-α and IL-6 levels are elevated only in the plasma of people with HIV.
items.explanation: Elevated levels of TNF-α and IL-6 have been demonstrated both in plasma and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of people with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: SDF-1 inhibits infection by R5 strains of HIV.
items.explanation: SDF-1 inhibits infection by X4 strains of HIV, not R5 strains.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.statement: The shift in cells from a quiescent pool to one with higher turnover is not associated with increased cell death.
items.explanation: A higher rate of turnover likely leads to an increased rate of cell death.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION
items.statement: Mathematical models suggest the lymphoid pool consists of dynamically distinct subpopulations affected by HIV.
items.explanation: Models indicate that the lymphoid pool is composed of dynamically distinct subpopulations differentially impacted by HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOCYTE TURNOVER IN HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: The transition from R5 to X4 viruses is associated with a rapid decline in CD4+ T cell counts and increased HIV plasma viremia.
items.explanation: Detection of X4 variants is associated with a relatively rapid decline in CD4+ T cell counts, increased HIV plasma viremia, and progression of disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: $\alpha4\beta7$ is essential for the binding and infection of a CD4+ T cell by HIV.
items.explanation: $\alpha4\beta7$ is not essential for the binding and infection of a CD4+ T cell by HIV, but it plays an important role in transmission at mucosal surfaces.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.statement: Thymic precursor cells express low levels of CD3, CD4, and CD8 molecules.
items.explanation: Thymic precursor cells were assumed to be negative for CD3, CD4, and CD8 molecules but actually express low levels of CD4.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.statement: CD34+ monocyte precursor cells require CD4-independent mechanisms for infection by HIV.
items.explanation: It is likely that CD34+ monocyte precursor cells express low levels of CD4, making CD4-independent mechanisms unnecessary to explain their infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.statement: Normalization of CD4+ T cells in the GALT is complete after many years of ART.
items.explanation: Even after many years of ART, normalization of the CD4+ T cells in the GALT remains incomplete.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.statement: The loss of polyfunctional HIV-specific CD4+ T cells, especially those producing IL-2, occurs early in the disease.
items.explanation: Loss of polyfunctional HIV-specific CD4+ T cells, particularly those that produce IL-2, occurs early in the course of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.statement: The increase in serum IL-7 levels during advanced stages of CD4+ T lymphopenia is solely a homeostatic response to lymphopenia.
items.explanation: Recent findings suggest that the increase in serum IL-7 results from reduced utilization due to loss of cells expressing the IL-7 receptor, not just as a homeostatic response.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.statement: The destruction of lymphoid precursor cells contributes to the failure of CD4+ T cell pool reconstitution in HIV.
items.explanation: Destruction of lymphoid precursor cells, including thymic and bone marrow progenitor cells, is a major mechanism contributing to inadequate reconstitution.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.statement: CD8+ T cells lacking CD28 expression are decreased in HIV disease.
items.explanation: CD8+ T cells lacking CD28 expression are increased, reflecting a skewed expansion of less differentiated subsets.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.statement: The presence of high levels of HIV viremia is associated with cellular functional abnormalities in CD8+ T cells.
items.explanation: High levels of HIV viremia are linked to aberrant immune activation and cellular functional abnormalities in CD8+ T cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.statement: Untransformed B cells can be infected with HIV.
items.explanation: Untransformed B cells cannot be infected with HIV, although they can be activated by HIV or its products.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.statement: People with HIV respond well to primary and secondary immunizations.
items.explanation: People with HIV respond poorly to primary and secondary immunizations due to B cell defects.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.statement: LPS binds preferentially to macrophages through CD14 and Toll-like receptors, leading to cellular activation.
items.explanation: LPS is a highly inflammatory bacterial product that binds to macrophages via CD14 and Toll-like receptors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.statement: Monocytes are easily demonstrated as being infected in vivo with HIV.
items.explanation: In vivo infection of circulating monocytes is difficult to demonstrate.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.statement: HIV productively infects NK cells in vivo.
items.explanation: There are no convincing data that HIV productively infects NK cells in vivo.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)
items.statement: NK cell impairments during chronic HIV infection include reduced cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion.
items.explanation: During chronic HIV infection, both NK cell cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion become impaired.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 6)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: The CCR5 Δ32 allele is common in populations outside of Europe.
items.explanation: The CCR5 Δ32 allele is rare in populations other than those of European ancestry.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.statement: Heterozygosity for the CCR5 Δ32 allele is associated with a lower risk of acquiring HIV.
items.explanation: Individuals heterozygous for the CCR5 Δ32 allele have a reduced risk of acquiring HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.statement: Homozygosity for the CCR5-HHE haplotype is associated with slower disease progression in African populations.
items.explanation: Homozygosity for the CCR5-HHE haplotype is associated with an increased risk of acquiring HIV and rapid progression to AIDS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.statement: The discovery of the CCR5 $ \Delta32/\Delta32$ genotype led to the development of entry inhibitors.
items.explanation: The discovery spurred the development of entry inhibitors like maraviroc.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.statement: The HLA-B–21M allele influences the expression of the HLA-B gene.
items.explanation: The HLA-B–21M allele does not influence HLA-B gene expression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.statement: GWAS have identified SNVs in the MHC that influence HIV viral load, including among individuals who control viral replication without ART.
items.explanation: GWAS have indeed identified SNVs in the MHC influencing HIV viral load, particularly among HIV controllers.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.statement: GWAS studies have primarily been conducted in African populations.
items.explanation: Most GWAS studies have been performed in European populations.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)
items.statement: Variations in host genes may explain about 29% of the observed variability in HIV viral load.
items.explanation: Variations in host genes may explain about 10% of the observed variability, while viral genetic diversity explains 29%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 4)

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.statement: The E4/E4 genotype of the ApoE gene is associated with a decreased risk of HAND in HIV-seropositive individuals.
items.explanation: The E4/E4 genotype is associated with increased risk of dementia and cognitive impairment.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.statement: A GWAS identified a polymorphism in the T cell receptor α locus on chromosome 14 that may influence neurocognitive outcomes.
items.explanation: The passage mentions this association with neurocognitive outcomes.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: HIV gp120 can cause neurotoxicity by antagonizing vasoactive intestinal peptide.
items.explanation: HIV gp120 shed by virus-infected monocytes may cause neurotoxicity by antagonizing the function of VIP.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: The main cell types infected in the brain are neurons and astrocytes.
items.explanation: The main cell types infected in the brain are perivascular macrophages and microglial cells, not neurons.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: ART leads to improvement in neuropsychiatric manifestations and a decrease in cytokine levels in CSF.
items.explanation: Treatment with ART improves neuropsychiatric symptoms and reduces cytokine levels in the CSF.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: The CNS is not considered a site for a reservoir of latently infected cells.
items.explanation: The CNS may serve as a sequestered site for a reservoir of latently infected cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · NEUROPATHOGENESIS IN HIV DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.statement: The occurrence of Kaposi's Sarcoma in people with HIV is strictly related to the level of depression of CD4+ T cell counts.
items.explanation: Unlike typical opportunistic infections, KS occurrence in HIV patients is not strictly related to CD4+ T cell count levels.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
items.statement: HHV-8 DNA sequences can be found in the B cells of all patients with AIDS.
items.explanation: HHV-8 DNA is found in the B cells of 30–50% of KS patients and 7% of AIDS patients without clinically apparent KS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · PATHOGENESIS OF KAPOSI'S SARCOMA

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.statement: The immune response to HIV includes both adaptive and innate immune responses.
items.explanation: The immune response involves elements of both adaptive and innate immunity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.statement: It is well understood which immunologic effector mechanisms are most important in delaying the progression of HIV infection.
items.explanation: It remains unclear which immunologic effector mechanisms are most important in delaying progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.statement: Antibodies to HIV are always detectable in all individuals after primary infection.
items.explanation: Rare exceptions exist where individuals have defects in the ability to produce HIV-specific antibodies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.statement: Antibodies capable of mediating ADCC are highest during the later stages of HIV infection.
items.explanation: The levels of anti-envelope antibodies capable of mediating ADCC are highest in the earlier stages of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.statement: Complement-opsonized HIV virions have decreased infectivity.
items.explanation: The passage suggests that complement-opsonized HIV virions may have increased infectivity.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.statement: There is a clear correlation between levels of HIV-specific CD4+ T lymphocytes and plasma HIV RNA levels.
items.explanation: The passage states that no clear correlations exist between these parameters.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.statement: ADCC involves the killing of HIV-expressing cells by NK cells armed with specific antibodies.
items.explanation: ADCC is described as involving NK cells and specific antibodies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.statement: The window period for detecting HIV infection with NAT is 22 days.
items.explanation: The window period for NAT is reduced to 12 days.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.statement: Antibodies to HIV generally appear in the circulation within 3–12 weeks following infection.
items.explanation: Antibodies to HIV typically become detectable in the blood between 3 and 12 weeks post-infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.statement: A negative result on a fourth-generation assay always rules out HIV infection.
items.explanation: A negative result does not rule out early HIV infection; retesting may be needed if there is strong reason to suspect recent exposure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.statement: The p24 antigen capture assay is commonly used as a stand-alone test for routine blood donor screening.
items.explanation: The use of the p24 antigen capture assay as a stand-alone test for routine blood donor screening has been replaced by fourth-generation assays.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.statement: HIV RNA levels can be used to monitor the response to antiretroviral therapy.
items.explanation: Measuring and monitoring levels of HIV RNA is valuable for assessing the response to antiretroviral therapies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.statement: RT-PCR can be used to amplify defined areas of the HIV genome for sequence analysis.
items.explanation: The passage states RT-PCR is useful for amplifying defined areas of the HIV genome.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)
items.statement: RT-PCR and DNA-PCR should be used as primary diagnostic tools in all patients.
items.explanation: These tests are recommended only when standard serologic testing fails to provide a definitive result.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.statement: CD4+ T cell counts are the best indicator of immunologic competence in patients with HIV infection.
items.explanation: The CD4+ T cell count is generally accepted as the best indicator of the immediate state of immunologic competence.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.statement: Patients with CD4+ T cell counts >500/μL require frequent monitoring.
items.explanation: For patients with CD4 counts >500/μL, the monitoring of the CD4 count is felt by many to be optional.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.statement: Resistance testing enhances the ability to decrease viral load by approximately 0.5 log when changing drugs based on drug history.
items.explanation: Resistance testing enhances viral load reduction by ~0.5 log compared with changing drugs merely based on drug history.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.statement: The Profile assay is a quick and inexpensive method to determine co-receptor tropism.
items.explanation: The Profile assay takes weeks to perform and is expensive.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.statement: The level of initial burst of viremia in acute HIV infection correlates with the subsequent course of disease.
items.explanation: There does not appear to be a correlation between the level of the initial burst of viremia and the subsequent course of disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.statement: ART has had a major impact on preventing and reversing the progression of disease in adequately treated patients.
items.explanation: Since the mid-1990s, ART has significantly impacted preventing and reversing disease progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.statement: Patients with low levels of HIV RNA progress more slowly to symptomatic disease.
items.explanation: Low levels of HIV RNA are associated with slower progression to symptomatic disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY
items.statement: The median time from initial infection to clinical disease in untreated patients is less than 5 years.
items.explanation: The median time for untreated patients is approximately 10 years.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ASYMPTOMATIC STAGE—CLINICAL LATENCY

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: The incidence of secondary infections in HIV-infected patients has increased due to ART.
items.explanation: Following the widespread use of ART, the incidence of secondary infections has decreased dramatically.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: Cardiovascular disease accounts for approximately 15% of deaths in HIV-infected patients.
items.explanation: Cardiovascular disease is responsible for about 15% of deaths among HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: The incidence of bacterial pneumonia is reduced by half when HIV-infected patients stop smoking.
items.explanation: Smoking cessation cuts the incidence of bacterial pneumonia in half.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.statement: A definitive diagnosis of PCP can be made with routine laboratory evaluations.
items.explanation: Routine laboratory evaluation is usually not helpful for diagnosing PCP; specific tests are required.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.statement: Primary or secondary prophylaxis for PCP can be discontinued in HIV patients with CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL and viral suppression <50 copies/mL for at least 6 months.
items.explanation: Prophylaxis can be discontinued if these criteria are maintained for at least 3 months, not 6.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.statement: Active TB is most common in patients with HIV infection who have CD4+ T cell counts >500/μL.
items.explanation: Active TB often develops relatively early in the course of HIV infection, not specifically when CD4+ T cell counts are high.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.statement: Anergy testing is valuable in diagnosing TB in patients with HIV.
items.explanation: Anergy testing is not of value in this setting.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.statement: Patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200 cells/μL should be retested for TB if their counts rise above 200.
items.explanation: Retesting is recommended when CD4+ T cell counts rise persistently above 200.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.statement: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infections are common in Africa.
items.explanation: The passage states that MAC infections are rare in Africa.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.statement: Primary prophylaxis for MAC is indicated in patients with HIV infection and CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that primary prophylaxis for MAC is recommended when CD4+ T cell counts are <50/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.statement: Nonspecific interstitial pneumonitis requires specific treatment beyond managing the underlying HIV infection.
items.explanation: No therapy is indicated for NIP other than appropriate management of the underlying HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.statement: HIV-associated cardiomyopathy can be directly related to myocarditis.
items.explanation: HIV-associated cardiomyopathy displays elements of myocarditis histologically.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.statement: Oral hairy leukoplakia is considered a premalignant condition.
items.explanation: Despite its name, oral hairy leukoplakia is not considered a premalignant condition.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.statement: Patients with HIV infection have an increased risk of infection with S. typhimurium.
items.explanation: Patients with untreated HIV have approximately a 20-fold increased risk of infection with S. typhimurium.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.statement: Cryptosporidial infection may cause biliary tract disease leading to cholecystitis in patients with HIV.
items.explanation: Cryptosporidia can lead to biliary tract disease, including cholecystitis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.statement: CMV colitis is now more common with the advent of ART.
items.explanation: CMV colitis is much less common with the advent of ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.statement: HIV enteropathy is diagnosed when stool examinations reveal pathogenic bacteria, mycobacteria, or protozoa.
items.explanation: A presumptive diagnosis of HIV enteropathy can be made if the diarrhea has persisted for >1 month and initial stool examinations are negative.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.statement: Hepatobiliary diseases in patients with HIV infection are often due to co-infection with hepatitis B or C.
items.explanation: Approximately 15% of deaths related to liver disease in HIV patients reflect problems encountered in the setting of co-infection with hepatitis B or C.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.statement: Hepatitis A virus infection is seen more frequently in patients with HIV.
items.explanation: Hepatitis A virus infection is not seen with an increased frequency in patients with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.statement: Successful treatment of HCV in patients with HIV decreases mortality.
items.explanation: Use of directly acting agents for the treatment of HCV leads to high cure rates and decreased mortality.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.statement: HIV-associated nephropathy is more prevalent in African-American and Hispanic populations.
items.explanation: More than 90% of reported cases have been in African-American or Hispanic individuals.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.statement: Edema and hypertension are common presentations of HIV-associated nephropathy.
items.explanation: Edema and hypertension are rare in HIV-associated nephropathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.statement: Neurosyphilis can present as acute meningitis, neuroretinitis, deafness, or stroke.
items.explanation: Neurosyphilis may present with symptoms such as acute meningitis, neuroretinitis, deafness, or stroke.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)
items.statement: The anti-fluorescent treponemal antibody test is always positive in patients with HIV and syphilis.
items.explanation: The anti-FTA test may be negative in the setting of immunodeficiency.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 12)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.statement: HIV-associated lipodystrophy is exclusively linked to protease inhibitor therapy.
items.explanation: Lipodystrophy can be induced by both protease inhibitors and protease-sparing regimens.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.statement: Hypothyroidism is the predominant thyroid abnormality in HIV-infected patients.
items.explanation: Subclinical hypothyroidism is the most common thyroid abnormality observed.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.statement: Patients with HIV have an increased incidence of systemic lupus erythematosus.
items.explanation: There is no evidence that people with HIV have an increase in systemic lupus erythematosus.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.statement: Reactive arthritis in patients with HIV generally responds well to standard treatment.
items.explanation: Reactive arthritis in people with HIV usually responds well to standard treatments.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.statement: Osteonecrosis in HIV patients treated with ART has been associated with the use of testosterone.
items.explanation: Osteonecrosis or avascular necrosis has been associated with testosterone among other factors.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.statement: IRIS is most commonly seen in patients starting ART therapy with CD4+ T cell counts >200 cells/μL.
items.explanation: IRIS is most common in patients starting therapy with CD4+ T cell counts <50 cells/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.statement: True autoimmune hemolytic anemia is common in patients with HIV infection.
items.explanation: True autoimmune hemolytic anemia is rare, although ~20% of patients may have a positive direct antiglobulin test due to polyclonal B cell activation.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.statement: Erythropoietin levels in HIV-infected patients with anemia are generally lower than expected given the degree of anemia.
items.explanation: Erythropoietin levels in patients with HIV infection and anemia are generally lower than expected, except in zidovudine-associated anemia where they may be high.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.statement: Thrombocytopenia in HIV patients is often a serious clinical problem.
items.explanation: Thrombocytopenia rarely presents as a serious clinical issue and generally responds well to ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.statement: Seborrheic dermatitis in HIV patients is aggravated by Pityrosporum infection.
items.explanation: Seborrheic dermatitis may be aggravated by concomitant infection with Pityrosporum.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.statement: Patients with HIV infection are more likely to develop AIDS if the outbreak of zoster is mild and limited.
items.explanation: AIDS is more likely to develop if the outbreak of zoster is associated with severe pain, extensive skin involvement, or specific dermatome involvement.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.statement: Patients with HIV infection tend to have recurrences of shingles with a relapse rate of ~20%.
items.explanation: Patients with HIV do indeed have a higher recurrence rate of shingles, around 20%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.statement: Aseptic meningitis is more common after the development of AIDS.
items.explanation: Aseptic meningitis is rare following the development of AIDS, suggesting it is an immune-mediated disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.statement: Cryptococcal meningitis occurs primarily in patients with CD4+ T cell counts >200/μL.
items.explanation: Cryptococcal meningitis generally occurs in patients with CD4+ T cell counts <100/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.statement: HIV-associated dementia is commonly the initial AIDS-defining illness.
items.explanation: HIV-associated dementia is the initial AIDS-defining illness in ~3% of patients, thus only rarely precedes clinical evidence of immunodeficiency.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.statement: Aphasia and apraxia are common features of HIV-associated dementia.
items.explanation: In contrast to 'cortical' dementia, aphasia, apraxia, and agnosia are uncommon in HIV encephalopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.statement: HIV RNA detection in the spinal fluid is specific for HIV encephalopathy.
items.explanation: Detection of HIV RNA in the spinal fluid is not specific for HIV encephalopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.statement: Combination antiretroviral therapy has been shown to improve neuropsychiatric test scores in patients with HIV-associated dementia.
items.explanation: Improvement in neuropsychiatric test scores is noted with combination antiretroviral therapy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.statement: The incidence of toxoplasmosis as a secondary CNS infection is increasing due to ART.
items.explanation: The incidence of toxoplasmosis is decreasing in the era of ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.statement: Secondary prophylaxis for toxoplasmosis can be discontinued if CD4+ T cell counts rise above 200/μL for 6 months.
items.explanation: Secondary prophylaxis may be stopped with effective ART and sustained increases in CD4+ T cell counts to >200/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.statement: Headache and fever are common symptoms of PML.
items.explanation: Headache, fever, nausea, and vomiting are rarely seen in patients with PML.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.statement: Reactivation American trypanosomiasis is considered an AIDS-defining condition in South America.
items.explanation: In South America, reactivation of Chagas' disease is considered to be an AIDS-defining condition.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.statement: Most cases of myelopathy seen in patients with HIV infection are due to vitamin B12 deficiency.
items.explanation: Although vitamin B12 deficiency can be seen, it is not responsible for most cases of myelopathy in HIV-infected patients.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
