items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.statement: Spinal cord syndromes in HIV infection respond well to antiretroviral drugs.
items.explanation: Spinal cord syndromes do not respond well to antiretroviral drugs; therapy is mainly supportive.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.statement: CMV retinitis is typically associated with visual loss.
items.explanation: CMV retinitis usually presents as a painless, progressive loss of vision.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.statement: Cotton-wool spots are associated with visual loss.
items.explanation: Unlike CMV retinitis, cotton-wool spots do not cause visual loss and tend to remain stable or improve over time.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.statement: Histoplasmosis is an AIDS-defining condition only when it presents as a primary infection of the lung.
items.explanation: Disseminated histoplasmosis, not just primary pulmonary infection, can be an initial AIDS-defining condition.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.statement: Bacillary angiomatosis lesions are typically painless and do not occur with systemic symptoms.
items.explanation: The lesions of bacillary angiomatosis generally branch, are painful, and typically occur in the setting of systemic symptoms.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.statement: Antibody titers are highly useful for diagnosing visceral leishmaniasis in patients with HIV infection.
items.explanation: Antibody titers are of little help in diagnosing visceral leishmaniasis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.statement: ART has significantly reduced the incidence of HPV-associated malignancies.
items.explanation: ART has had little effect on human papillomavirus (HPV)-associated malignancies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.statement: Lymph node involvement in Kaposi's sarcoma implies a poor prognosis.
items.explanation: In KS, lymph node involvement may be seen early and is of no special clinical significance.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.statement: Effective ART can lead to the spontaneous regression of Kaposi's sarcoma lesions.
items.explanation: ART has been associated with the spontaneous regression of KS lesions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.statement: The incidence of AIDS-associated systemic lymphomas has decreased dramatically due to the widespread use of effective ART.
items.explanation: The incidence of AIDS-associated systemic lymphomas has not experienced a dramatic decrease as a consequence of the widespread use of effective ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.statement: Liposomal daunorubicin and pomalidomide are approved for patients who have failed standard chemotherapy.
items.explanation: Liposomal daunorubicin and pomalidomide are approved as first-line therapy for patients with advanced KS despite ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.statement: Primary CNS lymphomas are usually positive for EBV.
items.explanation: In contrast to HIV-associated Burkitt's lymphoma, primary CNS lymphomas are usually positive for EBV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.statement: Systemic lymphoma generally presents at a later stage of HIV infection than primary CNS lymphoma.
items.explanation: Systemic lymphoma is seen at earlier stages of HIV infection compared to primary CNS lymphoma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.statement: ART has decreased the incidence of Multicentric Castleman's disease.
items.explanation: The incidence of Multicentric Castleman's disease has increased in the setting of ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.statement: HPV-associated dysplasia is less common in people with HIV compared to the general population.
items.explanation: Infection with HPV, associated with intraepithelial dysplasia, is approximately twice as common in people with HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.statement: Approximately half of the patients with ICL are women.
items.explanation: The passage states that approximately half of the reported ICL patients were women.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.statement: Virologic studies have identified a transmissible microbe responsible for ICL.
items.explanation: No evidence of any transmissible microbe was found in virologic studies of patients with ICL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.statement: A diagnosis of HIV infection should be equated with an inevitably fatal disease.
items.explanation: With appropriate ART, a diagnosis of HIV is no longer considered inevitably fatal.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.statement: HIV can be transmitted even when viral levels are undetectable.
items.explanation: 'Undetectable' virus levels refer to assay sensitivity, not the absence of transmissibility.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.statement: HIV medicine is a static field with little change over time.
items.explanation: The field of HIV medicine is changing rapidly.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)
items.statement: Patients should be counseled about the importance of informing people who may also be infected.
items.explanation: Counseling should be offered to people whom the patient knows, or suspects, also may be infected.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.statement: ART should be initiated immediately following a diagnosis of HIV infection in all settings.
items.explanation: ART initiation may be delayed in specific cases like cryptococcal meningitis or TB to reduce severe IRIS risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.statement: Drug resistance testing is recommended before initiating therapy and when considering a change due to treatment failure.
items.explanation: Testing helps guide the selection of effective drugs in combination regimens.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.statement: Ritonavir is used as a pharmacokinetic enhancer due to its high affinity for cytochrome P450 enzymes.
items.explanation: Ritonavir increases the plasma concentrations of co-administered drugs metabolized by cytochrome P450.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.statement: Nucleoside analogues are selective for HIV-1 reverse transcriptase and do not affect other DNA polymerases.
items.explanation: Nucleoside analogues inhibit a variety of DNA polymerases, not just those of the HIV-1 reverse transcriptase.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.statement: ART leads to the eradication or cure of HIV in most patients.
items.explanation: ART does not lead to eradication or cure, except in rare cases.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.statement: Intermittent ART regimens are associated with fewer serious adverse events.
items.explanation: Intermittent therapy has been linked to an increase in serious adverse events.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.statement: HIV is capable of rapidly developing resistance to any single agent.
items.explanation: Therapy must be given as a multidrug combination due to rapid development of resistance.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.statement: The same principles apply to children and adults without any unique considerations.
items.explanation: Treatment of HIV-infected children involves unique pharmacologic, virologic, and immunologic considerations.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.statement: Drug toxicity will usually begin to show signs of reversal within 1–2 weeks.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that drug toxicity will usually begin to show signs of reversal within 1–2 weeks.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.statement: Resistance testing should be conducted after the patient has been switched from a failing regimen.
items.explanation: The passage recommends that resistance testing in the setting of drug failure be conducted while the patient is still on the failing regimen.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.statement: The risk of HIV transmission after nonintact skin exposure has been precisely quantified.
items.explanation: The average risk for transmission by this route has not been precisely quantified.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.statement: Most cases of health care worker seroconversion have occurred as a result of needle-stick injuries.
items.explanation: Most cases of health care worker seroconversion have indeed resulted from needle-stick injuries.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.statement: The risk of HBV infection following a needle-stick injury from an HBsAg-positive patient is lower than the risk of HIV infection.
items.explanation: The risk of HBV infection following a needle-stick injury from an HBsAg-positive patient is much higher than the risk of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)
items.statement: Healthcare workers with pre-existing HIV infection are at increased risk when dealing with drug-resistant TB strains.
items.explanation: The emergence of drug-resistant organisms, including extensively drug-resistant TB strains, has made TB an increasingly important problem for healthcare workers, particularly those with pre-existing HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.statement: Petroleum-based gels are safe for use with latex condoms.
items.explanation: Petroleum-based gels should never be used for lubrication of the condom, as they increase the likelihood of rupture.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.statement: The HPTN 052 trial showed that ART can reduce HIV transmission risk by up to 96%.
items.explanation: The HPTN 052 clinical trial demonstrated a 96% reduction in HIV transmission risk among heterosexual HIV-discordant couples where the partner with HIV started ART immediately.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.statement: Needle exchange programs have been successful in decreasing HIV transmission among injection drug users without increasing the use of injection drugs.
items.explanation: Needle exchange programs are noted to decrease HIV transmission without increasing drug use.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 2)
items.statement: Lenacapavir injections have been studied only in cisgender women for PrEP.
items.explanation: Studies of lenacapavir for PrEP are being conducted in various populations, not just cisgender women.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.statement: An effective HIV vaccine is available for preventing infection.
items.explanation: There is currently no safe and effective vaccine approved for the prevention of HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.statement: The natural immune response to HIV can usually clear the virus from the body.
items.explanation: The natural immune response to HIV is unable to clear the virus from the body.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.assertion: HIV-1 is the most common cause of HIV disease worldwide.
items.reason: HIV-1 comprises several subtypes with different geographic distributions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT
items.assertion: HIV-2 viruses have caused widespread epidemics globally.
items.reason: HIV-2 was originally confined to West Africa but has been identified worldwide.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; HIV-2 has not caused widespread global epidemics. The reason is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease: AIDS and Related Disorders | DEFINITION | ETIOLOGIC AGENT

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV proviral DNA integrates into regions of active transcription within the host genome.
items.reason: This integration preference facilitates efficient viral gene expression and replication.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HIV prefers integrating into actively transcribed regions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)
items.assertion: TRIM5α causes premature disassembly of retroviral capsids.
items.reason: This action is part of the innate immune response to block infection progression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains TRIM5α's role in preventing HIV infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MORPHOLOGY OF HIV | REPLICATION CYCLE OF HIV (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV can evolve through recombination.
items.reason: Recombination occurs when an individual is infected with viruses of more than one subtype, particularly in areas where subtypes overlap.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HIV evolves through recombination.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)
items.assertion: The reverse transcriptase of HIV is highly variable.
items.reason: Reverse transcriptase has important enzymatic functions and is under immune selective pressure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reverse transcriptase is relatively conserved in the absence of drug-induced selective pressure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION
items.assertion: HIV's molecular heterogeneity complicates the development of a universally effective vaccine.
items.reason: A broadly useful vaccine must induce protective responses against multiple viral strains.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HIV's diversity is an obstacle to vaccine development.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · MOLECULAR HETEROGENEITY OF HIV-1 (part 2) | TRANSMISSION

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV can be transmitted through both direct inoculation and infection of target cells during anal intercourse.
items.reason: Anal intercourse provides at least two modalities of infection: direct inoculation into blood in cases of traumatic tears, and infection of susceptible target cells like Langerhans cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)
items.assertion: Anti-HSV therapy reduces HIV acquisition by healing genital ulcers.
items.reason: HIV receptor-positive inflammatory cells persist in genital tissue despite ulcer healing, maintaining susceptible targets for HIV.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because anti-HSV therapy does not reduce HIV acquisition; the reason explains why.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 1)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Male circumcision reduces the risk of HIV acquisition.
items.reason: Circumcision decreases susceptibility to ulcerative STIs and other factors that increase HIV target cells in the foreskin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why circumcision reduces HIV risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)
items.assertion: Oral contraceptives increase the incidence of HIV infection.
items.reason: Drug-induced changes in cervical mucosa make it more vulnerable to viral penetration.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason explaining how oral contraceptives may increase HIV risk.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SEXUAL TRANSMISSION (part 2)

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.assertion: The use of injection drugs in a geographic location with high HIV prevalence increases the risk of infection.
items.reason: Higher local prevalence of HIV increases exposure to contaminated paraphernalia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS
items.assertion: The procedures involved in processing certain blood products do not affect HIV transmission.
items.reason: These procedures either inactivate or remove the virus, preventing transmission.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these procedures do prevent HIV transmission by inactivating or removing the virus.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TRANSMISSION THROUGH INJECTION DRUG USE | TRANSMISSION BY TRANSFUSED BLOOD AND BLOOD PRODUCTS

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.assertion: Health care workers should receive postexposure prophylaxis after a percutaneous injury involving blood from an HIV-infected patient.
items.reason: Postexposure prophylaxis with combination antiretroviral therapy has reduced the occurrence of puncture-related transmissions of HIV to health care workers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)
items.assertion: The risk of HIV transmission from health care workers to patients is high in developed countries.
items.reason: Several retrospective epidemiologic studies have shown no cases of transmission linked to healthcare providers other than documented instances.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.assertion: The use of ART during pregnancy significantly reduces the risk of mother-to-child transmission of HIV.
items.reason: ART lowers maternal plasma viremia levels, which is a major factor in reducing transmission risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV
items.assertion: Breast-feeding by an untreated mother with HIV is recommended in developed countries.
items.reason: Alternative forms of adequate nutrition are readily available in these settings.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as breast-feeding by a mother with HIV is contraindicated in developed countries.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · OCCUPATIONAL TRANSMISSION OF HIV: HEALTH CARE WORKERS, LABORATORY WORKERS, AND THE HEALTH CARE SETTING (part 2) | MOTHER-TO-CHILD TRANSMISSION OF HIV

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.assertion: The HIV epidemic has occurred in 'waves' with different characteristics across regions.
items.reason: Each wave's characteristics depend on the demographics and timing of HIV introduction into the population.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)
items.assertion: The Eastern and Southern Africa region has a lower HIV prevalence compared to other regions.
items.reason: This region accounts for more than half of the people with HIV worldwide.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; the region actually has the highest prevalence.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.assertion: The HIV epidemic is expanding in Eastern Europe and Central Asia.
items.reason: There was a 20% increase in annual new HIV infections between 2010 and 2023.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the expansion of the epidemic.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION AND AIDS WORLDWIDE (part 2)
items.assertion: Brazil has the largest number of HIV-infected persons in Latin America.
items.reason: Latin America reported an increase in new infections by 9% between 2010 and 2023.
