items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.assertion: The rate of death due to HIV disease in the United States has decreased since 2010.
items.reason: This trend is likely due to improved prophylaxis and treatment of opportunistic infections, growing experience among health professionals, and increased use of antiretroviral therapy (ART).
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why the rate of death has decreased.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES
items.assertion: The burden of HIV infection is evenly distributed across states and regions in the United States.
items.reason: In most areas, HIV is concentrated in urban areas, with larger percentages of diagnoses in smaller metropolitan and nonmetropolitan areas in the southern U.S.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the burden of HIV infection is not evenly distributed; it is more concentrated in certain areas.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV INFECTION IN THE UNITED STATES

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.assertion: The virus is not detected in plasma during the initial days after exposure.
items.reason: This period, known as the 'eclipse' phase, occurs because the virus replicates initially within lymphoid tissues before being disseminated to the bloodstream.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the virus is not detected in plasma during this period.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)
items.assertion: Activated CD4+ T cells are less abundant than resting CD4+ T cells.
items.reason: Activated CD4+ T cells support productive infection and generate larger amounts of virus.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason does not explain why activated CD4+ T cells are less abundant.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV infection is unique because it establishes a chronic, persistent infection.
items.reason: The virus thrives on immune activation and escapes complete immune-mediated clearance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why HIV establishes a chronic infection.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)
items.assertion: The peak level of plasma viremia determines the rate of disease progression in HIV.
items.reason: The set point of steady-state plasma viremia correlates with disease progression.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; it's the set point, not the peak level, that correlates with disease progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · EARLY EVENTS IN HIV INFECTION: PRIMARY INFECTION AND INITIAL DISSEMINATION OF VIRUS (part 2) | ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.assertion: The humoral immune system does not readily produce classic neutralizing antibodies against the HIV envelope.
items.reason: This is due to the high rate of virus replication and mutation, which hinders antibody production.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason explains why the humoral immune system struggles with neutralizing antibodies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)
items.assertion: Viral escape from CD8+ CTLs is solely due to high mutation rates.
items.reason: Escape also involves immune exhaustion and downregulation of HLA class I molecules.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as it overlooks other mechanisms like immune exhaustion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.assertion: The persistent reservoir of HIV-infected cells is a major obstacle to eradicating the virus.
items.reason: This reservoir can be replenished by low levels of persistent virus replication and proliferation of latently infected cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)
items.assertion: Preintegration latency can last for several years.
items.reason: The proviral DNA in preintegration latency integrates into the host genome.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as preintegration latency lasts hours to days, and the reason is also false.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ESTABLISHMENT OF CHRONIC INFECTION (part 3)

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.assertion: Elite controllers have a robust and superior HIV-specific immune response.
items.reason: Their HIV-specific CD8+ CTLs can effectively clear infected CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: The reason given correctly explains why elite controllers have a superior immune response.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS
items.assertion: All long-term nonprogressors require antiretroviral therapy.
items.reason: This group gradually progresses in their degree of immune deficiency over time.
items.explanation: Not all long-term nonprogressors require ART as they maintain stable CD4+ T cell counts and low viremia without it.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ADVANCED HIV DISEASE | LONG-TERM SURVIVORS, LONG-TERM NONPROGRESSORS, AND ELITE CONTROLLERS

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: During early stages of HIV disease, lymphoid tissue architecture is generally preserved.
items.reason: This preservation is due to increased presence of B cells and follicular helper CD4+ T cells in germinal centers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the increased presence contributes to hyperplasia.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Persistent cellular activation in lymphoid tissues diminishes immune response effectiveness against HIV.
items.reason: This is due to a shift towards secretion of proinflammatory cytokines like IL-6 and TNF-alpha.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; proinflammatory cytokines induce viral replication.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LYMPHOID ORGANS AND HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: Persistent immune activation contributes to the pathogenesis of HIV.
items.reason: Immune activation and inflammation provide substrates necessary for persistent virus replication.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV replication is reduced to zero in patients receiving ART.
items.reason: ART suppresses plasma viremia to <20 copies per milliliter.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because low-level persistent virus replication can still occur even with ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.assertion: Chronic immune activation contributes to immune dysfunction in HIV.
items.reason: Persistent antigen exposure leads to functional exhaustion of virus-specific T cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how chronic immune activation contributes to immune dysfunction.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.assertion: Pyroptosis is a non-inflammatory form of cell death.
items.reason: It involves the release of proinflammatory cytokines IL-1β and IL-18.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because pyroptosis is an inflammatory form of cell death, as indicated by the involvement of IL-1β and IL-18.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.assertion: IRIS is associated with a decrease in viral load and partial recovery of immune competence.
items.reason: IRIS occurs due to an increase in immune response against residual antigens, often microbial.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the occurrence of IRIS.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF IMMUNE ACTIVATION AND INFLAMMATION IN HIV PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: IL-18 is involved in the development of HIV-associated lipodystrophy syndrome.
items.reason: IL-18 has been shown to play a role in the development of the HIV-associated lipodystrophy syndrome.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: Cytokines like IFN-α induce immune activation that leads to enhanced viral production.
items.reason: IFN-α is one of the first cytokines induced during primary HIV infection and plays a role in innate immune sensing.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why IFN-α induces immune activation leading to enhanced viral production.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CYTOKINES AND OTHER SOLUBLE FACTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: R5 viruses are more efficient in infecting monocytes/macrophages and microglial cells.
items.reason: This efficiency is due to the ability of R5 viruses to utilize CCR5 as a co-receptor, which is abundant on these cell types.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why R5 viruses efficiently infect monocytes/macrophages and microglial cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS
items.assertion: The transition from R5 to X4 virus predominance occurs in all individuals infected with subtype B.
items.reason: This transition is often preceded by dual R5X4 strains and associated with rapid disease progression.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the transition does not occur in all individuals; only about half of those infected with subtype B experience this change.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · THE ROLE OF VIRAL RECEPTORS AND CO-RECEPTORS IN HIV PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV
items.assertion: Infection and depletion of thymic epithelial cells contribute to the impaired ability of the CD4+ T cell pool to reconstitute itself.
items.reason: Thymic epithelial cells may play a role in the normal regeneration of CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR TARGETS OF HIV

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.assertion: T-reg cells correlate with lower viral loads and higher CD4+/CD8+ T cell ratios.
items.reason: A subset of CD4+ T cells referred to as T-regulatory cells may be involved in damping aberrant immune activation that propagates HIV replication.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why T-reg cells correlate with lower viral loads.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)
items.assertion: The increase in TF_H cell numbers is beneficial to the quality of humoral immune response against HIV.
items.reason: TF_H cells are highly susceptible to HIV infection and their number increases in lymph nodes of people with HIV, especially those who are viremic.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the increase in TF_H cell numbers is likely detrimental to the quality of humoral immune response against HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 1)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)
items.assertion: The inability of the immune system to regenerate CD4+ T cells efficiently is partly due to HIV-mediated attrition.
items.reason: HIV infection leads to both direct and indirect effects on CD4+ T cell function and survival.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion regarding CD4+ T cell regeneration challenges.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 2)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.assertion: CD8+ T cells secrete factors that inhibit HIV replication.
items.reason: These include CC-chemokines like RANTES, MIP-1α, and MIP-1β.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)
items.assertion: The functional capability of CD8+ T cells increases as HIV disease progresses.
items.reason: This is due to the persistent nature of HIV infection causing functional exhaustion via upregulation of inhibitory receptors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the functional capability decreases, while the reason correctly describes a contributing factor.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 3)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.assertion: B cells from people with HIV manifest increased secretion of immunoglobulins.
items.reason: This reflects their highly differentiated state in vivo.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)
items.assertion: The absolute number of circulating B cells may be depressed in HIV infection.
items.reason: This is due to decreased activation-induced apoptosis and redistribution into lymphoid tissue.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; it is increased activation-induced apoptosis and redistribution that contribute to depression of circulating B cells.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 4)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.assertion: Monocytes express CD4 and co-receptors for HIV, making them potential targets of infection.
items.reason: In vivo infection of circulating monocytes is difficult to demonstrate.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why monocytes are potential targets.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)
items.assertion: Dendritic cells play a role in HIV infection initiation by presenting intact virus to CD4+ T cell targets.
items.reason: DC-SIGN retains HIV in early endosomal compartments, facilitating presentation to CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the role of DC-SIGN.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · QUALITATIVE AND QUANTITATIVE ABNORMALITIES OF MONONUCLEAR CELLS (part 5)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: The CCR5 Δ32 allele results in a truncated protein that is not expressed on the cell surface.
items.reason: This lack of expression confers resistance to R5 strains of HIV.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the CCR5 Δ32 allele results in resistance to HIV.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)
items.assertion: The major histocompatibility locus on chromosome 6 is associated with consistent effects on HIV acquisition.
items.reason: It encodes the HIV co-receptor CCR5.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because CCR5 is located on chromosome 3, not in the major histocompatibility locus on chromosome 6.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.assertion: The CCR5-HHA haplotype is associated with slower disease progression in African populations.
items.reason: The ancestral HHA haplotype is more common in persons of African ancestry and is linked to slower disease progression.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)
items.assertion: The CCR5-HHE haplotype is associated with resistance to HIV infection.
items.reason: Homozygosity for the CCR5-HHE haplotype increases susceptibility to HIV and rapid progression to AIDS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 2)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.assertion: The HLA-B $ ^{57} $ and HLA-B $ ^{27} $ alleles are associated with slower progression of HIV.
items.reason: These alleles are linked to a lower virologic set point and higher cell-mediated immunity in people with HIV.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why these alleles are associated with slower progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)
items.assertion: The HLA-B $ ^{35} $ allele is protective against rapid progression to AIDS.
items.reason: This allele is associated with faster progression and higher viral load.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the HLA-B $ ^{35} $ allele is detrimental, not protective.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC FACTORS IN HIV-1 AND AIDS PATHOGENESIS (part 3)

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.assertion: Variations in the MYH9 and APOL1 genes are associated with a higher risk of HIV-associated nephropathy.
items.reason: These gene polymorphisms explain nearly 35% of the susceptibility to HIVAN in persons of African descent.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS
items.assertion: The HLA-B*57:01 allele is associated with a faster progression to AIDS.
items.reason: HLA-B*57:01 carriers have a slower HIV disease course.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the HLA-B*57:01 allele is associated with a slower HIV disease course, not faster.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENETIC ASSOCIATIONS WITH SPECIFIC AIDS AND NON-AIDS CONDITIONS

items.subtopic: IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV
items.assertion: CD4+ T cells specific for HIV are likely to be early targets for productive infection.
items.reason: These CD4+ T cells are activated, making them susceptible to HIV infection and associated dysfunction.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why these CD4+ T cells serve as early targets.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IMMUNE RESPONSE TO HIV

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.assertion: Antibodies directed to gp41 can facilitate HIV cell infection.
items.reason: These antibodies, when present in low titer, are capable of facilitating infection through an Fc receptor-mediated mechanism known as antibody enhancement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.assertion: ADCC-mediated killing of HIV-infected cells can also kill uninfected CD4+ T cells.
items.reason: Anti-gp120 antibodies might kill uninfected CD4+ T cells if they have bound free gp120, a phenomenon called bystander killing.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.assertion: CD8+ T cells in HIV-1 infection can be induced to express cytokines such as IFN-$\gamma$.
items.reason: These CD8+ T cells are driven to in vivo expansion by HIV antigen.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why CD8+ T cells express cytokines.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)
items.assertion: CD4+ T cells undergo clonal expansions in response to HIV antigens.
items.reason: This allows them to survive despite the virus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason explaining why CD4+ T cells can survive.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HUMORAL IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION
items.assertion: Sensitive assays for plasma viremia allow closer monitoring of HIV disease progression.
items.reason: These tests, along with CD4+ T lymphocyte levels, are crucial in managing HIV infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · CELLULAR IMMUNE RESPONSE (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS AND LABORATORY MONITORING OF HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.assertion: A positive fourth-generation assay should always be confirmed with a more specific test.
items.reason: The specificity of the fourth-generation assay is not optimal, especially in low-risk populations.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why confirmation is necessary.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.assertion: The Western blot is still used as a confirmatory test for HIV.
items.reason: Western blot has historically been used to confirm HIV diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the Western blot is no longer used; however, the reason is true regarding its historical use.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.assertion: The p24 antigen capture assay detects viral protein p24 in blood.
items.reason: It consists of antibodies to the p24 antigen on a solid phase, detecting either free antigen or complexed with anti-p24 antibodies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the assay detects p24.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.assertion: DNA PCR tests for HIV can detect proviral DNA at a frequency of one copy per cell.
items.reason: These tests are employed by research laboratories to amplify HIV proviral DNA from peripheral blood mononuclear cells.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because DNA PCR tests detect proviral DNA at a frequency of one copy per 10,000–100,000 cells, not per cell.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · DIAGNOSIS OF HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.assertion: CD4+ T cell percentage may be more reliable than CD4+ T cell count in patients with hypersplenism.
items.reason: In these patients, the absolute number of CD4+ T cells might be reduced due to splenic sequestration.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV RNA levels below detection limits imply no viral presence.
items.reason: Sensitive techniques can detect as few as one copy per milliliter of plasma.
items.explanation: A is true, but R is false; 'undetectable' does not mean the virus level is zero.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.assertion: Maraviroc is effective against CXCR4-tropic viruses.
items.reason: Maraviroc is a CCR5 antagonist and works only against CCR5-tropic viruses.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; maraviroc is not effective against CXCR4-tropic viruses.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)
items.assertion: Genotypic assays are used to determine the sensitivity of HIV viral quasispecies to antiretroviral agents.
items.reason: Genotypic assays involve sequence analysis compared with known resistance profiles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 2)

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.assertion: Opportunistic infections can occur during the acute stage of HIV infection.
items.reason: This is due to immunodeficiency resulting from reduced numbers and dysfunction of CD4+ T cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION
items.assertion: The acute HIV syndrome is typical of an acute bacterial infection.
items.reason: It has been likened to acute infectious mononucleosis, which is a viral syndrome.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the acute HIV syndrome is likened to a viral syndrome, not a bacterial one.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · LABORATORY MONITORING OF PATIENTS WITH HIV INFECTION (part 3) | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | ACUTE HIV INFECTION

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Patients with low CD4+ T cell counts are prone to mucormycosis infections.
items.reason: Mucormycosis of the sinuses in patients with HIV infection may progress more slowly compared to other patient populations.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why low CD4+ T cell counts lead to mucormycosis infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Non-AIDS-related illnesses now dominate the disease burden in HIV patients treated with ART.
items.reason: The widespread use of ART has led to a decrease in AIDS-defining illnesses.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason explains why non-AIDS events have become more prevalent.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: The risk of pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) is highest in patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL.
items.reason: The majority of PCP cases occur when CD4+ T cell counts are below this threshold.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)
items.assertion: Patients with PCP typically present with a productive cough.
items.reason: Chest x-ray findings in PCP include dense perihilar infiltrates.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as patients generally have a nonproductive or scant sputum-producing cough; the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 2)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: Untreated TB can accelerate the course of HIV infection.
items.reason: Levels of plasma HIV RNA increase in the setting of active TB and decline with successful TB treatment.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains why untreated TB accelerates HIV progression.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)
items.assertion: In patients with lower CD4+ T cell counts, pulmonary reactivation is more common than disseminated disease.
items.reason: Patients with low CD4+ T cell counts are more susceptible to disseminated forms of TB.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; in patients with lower CD4+ T cell counts, disseminated disease is more common.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 3)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.assertion: Initiation of ART may lead to immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) in patients with HIV.
items.reason: IRIS is most common when both ART and anti-TB therapy are initiated simultaneously.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)
items.assertion: Drug susceptibility testing is unnecessary for TB treatment in HIV patients.
items.reason: Due to the possibility of multidrug-resistant or extensively drug-resistant TB, drug susceptibility testing should be performed.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; drug susceptibility testing is necessary.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 4)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.assertion: Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infections can be diagnosed by the culture of blood or involved tissue.
items.reason: Cultures for MAC may take up to 2 weeks to turn positive.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why the diagnosis is made by culturing.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)
items.assertion: Invasive aspergillosis is commonly seen in patients with AIDS.
items.reason: Aspergillus infection may occur in patients with AIDS who have neutropenia or are on glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because invasive aspergillosis is not generally seen in patients with AIDS unless they have neutropenia or are on glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 5)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.assertion: The rate of cardiovascular disease events is lower in patients on antiretroviral therapy.
items.reason: There is a clear association between HIV replication and the risk of cardiovascular disease.
items.explanation: Antiretroviral therapy reduces HIV replication, which is associated with a decreased risk of cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)
items.assertion: HIV can be directly demonstrated in cardiac tissue in cases of HIV-associated cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: This supports the hypothesis that HIV plays a direct role in this condition.
items.explanation: While HIV is found in cardiac tissue, there is debate over whether it plays a direct role in HIV-associated cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 6)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.assertion: Thrush is indicative of advanced immunologic decline in HIV patients.
items.reason: Thrush generally occurs in patients with CD4+ T cell counts of <300/μL.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)
items.assertion: Thalidomide is used to treat aphthous ulcers in HIV patients.
items.reason: The pathogenesis of aphthous ulcers may involve tissue-destructive cytokines.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 7)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.assertion: Microsporidia can be found in extraintestinal locations.
items.reason: Microsporidia have been noted in a variety of extraintestinal locations, including the eye and brain.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)
items.assertion: Cryptosporidial diarrhea can be effectively treated with nitazoxanide.
items.reason: Nitazoxanide treatment results in improvement for about half of the patients.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A as its overall role remains unclear.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 8)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)
items.assertion: HIV enteropathy is a direct result of HIV infection in the GI tract.
items.reason: It improves with ART and involves low-grade mucosal atrophy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains that HIV enteropathy results from HIV infection in the GI tract.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 9)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.assertion: HIV co-infection with HBV is associated with decreased evidence of inflammatory liver disease.
items.reason: This is due to the immunosuppressive effects of HIV infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)
items.assertion: Hepatitis G virus co-infection with HIV leads to a higher rate of progression to AIDS.
items.reason: Patients with HIV infection co-infected with hepatitis G virus have a decreased rate of progression to AIDS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 10)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.assertion: Switching from tenofovir disoproxil fumarate to tenofovir alafenamide may decrease renal injury.
items.reason: Tenofovir alafenamide has a different pharmacokinetic profile that results in less renal exposure.
items.explanation: Switching from TDF to TAF can lead to decreased renal injury due to reduced renal exposure.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)
items.assertion: TMP-SMX causes a true decline in glomerular filtration rate.
items.reason: TMP-SMX competes for tubular secretion with creatinine, causing an increase in serum creatinine levels.
items.explanation: TMP-SMX does not cause a true decline in GFR; it only increases serum creatinine by competing for tubular secretion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 11)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.assertion: HIV infection can lead to both hypo- and hyperthyroidism.
items.reason: Thyroid function may be altered in 10–15% of patients with HIV infection, including subclinical hypothyroidism.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)
items.assertion: Hypogonadism in HIV-infected men is highest when ART is administered.
items.reason: Hypogonadism is associated with underlying illness and can be a side effect of ganciclovir therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; hypogonadism is generally lowest in the setting of ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 13)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.assertion: HIV infection may ameliorate systemic lupus erythematosus and rheumatoid arthritis.
items.reason: An intact CD4+ T cell limb of the immune response plays an integral role in the pathogenesis of these conditions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)
items.assertion: Methotrexate is a safe treatment for reactive arthritis in HIV patients.
items.reason: Therapy with methotrexate has been associated with an increase in the incidence of opportunistic infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because methotrexate should be used cautiously due to increased risk of opportunistic infections.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 14)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.assertion: Osteoporosis has been reported in a significant percentage of women with HIV infection.
items.reason: Several studies have documented decreases in bone mineral density following the initiation of ART.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the decrease in bone mineral density is not the direct reason for osteoporosis reporting.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)
items.assertion: IRIS can lead to fatal outcomes.
items.reason: The underlying mechanism of IRIS involves type IV hypersensitivity reactions.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not directly explain why IRIS can be fatal.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 15)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.assertion: In patients with HIV infection and CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL, lymph node biopsy is indicated.
items.reason: Lymph node biopsy is not indicated in early-stage disease unless there are signs of systemic illness or changes in the nodes.
items.explanation: The assertion is true; however, the reason provided does not apply to patients with CD4+ T cell counts <200/μL.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)
items.assertion: Monoclonal gammopathy of unknown significance (MGUS) is associated with other viral infections and malignancies.
items.reason: MGUS has been reported in 3% of patients with HIV infection, indicating a potential association.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 16)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.assertion: Splenectomy makes CD4+ T cell counts unreliable markers of immunocompetence in HIV patients.
items.reason: Removal of the spleen results in an increase in peripheral blood lymphocyte count, making absolute CD4+ T cell counts unreliable.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)
items.assertion: The most effective medical approach to thrombocytopenia in HIV patients is splenectomy.
items.reason: Splenectomy is rarely needed and reserved for refractory cases.
items.explanation: A is false; the most effective approach is ART, not splenectomy.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 17)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.assertion: Reactivation herpes zoster in patients with HIV indicates a modest decline in immune function.
items.reason: Patients who developed shingles did so an average of 5 years after HIV infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the timing of shingles development is not the direct explanation for the indication of immune decline.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)
items.assertion: Herpes simplex virus infections in HIV patients become more frequent as CD4+ T cell counts increase.
items.reason: As HIV disease progresses and CD4+ T cell count declines, these infections become more severe.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; HSV infections become more frequent and severe as CD4+ T cell counts decline.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 18)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.assertion: HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders can range from asymptomatic to severe dementia.
items.reason: The spectrum of HAND includes asymptomatic neurocognitive impairment, minor neurocognitive disorder, and clinically severe dementia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)
items.assertion: CSF abnormalities always imply cognitive impairment.
items.reason: Evidence of infection of the CNS with HIV does not necessarily mean there is cognitive impairment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · SYMPTOMATIC DISEASE (part 19) | TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.assertion: HIV-associated dementia can occur even when CD4+ T cell counts are >350 cells/μL.
items.reason: It is generally a late complication of HIV infection that progresses slowly over months.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why dementia can occur at higher CD4+ T cell counts.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)
items.assertion: Fluconazole is used as initial treatment for cryptococcal meningitis.
items.reason: It is administered intravenously at a dose of 800 mg/d PO for 8 weeks after amphotericin therapy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; fluconazole is not used as initial treatment, but rather follows amphotericin B or liposomal amphotericin.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.assertion: Patients with HIV encephalopathy have an increased sensitivity to the side effects of neuroleptic drugs.
items.reason: The use of these drugs is associated with an increased risk of extrapyramidal side effects.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)
items.assertion: Seizures may be a presenting clinical symptom of HIV disease.
items.reason: Cerebral mass lesions are the most common cause of new-onset seizures in patients with HIV infection.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why seizures can present as an initial symptom.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.assertion: Cerebral toxoplasmosis is more common in patients who are seronegative for T. gondii.
items.reason: Toxoplasmosis represents a reactivation of latent tissue cysts and is more common in those with antibodies to the organism.
items.explanation: Cerebral toxoplasmosis is actually 10 times more common in patients who are seropositive.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)
items.assertion: Brain biopsy is the first-line diagnostic procedure for suspected cerebral toxoplasmosis.
items.reason: The morbidity associated with brain biopsy makes it a reserved procedure after failure of empiric therapy.
items.explanation: Brain biopsy is usually reserved for patients who fail empiric therapy, not first-line.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 4)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.assertion: ART has significantly improved the survival of patients with PML.
items.reason: A median survival of 2 years and survival of >15 years have been reported in patients treated with ART for their HIV disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the improvement in survival due to ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)
items.assertion: JC virus DNA levels in CSF have a diagnostic specificity of 50% for PML.
items.reason: The measurement of JC virus DNA levels has a diagnostic sensitivity of 76% and specificity close to 100%.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the specificity is close to 100%, not 50%.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 5)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.assertion: Distal sensory polyneuropathy may be a side effect of ART with dideoxynucleosides.
items.reason: ART can cause peripheral neuropathy, which presents as painful burning sensations in the feet and lower extremities.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)
items.assertion: CMV-associated myelopathy can be effectively treated with glucocorticoids.
items.reason: Intravenous therapy with ganciclovir or foscarnet is important for minimizing permanent neurologic damage.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, as CMV-associated myelopathy is treated with antiviral drugs, not glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 6)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.assertion: Zidovudine-induced myopathy is reversible.
items.reason: It is dose-dependent and related to interference with mitochondrial polymerases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reversibility of zidovudine-induced myopathy is not directly explained by its dose-dependence or mechanism.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)
items.assertion: Acute retinal necrosis syndrome is associated with pain.
items.reason: It is caused by HSV and varicella zoster virus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why acute retinal necrosis syndrome is associated with pain.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 7)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.assertion: Histoplasmosis is commonly seen in AIDS patients across the United States.
items.reason: Histoplasmosis is only seen in approximately 0.5% of AIDS cases in the United States due to its limited geographic distribution.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; histoplasmosis is not commonly seen across the U.S., but rather has a limited geographic distribution.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)
items.assertion: Treatment for mild Bartonella infection may include itraconazole.
items.reason: Itraconazole is used as a treatment for histoplasmosis, not typically for Bartonella infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; doxycycline or erythromycin are used for Bartonella infection, not itraconazole.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 8)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.assertion: Kaposi's sarcoma is a multicentric neoplasm presenting as multiple vascular nodules.
items.reason: HHV-8 has been strongly implicated as a viral cofactor in the pathogenesis of Kaposi's sarcoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and HHV-8 is indeed implicated in the pathogenesis of Kaposi's sarcoma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)
items.assertion: Non-AIDS-defining malignancies now account for more morbidity and mortality than AIDS-defining malignancies.
items.reason: The incidence of non-Hodgkin's lymphoma has declined dramatically since the introduction of ART.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the decline in non-Hodgkin's lymphoma is not as dramatic as the decline in AIDS-defining malignancies.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 9)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.assertion: Pulmonary involvement in Kaposi's sarcoma often presents with pleural effusions.
items.reason: Pleural effusions are seen in 70% of cases of pulmonary KS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)
items.assertion: Patients with Kaposi's sarcoma limited to lymph nodes have a poor prognosis.
items.reason: These patients generally have relatively intact immune function and thus the best prognosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these patients actually have the best prognosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 10)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.assertion: The response rate to IFN-$\alpha$ is higher in patients with CD4+ T cell counts >600/$\mu$L.
items.reason: Higher CD4+ T cell counts are associated with better immune function and response to treatment.
items.explanation: The relationship between response rate and baseline CD4+ T cell count is particularly true for IFN-$\alpha$.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)
items.assertion: Primary CNS lymphoma is a common manifestation of AIDS-associated systemic lymphomas.
items.reason: Lymphomas occur frequently in patients with T cell immunodeficiencies, including those with AIDS.
items.explanation: The incidence of primary CNS lymphoma has not experienced a dramatic decrease as a consequence of the widespread use of effective ART.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 11)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.assertion: The prognosis for patients with primary CNS lymphoma is generally poor.
items.reason: Primary CNS lymphoma usually occurs at a later stage of HIV infection when CD4+ T cell counts are very low.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the prognosis for primary CNS lymphoma is poor.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)
items.assertion: The incidence of Epstein-Barr positivity is higher in African Burkitt's lymphoma than in HIV-associated Burkitt's lymphoma.
items.reason: 97% of African Burkitt's lymphomas contain the EBV genome compared to only 50% of HIV-associated Burkitt's lymphomas.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the incidence of Epstein-Barr positivity is higher in African Burkitt's lymphoma.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 12)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.assertion: The use of corticosteroids may increase the risk of developing Kaposi's sarcoma.
items.reason: Corticosteroids can exacerbate coexisting conditions such as Kaposi's sarcoma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)
items.assertion: The current HPV vaccines may be less effective for patients with HIV.
items.reason: High-risk HPV genotypes such as 58 and 53 are more prominent in people with HIV compared to the general population.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-14 Neurologic Diseases in Patients with HIV Infection (part 13)

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.assertion: Patients with ICL should receive prophylaxis for opportunistic infections based on their CD4+ T cell count.
items.reason: Opportunistic diseases such as cryptococcosis and nontuberculous mycobacterial infections are common in patients with ICL.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why prophylaxis is recommended.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA
items.assertion: Idiopathic CD4+ lymphocytopenia (ICL) has a common cause across all patients.
items.reason: ICL is a heterogeneous syndrome with no evidence of a transmissible microbe.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ICL likely does not have a single common cause, while the reason is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · IDIOPATHIC CD4+ T LYMPHOCYTOPENIA

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.assertion: Physicians should educate patients about the natural history of HIV.
items.reason: Understanding their illness helps patients manage it better and make informed decisions.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why education is important.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)
items.assertion: A PPD test or IFN-γ release assay should be performed following an HIV diagnosis.
items.reason: These tests are used to assess the patient's nutritional status post-diagnosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; these tests screen for latent tuberculosis.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF PATIENT MANAGEMENT (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.assertion: ART has led to a decline in AIDS-defining conditions since its widespread use.
items.reason: Suppression of HIV replication prolongs life and improves quality for patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and suppression of HIV replication explains the decline.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)
items.assertion: Nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors should be used in ART regimens for patients with HIV-2.
items.reason: HIV-2 is treated similarly to HIV-1, but some drugs are ineffective against it.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors should not be used in HIV-2.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 1)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.assertion: None of the reverse transcriptase inhibitors should be used as monotherapy for HIV infection.
items.reason: Drug resistance can develop rapidly when these drugs are used alone.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)
items.assertion: Cobicistat is recommended for patients with estimated creatinine clearances <70 mL/min.
items.reason: Cobicistat inhibits tubular secretion of creatinine, resulting in increases in serum creatinine.
items.explanation: A is false because cobicistat is not recommended for patients with CrCl <70 mL/min; R is true.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 2)

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.assertion: ART should be initiated as soon as possible after HIV diagnosis.
items.reason: Early initiation of ART decreases disease progression and transmission risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY
items.assertion: A delay in ART initiation is recommended for all patients diagnosed with HIV.
items.reason: Delaying ART allows time to focus on treating opportunistic infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a delay is only considered under specific conditions.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (part 3) | PRINCIPLES OF THERAPY

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.assertion: Maximal suppression of viral replication is a goal of therapy.
items.reason: The greater the suppression, the less likely the appearance of drug-resistant quasispecies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)

items.subtopic: Nucleoside and Nucleotide Analogue Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (nRTIs) | TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 1)
items.assertion: The number of available drugs is limited.
items.reason: Any decisions on antiretroviral therapy have a long-term impact on future options for the patient.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.assertion: Resistance testing can help distinguish between drug-resistant virus and poor patient compliance.
items.reason: Resistance testing is valuable in determining new regimens for patients experiencing virologic failure while on therapy.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why resistance testing can help distinguish between drug-resistant virus and poor patient compliance.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)

items.subtopic: TABLE 208-23 Principles of Therapy of HIV Infection (part 2)
items.assertion: Phenotypic assays measure the enzymatic activity of viral enzymes in the presence or absence of different concentrations of drugs.
items.reason: These assays are used to determine co-receptor tropism and detect quasispecies present at a frequency of ≥10%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the use of phenotypic assays.

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.assertion: Adhering to standard guidelines for dealing with sharp objects would result in a significant decrease in needle-stick injuries.
items.reason: 27% of needle-stick injuries resulted from improper disposal of the needle, often due to recapping.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)
items.assertion: The risk of HIV infection following a mucous membrane exposure is higher than that from nonintact skin exposure.
items.reason: Episodes of HIV transmission after nonintact skin exposure have been documented, but the risk is less than for mucous membrane exposures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains that the risk from mucous membrane exposure is higher.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV AND THE HEALTH CARE WORKER (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.assertion: The use of condoms can markedly decrease the chance of HIV transmission.
items.reason: Condoms are not 100% effective in preventing HIV transmission, with a ~10% failure rate due to breakage or improper usage.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why condoms decrease but do not eliminate the risk of HIV transmission.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV PREVENTION (part 1)
items.assertion: ART for people with HIV has no role in preventing HIV transmission.
items.reason: Clinical trials have shown that ART can prevent sexual transmission to uninfected partners if the viral load is suppressed.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ART plays a significant role in preventing HIV transmission, as demonstrated by clinical trials.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV PREVENTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: HIV VACCINES
items.assertion: Developing a preventive HIV vaccine has been challenging.
items.reason: The high mutability of HIV and the inability of the natural immune response to clear it are significant factors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why developing an HIV vaccine is difficult.
items.reference: Human Immunodeficiency Virus Disease AIDS and Related Disorders · HIV VACCINES
